### Manali-Khardung La Cycling Championship
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 347 mi | 35,000 ft | Manali to Leh, Himachal Pradesh / Ladakh, India | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/manali-khardungla-cycling-championship/

The world's highest long-distance cycling race—a transcendent Himalayan ultra-endurance test combining extreme altitude, technical terrain, and notable scenery for elite mountain cyclists seeking ultimate adventure.

### Marmotte Granfondo Pyrenees
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 110 mi | 18,701 ft | Argelès-Gazost / Luz-Saint-Sauveur, Hautes-Pyrénées, France | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/marmotte-pyrenees/

Five Pyrenean cols. Double Tourmalet. 5,700m of climbing. Summit finish. The Marmotte's Pyrenean sister hits harder per kilometer than the Alpine original. The Marmotte Pyrénées is the event that cycling insiders know but the broader world has not fully discovered yet. On paper, it is the younger sister of the legendary Alpine Marmotte. In practice, it might be the harder ride. The numbers do not lie: 5,700m of climbing in 177 km gives it a higher elevation-per-kilometer ratio than the Alps. The double Tourmalet — climbing the most storied mountain in the Pyrenees from both sides in a single day — is an act of organized cruelty that, after 120 km, will have you questioning the sanity of the route designers. And then, when the Tourmalet has taken everything, they send you up Hautacam with its irregular gradients that refuse to let you find a rhythm.

The Pyrenees are darker, wilder, and less forgiving than the Alps, and this event embraces that character completely. Where the Alpine Marmotte finishes on the iconic 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez with crowds four deep, the Pyrénées edition sends you up a ski station road where the only witnesses are sheep and your own suffering. The Tourmalet at dawn, with mist rolling off the Pic du Midi and the road rising endlessly above the treeline, is one of cycling's great sensory experiences. Doing it a second time, 100 km later on legs that have already climbed three other mountain passes, is one of cycling's great tests of character.

If the Alpine Marmotte is the bucket-list mountain sportive, the Pyrénées edition is the insider's version — harder per kilometer, less crowded, and set against the wild, dark drama of the Tour de France's spiritual home. Give it ten more years and this event will be spoken about in the same breath as its Alpine sibling. For now, it remains the connoisseur's choice. Yes if you want the intensity of the Alpine Marmotte with more climbing per kilometer and fewer crowds. Yes if the Tourmalet means something to you — doing it twice in one day is a career cycling memory. Skip if you want the prestige and global atmosphere of the Alpine original. Skip if you need technical terrain — this is pure road climbing on tarmac.

### Gran Fondo Milano-Sanremo
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 184 mi | 14,474 ft | Milan to Sanremo, Liguria, Italy | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/milano-sanremo-gran-fondo/

La Classicissima — 296 km from Milan to the Ligurian Riviera. The Turchino, the Capi, Cipressa, Poggio, Via Roma. The longest day in amateur cycling. A race of three acts across three regions of Italy. Act One: the Lombardy and Piedmont plains — 100+ km of flat, fast, relentless agricultural roads where the peloton moves at 40+ km/h and the only challenge is staying in the group and out of the wind. Act Two: the Turchino — the first real climb, the passage through the Apennines where Lombardy becomes Liguria and the grey plains give way to the blue Mediterranean. Act Three: the Ligurian coast — 100 km of increasingly dramatic riding as the three Capi, the Cipressa, and the Poggio pile suffering onto legs that have already ridden 200+ km. The course is deceptive in its simplicity. No individual climb is fearsome. The Poggio averages 3.7%. The Cipressa averages 4.1%. But at kilometer 270 and kilometer 280, those modest percentages become the hardest climbs of your life. The pro peloton calls this phenomenon 'the course that bites you at the end.' For amateurs riding 2-3 hours longer than the professionals, the bite is sharper. Signature challenge: The distance. 296 kilometers. Not the Turchino, not the Cipressa, not the Poggio — those are just climbs. The signature challenge of the Gran Fondo Milano-Sanremo is the distance itself. It is the longest one-day amateur cycling event in the world, and there is no way to simulate it in training. You can ride 200 km. You can ride 250 km. But the zone between 250 km and 296 km is uncharted territory for nearly every rider who lines up. The legs stop working normally. The nutrition strategy that seemed bulletproof starts to fail. The mind begins its negotiations with the body. The Poggio at kilometer 280 is the cruelest joke in cycling — a climb that would be trivial at kilometer 80, transformed by distance into a monument of suffering. There are five Monuments in professional cycling. Milan-Sanremo is the longest, the oldest continuously run, and — for many — the most beautiful. La Classicissima. The Spring Classic. 296 kilometers from the industrial flatlands of Lombardy across the Apennines to the flower-scented Riviera di Ponente. The gran fondo version has been running since 1970, making it older than most of the riders who line up for it. You leave Settala at dawn and ride through the flat agricultural plains, the Turchino pass, the Ligurian coast, the three Capi, and then — with nearly 280 km in your legs — you climb the Cipressa and the Poggio. Two climbs that are not objectively hard. Two climbs that, after 280 km, will feel like the steepest roads you have ever ridden. Then you descend to the Via Roma in Sanremo and you are done. You have ridden La Classicissima. The same road Merckx rode seven times to victory. The same Poggio where Cancellara attacked from the front. The same Via Roma where van der Poel sprinted. No other amateur event in the world offers 296 km of Monument history in a single day. Yes if you are an endurance cyclist who wants the ultimate one-day road challenge — 296 km is a threshold experience that changes how you think about distance. Yes if you care about cycling history and want to ride every meter of the oldest, longest Monument. Yes if you have the discipline to train specifically for ultra-endurance and the self-sufficiency to manage nutrition for 10-13 hours with minimal feed station support. Skip if 296 km genuinely exceeds your ambition or your available training time. Skip if you want a well-serviced, festival-atmosphere sportive with aid stations every 30 km. Skip if you are looking for technical terrain or off-road riding — this is road cycling, pure and relentless.

### Race Across America
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 3000 mi | 175,000 ft | Oceanside, CA to Atlantic City, NJ | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-across-america/

3,000 miles. Coast to coast. 12 days. The most extreme endurance cycling race on Earth. A 3,000-mile transcontinental odyssey across the full diversity of the American landscape. Desert, mountains, plains, ridgelines, and coastline — all in a single continuous effort. Signature challenge: 3,000 miles across the entire continental United States — desert heat, Rocky Mountain passes, Great Plains wind, Appalachian climbs, and 175,000 feet of elevation gain — all in a single continuous, nonstop effort under 12 days. Race Across America is not a bike race in any conventional sense. It is a 3,000-mile, coast-to-coast war of attrition against sleep deprivation, extreme climate, and the fundamental limits of the human body. Running since 1982, with fewer than 300 solo finishers in history and a 50% DNF rate, RAAM occupies a category of one. The logistics are staggering — you need a crew, vehicles, and a budget that rivals a small expedition. The experience is transformative. The prestige is unmatched in ultra-cycling. This is not an event most people should attempt. It is an event everyone should know about. If you are an elite ultra-endurance cyclist with $15,000-$100,000 to spend, a crew of 8-12 people willing to sacrifice two weeks, qualifying results from approved ultra events, and the physical and mental capacity to ride 20+ hours a day for 8-12 consecutive days across the entire continental United States — yes. For everyone else, RAAM is best appreciated from the crew van, the documentary couch, or the dot-watching screen.

### Race Through Poland
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 1118 mi | 88,583 ft | Nowy Targ, Lesser Poland | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-through-poland/

Epic self-supported bikepacking odyssey through Carpathian mountains across 7 countries.

### Raid Pyreneen
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 447 mi | 36,100 ft | Hendaye (Atlantic) to Cerbere (Mediterranean), France | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/raid-pyreneen/

Ocean to ocean. 18 cols. 100 hours. The Pyrenees crossing that's been humbling cyclists since 1950. Epic. Point-to-point, ocean to ocean, 18 mountain passes over 3-5 days. The Pyrenees from west to east, starting in the green, rain-prone Basque country and finishing on the sun-baked Mediterranean coast. Each day brings a new set of cols, a new landscape, and a new challenge. The format — self-navigated with a stamped logbook — strips cycling back to its essentials: you, the mountains, and the road. Signature challenge: The Tourmalet at 2,115m is the symbolic peak, but the real challenge is the cumulative toll of 18 passes over 3-5 days with no support. The Raid tests self-reliance as much as fitness. The Raid Pyreneen is not an event. It's a tradition. Since 1950, cyclists have collected stamps in a logbook as they cross the Pyrenees from ocean to ocean — no timing chips, no aid stations, no spectators. Just you, 18 mountain passes, and 100 hours. The format hasn't changed because it doesn't need to. In an era of sold-out sportives with Instagram hashtags and finisher's beer gardens, the Raid stands apart as a declaration of what cycling adventure was always meant to be: self-reliant, mountain-focused, and earned. Yes if you want cycling's most authentic mountain challenge — no frills, no crowds, pure self-supported adventure. Yes if you value tradition over Instagram. Skip if you need organized support — this is fully on you. Skip if you want a single-day event.

### Sierra Nevada Límite Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 91 mi | 11,000 ft | Cenes de la Vega, Granada, Spain | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/sierra-nevada-limite/

Ultimate Spanish climbing crucible for masochists who crave thin air and endless VAM. Brutal high-altitude road gran fondo through Sierra Nevada's demanding alpine passes, blending endurance suffering with panoramic mountain beauty. Signature challenge: The continuous 38km+ mountain passes culminating near Veleta peak at over 3100m, Europe's highest road finish line.

### Struggle Dales
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 109 mi | 9,430 ft | Harrogate, North Yorkshire, England | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/struggle-dales/

Struggle Dales is one of the UK's most challenging and prestigious sportives, offering ambitious road cyclists an unforgettable test of endurance across the stunning Yorkshire Dales. Struggle Dales is one of the UK's hardest sportives, set in the beautiful but brutal Yorkshire Dales landscape. The event takes riders over stunning yet unforgiving terrain through the Yorkshire Dales National Park, featuring seven iconic climbing challenges that have earned the route cult status among ambitious road cyclists nationwide. Signature challenge: Park Rash stands as the most renowned climb on the route, recognized as a defining feature of the Struggle Dales experience and a bucket-list challenge for serious road cyclists.

### TORTOUR Ultracycling
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 621 mi | 45,932 ft | Schaffhausen, Switzerland | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tortour/

1,000 km non-stop across Switzerland. Alpine passes by starlight. The world's ultimate ultra-endurance cycling test. The TORTOUR 1000 route traverses Switzerland in a massive loop, changing year to year but always incorporating multiple Alpine passes, Swiss lowland roads, and urban transitions. The character is defined not by any single climb or descent but by the accumulation: 1,000 km of terrain changes, day-to-night-to-day temperature cycles, the psychological battle of sustained effort, and the Swiss Alps appearing on the horizon when your body is already finished. The route takes riders through multiple cantons, language regions, and climate zones. It is Switzerland distilled into a 48-hour continuous experience. Signature challenge: The second night. After 24+ hours and 500+ km of continuous riding, darkness falls again and the body demands sleep. Pushing through the second night — often on Alpine terrain — is the defining challenge of the TORTOUR 1000. Everyone can ride the first 500 km. The TORTOUR is decided in the dark hours. The TORTOUR is not a bike race. It is a 1,000 km argument with yourself about whether you can keep pedaling through the night, over Alpine passes, across an entire country, for 48 straight hours. The world's largest multi-day non-stop ultra-endurance cycling event takes everything Switzerland throws at you — 12,000m of climbing, 30-degree temperature swings, complete darkness, Alpine dawn — and compresses it into a single continuous effort.

Solo unsupported is the purest form: you, your bike, and whatever you can carry for 1,000 km. Solo with support gives you a crew car and checkpoint assistance. Teams of 2-6 relay the distance. Every format is punishing. The TORTOUR 1000 solo finisher has earned something that no one-day sportive can replicate.

The 2026 edition returns to Schaffhausen, the event's birthplace, after six years in the Zurich canton. The prologue, pasta party, and finisher's brunch return to the original format. This is a homecoming for an event that has grown into the defining ultra-cycling challenge in Europe. Yes if ultra-endurance cycling is your discipline and you want to test yourself against 1,000 km of Swiss terrain in 48 hours. Yes if the idea of Alpine passes at 3 AM excites rather than terrifies you. Skip if you have never ridden through the night or covered more than 300 km in a single effort. Skip if the budget (CHF 3,000-5,000 all-in) is prohibitive.

### Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 205 mi | 26,247 ft | Les Saisies, Haute-Savoie, France | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-du-mont-blanc-cyclo/

Circumnavigate Mont-Blanc through France, Switzerland, and Italy. 330 km. 8,000m of climbing. Dawn to dusk. One mountain. Three countries. Epic in scale, relentless in execution. The 330 km route is a circumnavigation — you are literally riding around a mountain massif, and the sense of circling Mont-Blanc drives the entire experience. The six major cols are not stacked as brutally as the Marmotte or Gavia events — there are valley recovery sections between climbs — but the sheer accumulation of climbing over 15+ hours creates a unique kind of attrition. The middle section through Switzerland and Italy, with the Great St. Bernard as the high point, is the psychological crux. The Cormet de Roselend as the final major col, when 260 km are in the legs, is the physical crux. Signature challenge: The 330 km distance with 8,000m of climbing through three countries creates a unique attrition challenge. No single climb is as brutal as the Mortirolo or as iconic as Alpe d'Huez, but the accumulated effect of 6+ cols over 15+ hours, with the Great St. Bernard at 2,469m as the high point after 130+ km, is the defining challenge. The Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo is not for the majority of cyclists. It is for the minority who have looked at the map, seen that you can ride around Western Europe's highest mountain through three countries in a single day, and thought: yes. 330 km. 8,000 meters. Six major cols. Dawn to dusk. France, Switzerland, Italy, and back to France. The Great St. Bernard Pass at 2,469m is a crossing that Napoleon used to invade Italy — you're doing it on a road bike with 200 km already in your legs. This is not a sportive. This is a geographical statement. Yes if you have deep ultra-endurance fitness and want the most epic single-day cycling adventure in Europe. Yes if riding through three countries around Western Europe's highest peak speaks to your sense of journey. Skip if you haven't completed at least one 200+ km mountain sportive. Skip if the logistics of a 15-hour day across three countries overwhelm rather than excite you.

### Tour of the Gila
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 334 mi | 15,000 ft | Silver City, Grant County, New Mexico, USA | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-of-the-gila/

America's only UCI stage race — open to amateurs. The Gila Monster at 7,400 ft is waiting. Multi-day stage racing at altitude through the Gila Wilderness. Each stage is a distinct test — the ITT sets the opening gaps, the road stages include genuine mountain climbs on narrow wilderness roads, and the criterium rewards tactical racing. The Gila Monster road race is the queen stage: 69 miles for amateurs with 5,200 ft of climbing, finishing at 7,436 ft on a narrow, winding mountain road through ponderosa pine forest. Signature challenge: The Gila Monster — 10.3 miles of narrow wilderness mountain road finishing at 7,436 ft. The only summit finish in American amateur stage racing that rivals European HC climbs for difficulty and atmosphere. Tour of the Gila is the most unique amateur road racing opportunity in the United States. It's the only place an amateur can pin on a number and race a UCI stage race course — the same climbs, the same roads, the same New Mexico wilderness that the professional peloton tackles. The Gila Monster climb is a genuine mountain summit finish that rivals anything in European professional cycling for atmosphere and difficulty. Silver City is remote, rugged, and authentically Western. This is not a corporate gran fondo with a finish-line festival — it's real stage racing in real wilderness. Yes if you want the unique experience of racing a UCI stage race as an amateur. Yes if you can handle altitude and remote logistics. Skip if you want a mass-participation gran fondo atmosphere.

### Tour of the Peak
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 113 mi | 9,500 ft | Bakewell, Derbyshire, England | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-of-the-peak/

Peak District's savage climbs on historic roads—a true test of grit. Brutal road sportive through Peak District National Park, relentless hills with savage short pitches and long grinds, rewarded by stunning vistas and fast descents. Signature challenge: The 'ride of heroes' long route tackling Winnats Pass, Holme Moss, and Cat and Fiddle in one day—over 10,000ft of Peak District punishment.

### Trans Pyrenees Race

Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/trans-pyrenees-race/

Epic self-supported Pyrenees double-crossing for grimpeurs seeking ultimate adventure.

### ADAC Velotour (Eschborn-Frankfurt)
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 65 mi | 6,562 ft | Eschborn/Frankfurt, Germany | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/adac-velotour/

Premier German amateur sportive on pro WorldTour roads with Taunus punch.

### The Assault on Mt. Mitchell
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 102.7 mi | 11,000 ft | Spartanburg, South Carolina to Mt. Mitchell, North Carolina | May | hillclimb
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/assault-on-mt-mitchell/

102.7 miles. 11,000 feet. The highest summit east of the Mississippi. The Beast of the East since 1975. The Assault on Mt. Mitchell is a study in patience and delayed devastation. The first 80 miles lull you into thinking this is a pleasant century ride — rolling Piedmont roads through South Carolina and the North Carolina foothills, gaining barely 500 feet of net elevation as you pass through rural towns between Spartanburg and Marion. The road is flat enough to make you forget what you signed up for. Then mile 85 arrives, and the road tilts upward, and it does not stop tilting for the next 18 miles. The climb to the Blue Ridge Parkway gains over 1,000 feet in under 2 miles. The Parkway itself is a sustained grind at 3,000-5,000 feet with no flat recovery. At mile 98, you turn off the Parkway toward Mt. Mitchell State Park, and those final 5 miles to the summit are the cruelest on the course — steep, relentless grades through ancient spruce-fir forest to the summit at 6,684 feet. The air is cooler. The trees are different. You are standing at the highest point east of the Mississippi River, and your legs stopped working two miles ago. Signature challenge: The final 18 miles from Marion to the summit. After 80+ miles of rolling Piedmont riding, the course demands 5,000+ feet of climbing in the final stretch — a backloaded elevation profile that punishes anyone who did not respect the first 80 miles. The last 5 miles up the Mt. Mitchell summit road, on legs that have been working for 7-10 hours, through gradients that would be hard on fresh legs, is where the Beast of the East earns every letter of its name. There is no higher point east of the Mississippi River. There is no older cycling challenge to reach it. Since 1975, when John Bryan and a handful of Freewheelers pedaled from Spartanburg into the clouds above western North Carolina, the Assault on Mt. Mitchell has been the defining endurance cycling event east of the Rockies. The Beast of the East does not care about your FTP, your Strava KOMs, or your deep-section carbon wheels. It cares about whether you can ride 102 miles through the Carolina Piedmont, turn upward at mile 85 when your legs are already spent, and then climb 5,000 feet to the summit of a mountain that has no business being this tall on the East Coast. The first 80 miles are a long, hot warm-up. The last 20 are the ride. At the top, you stand at 6,684 feet in a spruce-fir forest that looks like it belongs in Canada, not North Carolina. You rode there from South Carolina. That fact will matter to you for a very long time. Yes if you want the defining climbing challenge east of the Rockies. Yes if a century ride with 11,000 feet of backloaded climbing and a geographic superlative finish appeals to you. Yes if you value tradition and community over timing chips and podiums. Skip if you need competitive structure, technical terrain, or cannot handle 80 miles of rolling warm-up before the real climbing begins.

### Colnago GF Mont Ventoux
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 84 mi | 10,318 ft | Vaison-la-Romaine, Vaucluse, Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur, France | June | hillclimb
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/colnago-cycling-festival/

A legendary Mont Ventoux summit finish in Provence with world-class organization and unforgettable scenery—a true bucket-list gran fondo. A legendary Provençal gran fondo centered on Mont Ventoux, Europe's most iconic climb. The route traverses stunning landscapes—lavender fields, vineyards, Gorges de la Nesque, and quaint villages—before culminating in a dramatic summit finish at 1,912 m. The event emphasizes both sporting challenge and cultural immersion in Provence. Signature challenge: Mont Ventoux summit finish via Bédoin (Granfondo) or Sault (Mediofondo/Piccolofondo). The final ascent is renowned as one of France's most demanding climbs, combining sustained gradient, wind exposure, and psychological challenge of the barren, rocky summit. The Colnago GF Mont Ventoux is a bucket-list event that delivers on every front: iconic climb, stunning Provençal setting, world-class organization, and genuine suffering. The summit finish at 1,912 m with closed roads and professional support creates a truly memorable experience. Multiple distance options make it accessible to varied abilities. Primary concern is unknown registration costs and limited value transparency, though the included package (accommodation, guides, support) suggests premium positioning. The Colnago GF Mont Ventoux ranks among Europe's premier gran fondos. The event combines cycling's most iconic climb with stunning Provençal landscapes, professional-level logistics, and festive community atmosphere. The 135 km Granfondo with 3,144 m elevation delivers genuine challenge, while shorter options and Saturday gravel routes provide accessibility. Closed roads on Mont Ventoux, feed stations, mechanical support, and a summit finish at 1,912 m create a professional-grade experience. The event's reputation rivals Etape du Tour, and the inclusion of accommodation and guided rides in packages adds value for destination travelers. Primary limitations are undisclosed registration costs and limited field-size data. For cyclists seeking a legendary European gran fondo combining sporting challenge with cultural immersion, this event is essential.

### Formosa 900

Iconic 9-day circumnavigation blending epic Taiwanese scenery, culture, and social cycling vibes.

### Gran Fondo Asheville
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 100 mi | 8,000 ft | Asheville Yards Amphitheater, 75 Coxe Ave, Asheville, NC 28801 | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-asheville/

Serpentine roads, climbs, and thrilling Blue Ridge Parkway descents through the mountains near Asheville, NC Downtown Asheville start → serpentine roads through countryside → mountain ridge climbs → Blue Ridge Parkway descent back to town. Timed segments (#2 Gran-only, #3/#4 multi-route). **Suffering zones:** mountain climbs, timed KOM segments[1][2][4][5] Classic National Series gran fondo with mountainous adventure, timed competition, and community focus. Strong format but lacks precise elevation/field data.

### UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou Qiandao Lake
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 66.5 mi | 5,906 ft | Qiandao Lake, Chun’an County, Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, China | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-hangzhou/

T2 107km Gran Fondo starts north Qiandao Lake, big counterclockwise loop through lakeside roads, quiet villages, lush countryside. **Suffering zones**: Cat4 2.59km/3.7% (km11), Cat3 3km/5.2% (km30), Cat4 1.73km/6% (km48). Feed zone km60. Flat/fast 21km TT loop in Liangzhu (11m elevation). Historic first Chinese UCI Gran Fondo at stunning Qiandao Lake (1,078 islands). 2026's punchier 107km/1,800m course improves on 2025's flatter 136km. Qualifier prestige + tourist paradise = bucket-list event despite travel costs.

### Gran Fondo Jordan
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 47.2 mi | 4,921 ft | Dead Sea, Jordan | January | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-jordan/

A geographically stunning and physically brutal three-day climbing challenge that rewards serious cyclists willing to travel. Gran Fondo Jordan is a three-day stage race that weaponizes geography. The Dead Sea's position as Earth's lowest point becomes the launching pad for sustained climbing that tests every system in your body. Stage 1 is a deceptively simple time trial along the lake—flat but windy, a tactical opener. Stages 2 and 3 are climbing masterclasses: relentless ascents with variable gradient patterns, steep sections that demand pacing discipline, and descents that offer brief respite before the next push. The course is fully paved but feels raw and exposed, with Dead Sea views that are simultaneously beautiful and humbling as you suffer upward. This is climbing-focused cycling at its most unforgiving. Signature challenge: The 30 km Mount Panorama climb on Stage 3 is the event's defining challenge. Divided into three steep steps (first 5-6 km steep, second steep section after 10 km, third steep part at km 18), with moderate recovery sections and even short downhills between them, this climb demands tactical pacing and mental toughness. Riders must manage their effort across three distinct steep sections while managing fatigue from the previous day's climbing. The final 6 km flatten out slightly, but by then most riders are operating on empty. This is climbing that breaks riders mentally and physically. Gran Fondo Jordan is a rare breed: a genuinely distinctive cycling event that justifies the logistical effort to reach it. The elevation profile is legitimately brutal, the location is unforgettable, and the UCI credentials are real. This isn't a mass-participation gran fondo—it's a curated challenge for serious cyclists willing to travel. The small field creates intimacy but limits competitive depth. If you're seeking a cycling adventure with genuine geographic and physical challenge, this delivers. If you want a casual weekend ride with thousands of peers, look elsewhere. Yes if you're a strong climber seeking a unique, UCI-sanctioned challenge with genuine adventure and don't mind international travel logistics. Skip if you prefer mass-participation events, casual weekend rides, or budget-friendly racing.

### Granfondo Alpi del Mare
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 68.8 mi | 7,451 ft | Mondovì, Piedmont, Italy | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-alpi-del-mare/

A prestigious, well-organized gran fondo through stunning Piedmont hills with Tour de France-affiliated credibility and strong community atmosphere.

### Granfondo Antalya
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 87 mi | 5,200 ft | Antalya, Turkey | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-antalya/

Warm late-season UCI qualifier on Antalya's flats with luxury resort vibes.

### Granfondo San Gottardo
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 71 mi | 10,656 ft | Ambri, Ticino, Switzerland | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-gottardo/

115 km, 3,248m climbing, three HC passes, 1832 cobblestones. Switzerland's most beautiful suffering. The course is a clockwise loop from Ambri through three distinct Alpine worlds. It opens with the Tremola — the cobblestone ascent of the Gotthard Pass through 24 hairpins of 1832 granite, a road monument unlike anything in cycling. After summiting at 2,106m, a fast descent drops into the Urseren valley and the base of the Furka Pass. The 12.2 km Furka climb at 7.3% average takes you past the famous Goldfinger filming location and the Rhône Glacier viewpoint to the summit at 2,429m. The descent into the Valais is fast and exposed. Then comes the Nufenen — 13.7 km at 8.3% average, the hardest climb of the day, saved for when your legs have nothing left. The summit at 2,478m is the second-highest paved road in Switzerland. The reward is a 26 km descent all the way back to the finish in Ambri. Signature challenge: The Nufenen Pass. At 13.7 km and 8.3% average, it is the steepest and highest of the three passes — and it comes last, after 80+ km and two HC climbs have already emptied your legs. The Tremola is the spectacle. The Nufenen is the reckoning. The Granfondo San Gottardo packs three of Switzerland's most legendary Alpine passes into 115 km of pure, concentrated suffering and beauty. The Tremola cobblestones — 4 km of 1832 granite through 24 hairpins — are cycling's most photogenic climb. The Furka Pass descent past the Rhône Glacier is James Bond's literal road. The Nufenen, at 2,478m and averaging 8.3% over 13.7 km, is the silent assassin that breaks you when you thought the hard work was done.

Cycling Plus voted this the best 100 km loop in Europe. It is hard to argue. The variety is extraordinary — cobblestones to smooth tarmac, Italian-speaking Ticino to German-speaking Uri, valley warmth to Alpine cold, history to cinema. And all of it compressed into a day's ride that starts and finishes in a small Ticino village.

The event itself is Swiss-organized and appropriately precise. Three feeding zones, mechanical support, professional timing, finisher meal. No unnecessary frills, no corporate bloat. Just an honest sportive on extraordinary roads. Yes if you want concentrated Alpine climbing on three legendary passes in a single day. Yes if the Tremola cobblestones and Goldfinger's Furka road are on your cycling bucket list. Skip if you need a large, competitive field or global prestige. Skip if Switzerland's prices and the Nufenen's 8.3% average after two prior passes sound like punishment rather than purpose.

### Cycle to the Sun
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 36 mi | 10,023 ft | Pa'ia, Maui, Hawaii | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/haleakala-cycle-to-the-sun/

Sea level to 10,023 feet. One climb. One volcano. The House of the Sun. Pure uphill. There is no flat. There is no descent. The road leaves Pa'ia at sea level and climbs continuously for 36 miles through four distinct climate zones to the summit of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. The average gradient is 5%, but sections through Makawao hit 10% and the final summit approach reaches 10-18% in oxygen-depleted air. The landscape transforms completely as you climb: tropical vegetation gives way to eucalyptus, then pine, then barren volcanic rock above the treeline. You will ride above the clouds. You will arrive at the rim of a volcanic crater. Every foot of elevation is earned. Signature challenge: The final 8 miles above the park entrance. Above the cloud line, through barren volcanic landscape, with 70% oxygen and gradients hitting 18%, you grind to the summit crater of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. It is the closest thing to riding on another planet. Cycle to the Sun is not the hardest race in the world, or the most prestigious, or the most competitive. It is, however, one of the most extraordinary cycling experiences on the planet. You start at sea level on a tropical island and ride continuously uphill for 36 miles until you're standing at 10,023 feet on the rim of a volcanic crater, above the clouds, in 40-degree air that was 80 degrees when you started. You traverse every climate zone Hawaii has to offer — tropical, temperate, alpine, volcanic — in a single effort. The name Haleakala means 'House of the Sun,' and when you reach the summit, you understand why. This is not a race you enter to win. This is a ride you do to remind yourself that cycling can take you to places that feel like the edge of the Earth. Yes if you want one of cycling's most extraordinary single-climb experiences. Yes if riding through four climate zones to the summit of a Hawaiian volcano sounds like a lifetime memory. Skip if you need competitive depth or can't justify Hawaii travel costs for a single 36-mile uphill ride.

### Granfondo Nove Colli
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 124 mi | 12,513 ft | Cesenatico, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/nove-colli/

The queen of granfondos — 9 Romagna hills, 200km, Pantani's hometown, and the event that invented the gran fondo format in 1971. 200km of relentless Romagna hills from the Adriatic coast into the Apennine foothills and back. The course starts with a flat 28km coastal section at 40+ km/h with echelons, then hits 9 named climbs in succession. No single climb is alpine, but the cumulative effect of 3,814m over 200km with pitches of 18% is enormous. Barbotto (18% max) is the signature, Gorolo (17-20% finale) is the final sting. The food stations serve actual Romagna cuisine — this is a culinary tour as much as a cycling event. Signature challenge: Barbotto — 5.5km at 6.9% average with a devastating final kilometer of 14-18% gradients. The crowd atmosphere at the summit, with an announcer calling every rider's name, is one of the great moments in amateur cycling. Nove Colli is where the gran fondo was invented. 17 riders in 1971, 12,000 today. The course is not alpine — max altitude 791m — but the relentless succession of 9 hills with pitches to 18% over 200km creates genuine suffering. Barbotto with the announcer calling your name is one of the great moments in amateur cycling. The Pantani connection is everywhere — you're riding his training roads in his hometown. EUR 120 including a Gobik jersey and 9 food stations serving actual Romagna cuisine is exceptional value. Yes, if you appreciate cycling heritage and Italian food culture as much as course difficulty. The 200km long route with 3,814m of climbing is genuinely tough, but the altitude is modest. The medium route (137km) includes the iconic Barbotto. Come for the history, the Pantani connection, and the 9 food stations.

### Omloop Het Nieuwsblad Sportive
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 93 mi | 3,996 ft | Ghent, Belgium | March | sportive
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/omloop-het-nieuwsblad-sportive/

Essential Flemish classics cyclo for cobbles lovers. Cobbled Flemish hellingen and pavé sectors mimicking pro Omloop; new additions for 2026. Signature challenge: Muur van Geraardsbergen cobbles This sportive captures the soul of Flemish cycling—cobbles, wind, and bergs that humble even pros. Starting from 't Kuipke the day after MVDP's win? Pure magic for €25. Shorter Ninove options make it accessible, but 150km is the real test of Vlaamse koersbeleving. Iconic routes matching the pro race with new challenges like Haaghoek and Leberg. Excellent value, strong community, weather adds adventure. T2 tier gran fondo with top cultural punch.

### RAGBRAI
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 391 mi | 16,027 ft | Across Iowa, USA (Missouri River to Mississippi River, west to east) | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/ragbrai/

America's oldest, largest, and longest multi-day bicycle ride. 53 years of Iowa towns turning themselves inside out for 10,000+ riders. RAGBRAI is not a race. It is not even really a ride, in the way most cyclists understand rides. It is a 400-mile rolling block party across the entire state of Iowa, powered by pedals and sustained by the most extraordinary host community in sports. You will ride through towns where the entire population has turned out to feed you pie, spray you with garden hoses, and blast classic rock from a flatbed trailer. You will camp in church yards, drink beer at breakfast, and discover that Iowa is not flat. You will dip your tire in the Missouri River on Saturday and the Mississippi River the following Saturday and wonder where the week went.

Since 1973, RAGBRAI has defined American recreational cycling. It inspired over 200 similar events worldwide. It has been visited upon all 99 Iowa counties. Nearly half a million riders have completed it. The oldest, largest, and longest multi-day bicycle ride in the world is not a race — and that is precisely why it matters. RAGBRAI proves that the best reason to ride a bicycle is to ride a bicycle, preferably through a small town that has been waiting all year for you to arrive.

Do not come to RAGBRAI expecting a challenge. Come expecting a transformation. Come expecting the farmer playing saxophone on the hilltop at sunrise, the 90-year-old woman handing out cookies from her porch, and the moment on Day 5 when you realize you have been smiling for five straight days and you cannot remember the last time that happened. That is RAGBRAI. That is Iowa. That is cycling at its most fundamentally, unapologetically joyful. You do not race RAGBRAI. You ride it. You experience it. You eat pie at every stop and drink beer before noon and sleep in a tent in a stranger's yard and discover that Iowa is hillier, friendlier, and more beautiful than you imagined. If you have ever wanted to know what happens when an entire state decides to throw a week-long party for cyclists, register for RAGBRAI. Registration opens November each year and fills steadily. $250 for the week. Bring a bike, a tent, sunscreen, and an appetite. Leave the power meter at home.

### RAMROD — Ride Around Mount Rainier in One Day
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 154 mi | 10,000 ft | Enumclaw, Washington, USA | July | hillclimb
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/ramrod/

154 miles. 10,000 feet. One active volcano. The Pacific Northwest's defining cycling challenge since 1984. RAMROD is a circumnavigation. You ride all the way around an active volcano and come back to where you started. That simple premise — one loop, one mountain, one day — produces one of the most varied and rewarding days you will ever spend on a bicycle. The ride begins in the agricultural lowlands east of Tacoma, where Enumclaw sits at the Cascade foothills. Depending on the route edition, you may head counterclockwise toward the Nisqually Entrance and Paradise (the classic route) or clockwise toward Crystal Mountain and Cayuse Pass (the modern bypass). Either way, the terrain transitions dramatically: flat farmland gives way to rolling foothills, which give way to towering old-growth forest, which gives way to alpine passes where the air cools and the mountain dominates the sky. Stevens Canyon Road — when permitted — is the emotional heart of the ride: a 1930s-era park road through a glacial valley with stone bridges and tunnels. Cayuse Pass at mile 100+ is the physical heart: 8 miles at 6% when your legs have nothing left. The long descent back to Enumclaw in the Pacific Northwest's extended summer twilight is the reward. The mountain was always there. You rode all the way around it. Signature challenge: Cayuse Pass at mile 100. Eight miles at a constant 6% grade in afternoon sun with no shade, on legs that have already climbed 6,000-7,000 feet over 100 miles. Every RAMROD veteran knows the name. The rest stop halfway up Cayuse is the most grateful you will ever be for a cup of water and a folding chair. The climb is not technically difficult — it is 6% and relentless — but arriving at its base after 100 miles of riding around a volcano transforms a moderate climb into a test of will. Cayuse Pass is where RAMROD separates the people who trained for 150 miles from the people who trained for 100. Forty years ago, a cyclist named John Dixon convinced 51 other people to ride their bicycles all the way around Mount Rainier in a single day. Forty-one editions later, 800 people win a lottery each year for the privilege of doing the same thing. RAMROD is the Pacific Northwest condensed into a single day on a bicycle: old-growth forest, alpine passes, glacial rivers, quiet foothills towns, and a 14,411-foot active volcano that is always there — above the treeline, above the clouds, above everything. The ride starts in the dark of Enumclaw at 5 AM with breakfast cooked by the local Rotary club. It climbs to Paradise through cathedral forests with the mountain overhead. It crosses Stevens Canyon on 1930s stone bridges through a glacial valley. It grinds up Cayuse Pass at mile 100 on legs that stopped cooperating 20 miles ago. And it returns to Enumclaw 150 miles later, in the long Pacific Northwest twilight, with the mountain still watching. RAMROD is not the hardest ride in America. It is not the longest. But it may be the most complete — a full orbit of something genuinely magnificent, earned one pedal stroke at a time. Yes if you want to circumnavigate an active volcano in a single day on one of America's most storied cycling routes. Yes if 150+ miles through national park alpine passes, old-growth forest, and glacial valleys is your idea of a perfect day. Yes if you value community tradition over timing chips and age-group podiums. Enter the lottery every year and volunteer when you can. Skip if you need competitive structure, technical terrain, or a ride shorter than 150 miles. Skip if you cannot handle uncertainty — the route changes annually and the event's future depends on National Park Service permitting decisions.

### Ride Across Britain
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 980 mi | — | Land's End to John O'Groats, UK | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/ride-across-britain/

Premier fully-supported LEJOG charity ride delivering epic UK scenery and camaraderie.

### Sellaronda Bike Day
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 33 mi | 5,413 ft | Sella Group, Dolomites, Italy | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/sellaronda-bike-day/

Four car-free Dolomite passes, 20,000 cyclists, zero entry fee — the purest cycling experience in the mountains, twice a year. A counterclockwise circuit around the Sella Group — the massive limestone massif at the junction of four South Tyrolean valleys. The route links four Dolomite passes in a continuous loop of climbing and descending through some of the most dramatic mountain scenery on Earth. On Sellaronda Bike Day, the absence of motor traffic transforms this circuit from a challenging road ride into a transcendent cycling experience. The terrain is constantly undulating — you are either climbing a pass or descending one, with minimal flat valley transitions. Signature challenge: Not a single climb but the totality: four passes in 55 km with 1,780m of climbing, experienced in silence on car-free roads through the most dramatic mountain landscape in Europe. The physical challenge is modest; the experiential impact is extraordinary. Sellaronda Bike Day is the simplest concept in cycling events: close the roads around the Sella Group to cars for one day and let cyclists ride. No registration, no timing, no fees, no bibs. Just 55 km of four legendary Dolomite passes — Sella (2,240m), Pordoi (2,239m), Gardena (2,136m), and Campolongo (1,875m) — in complete silence. The result is one of the most magical cycling experiences on Earth.

The numbers are modest — 55 km and 1,780m of climbing is a half-day ride for any fit cyclist. But the numbers miss the point entirely. This is about the silence. On any other day, these roads carry cars, buses, and motorcycles through some of the most dramatic mountain scenery in Europe. On Sellaronda Bike Day, 20,000+ cyclists of every age and ability share these roads without a single engine noise. The only sounds are tires on tarmac, wind through the Dolomite spires, and the spontaneous cheering at pass summits.

The event runs twice annually — June and September — offering two different Dolomite experiences. June delivers wildflower meadows, long daylight, and occasionally lingering snow at the highest elevations. September brings autumn colors, stable weather, and a gentler light. Both are extraordinary. The 20th edition in 2026 is a celebration of a concept so pure it has not needed to change since its inception: close the roads, open the mountains, and let bicycles be bicycles. Ride this if you love cycling as pure experience — car-free Dolomite passes in UNESCO World Heritage scenery with zero bureaucracy. Skip it if you need competitive structure, timing, results, or a challenging distance (55 km is short). If you want a competitive Dolomite event, enter the Maratona dles Dolomites lottery instead.

### Tour of Georgia Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 102 mi | 10,000 ft | Helen, Georgia | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-of-georgia-gran-fondo/

Elite climbing gran fondo in alpine Georgia—pure vertical pain with Bavarian charm. Crisscrosses Appalachian Trail through North Georgia Mountains: steep climbs, secluded roads, National Forest, mountain gaps. Signature challenge: 9,000ft Gran with 4 timed climbs over brutal gaps A gravel god's road detour: savage climbs and series polish make the Gran a bucket-list sufferfest, even without dirt. Top-tier GFNS event with massive elevation, timed KOMs, full support, and series perks. Gran route crushes souls over big gaps; perfect for roadies chasing suffering and swag.

### Trek 100
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 100 mi | 4,500 ft | Trek HQ, 801 West Madison Street, Waterloo, Wisconsin 53594 | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/trek-100/

Premier charity ride with heart, hills, and headquarters party. Rolling paved roads through dairy farmland with quiet secondary roads, challenging hillsides, flats, and fun segments; new loop-style courses; MTB on private moderate singletrack (10mi laps, no jumps). Signature challenge: Hilly 100-mile route with 3,889ft elevation through beautiful Wisconsin countryside. The Trek 100 stands out as a feel-good charity powerhouse, blending accessible routes, Trek's polish, and a noble cause—perfect for community-minded riders seeking fun over suffering. Trek 100 delivers a polished, inclusive cycling experience through Wisconsin's picturesque farmland, raising millions for childhood cancer research while offering diverse distances and a festive vibe at Trek HQ. Ideal for fundraisers who want challenge without cutthroat competition.

### Bealach Mór
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 44.7 mi | 2,625 ft | Shieldaig, Applecross Peninsula, Scottish Highlands, Scotland | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/bealach-mor/

UK's ultimate road climb on closed roads: brutal, beautiful, bucket-list essential. Closed-road loop from Shieldaig over Bealach na Bà to Applecross peninsula, looping with Torridon/Skye views back to finish. Signature challenge: Bealach na Bà pass—UK's biggest road climb. A savage, scenic masterpiece that punches way above its modest distance—pure suffering rewarded with Highland majesty. Prestige 3 belies its 'Holy Grail' status among UK climbs. Bealach Mór delivers the Bealach na Bà—626m over 9km at 20% max—in a 72km charity loop through paradise. Remote, raw, and relentlessly rewarding; T2 tier hides its elite suffering.

### Bike Abu Dhabi Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 93.2 mi | 5,988 ft | Hazza bin Zayed Stadium, Al Ain to Sheikh Zayed Festival, Al Wathba, Abu Dhabi, UAE | December | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/bike-abu-dhabi-gran-fondo/

Prize-laden desert road epic for community cyclists. Fast desert roads with long straights, gentle rises, and light inclines; fully supported with 5 water stations. Signature challenge: 150km UAE inter-city haul with speed-group competition. A standout road gran fondo in the desert, blending massive prizes, pro-amateur racing, and UAE hospitality into an accessible yet challenging event. Perfect for speed-focused endurance riders seeking value and adventure without gravel or climbs. The Bike Abu Dhabi Gran Fondo delivers a fast 150km road challenge across UAE deserts, backed by top support and AED 2M prizes. Ideal for speed groups from elites to recreational riders, it's a cornerstone of Abu Dhabi's cycling rise.

### Boone Gran Fondo

### Cycling Shimanami
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 43.5 mi | 1,300 ft | Imabari, Ehime Prefecture to Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cycling-shimanami/

Japan's most prestigious international cycling event offering unparalleled logistics, scenery, and cultural immersion across the Seto Inland Sea. A scenic, relatively flat island-hopping route across nine iconic bridges spanning the Seto Inland Sea. The Shimanami Kaido connects six islands (Oshima, Omishima, Hakatajima, Innoshima, Ikuchi-jima, and Mukaishima) between Imabari on Shikoku Island and Onomichi in Hiroshima Prefecture. Dedicated blue-line cycling paths, 150+ rest oases, and comprehensive aid stations with local food and cultural experiences characterize the route. The event allows cyclists unique access to the normally car-only expressway main road, creating an unforgettable atmosphere with closed roads, cheering locals, and photographers lining the route. Signature challenge: The nine bridge crossings themselves, particularly the longest bridge onto Shikoku Island, which require sustained effort but reward with spectacular Seto Inland Sea vistas. The optional Shiratakiyama climb offers the most significant elevation challenge with stone kami (gods) statues lining the ascent. Cycling Shimanami represents the gold standard for mass-participation cycling events in Asia. The combination of world-class organization, stunning scenery, and genuine cultural integration makes it exceptional for riders seeking an unforgettable experience rather than a competitive challenge. The event's biennial schedule and international draw create a unique atmosphere where thousands of cyclists from around the world share the road simultaneously, with locals cheering from sidewalks and photographers lining the route. The flat terrain and excellent support infrastructure make it accessible to a broad range of abilities, though this also means it lacks the physical severity that hardcore cyclists might seek. For those prioritizing logistics, experience quality, and community engagement over raw difficulty, this is a premier destination event. Cycling Shimanami 2026 stands as a masterclass in event organization and cyclist-centric infrastructure. The event's strength lies not in physical difficulty but in its holistic experience: stunning bridge crossings, dedicated cycling paths, exceptional volunteer support, and authentic cultural integration. The October timing provides ideal weather, and the biennial schedule creates genuine international community. However, the relatively flat terrain and moderate distances mean this event appeals primarily to experience-seekers and recreational cyclists rather than those seeking competitive challenge or significant physical suffering. The comprehensive logistics—including bike rentals, luggage forwarding, and 150+ aid stations—eliminate typical cycling event friction points. For international cyclists planning a Japan cycling pilgrimage, this is non-negotiable; for those seeking a hard cycling challenge, look elsewhere.

### Love Tiles Douro Granfondo
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 94 mi | 7,460 ft | Peso da Régua, Douro Valley, Portugal | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/douro-gran-fondo/

Iconic Portuguese granfondo blending epic Douro climbs with notable wine country scenery.

### Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 143 mi | 11,483 ft | Margam Park, Port Talbot, South Wales, UK | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/dragon-ride/

The UK's oldest and hardest sportive. 230 km through the Brecon Beacons. Devil's Elbow at 10.3%. L'Etape by Tour de France branding earned through 22 years of Welsh mountain suffering. The Dragon Ride is the event that invented the UK sportive scene. Before it existed, British cyclists went to France to suffer on mountain roads. Lou Lusardi looked at the Brecon Beacons in 2004, saw Italian gran fondo terrain hiding in plain sight, and built an event that has drawn 4,700+ riders annually for over two decades. The Gran Fondo at 230 km with 3,500 m of climbing is a legitimate monster — longer and harder than most L'Etape du Tour editions. The Brecon Beacons deliver climbing that riders describe as the closest the UK gets to the Alps: Rhigos grinds on for 11 km, Black Mountain feels like the end of the world, and Devil's Elbow hits you with 10.3% through hairpins that would not look out of place on the Stelvio. Welsh weather adds a dimension that Italian gran fondos never deliver — you might get baked in June sunshine or hammered by horizontal rain, and the organisers tell you to pack for both.

The 2016 ASO partnership brought L'Etape by Tour de France branding and international credibility, but the Dragon Ride had already earned its reputation the hard way: 22 years of riders crawling up Welsh mountains and declaring it the hardest thing they have ever done on a bike. Starting from the gothic grandeur of Margam Park's Tudor mansion, climbing through increasingly wild terrain until you are alone on a windswept mountain with sheep for company, then descending through valleys that feel like they belong in Middle-earth — this is an experience that no amount of branding can manufacture. It is authentically, brutally, beautifully Welsh. The 2027 Tour de France Grand Depart will include a stage cresting Rhigos mountain, bringing international attention to climbs Welsh cyclists have known for decades. Yes if you want to ride Britain's most iconic sportive on roads that rival Alpine passes for scenery and suffering. Yes if a 230 km day with 3,500 m of climbing through Welsh mountain weather sounds like your definition of a good time. Skip if you need competitive racing — this is participatory, not a race. Skip if you cannot handle unpredictable weather — Welsh mountains do not care about your forecast. Skip if you demand continental European sportive prestige — this is the biggest fish in the UK pond.

### Giro d'Italia Ride Like a Pro USA
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 85 mi | 282 ft | Doral, Florida, USA | February | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/giro-ride-like-a-pro-usa/

Blistering flats with Giro glamour—fun but forget the climber's ego. Silky flat loops from Doral through Miami-Dade suburbs scream pure speed. Four timed segments spice endless asphalt with KOM hunts. Minimal elevation lets watts rule—no tactical climbing games. Urban glimpses mix with open-road blasts for pro-peloton feel. Signature challenge: Aggregate timed segments over full 85 miles—winners forged by consistent power, not one big attack. Flats amplify small gaps; strategy trumps suffering. This Giro-branded blast delivers pro polish on Florida's flattest canvas—85 miles of pure wattage without a single climb to cry about. Timed segments and pasta parties make it addictively fun for speed demons dodging winter. Prestige punches above its easy terrain, but don't expect suffering glory. Global expansion and Owen Shott's star power cement its buzz. Ideal for base miles with swagger. Yes if you're a power hunter chasing winter miles or family fondo vibes. Skip if you need climbs or elite battles—too easy for masochists.

Excellent amateur qualifier combining fast racing with Turkish Riviera luxury. Perfect for age-group qualification without suffering. 98km mainly flat/fast loop from resort: beach roads → backcountry near airport → short steep hills (253m northern section). 66km skips climbs. 23km flat TT river loop[1][2][7]. **Suffering zones**: Headwind sections (6 Beaufort), gravel corners, final kickers.

### Granfondo Bogotá
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 68 mi | 4,921 ft | Bogotá, Colombia | December | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-bogota/

Elite high-altitude road qualifier with massive suffering potential. Flat start builds to late high-altitude climbs in Andean plateau, finishing fast in Bogotá streets.[1][2] Signature challenge: La Calera climb at extreme elevation. Inaugural UCI Granfondo Bogotá punched above its weight with extreme altitude turning a moderate profile into a sufferfest. La Calera climb and Tominó reservoir loop at 3,000m demand acclimation; tight photo-finish sprints prove it's no gimmick. Perfect for altitude junkies chasing qualifiers in cycling-mad Colombia. Bogotá's Andes deliver a brutal debut: 128km with 1,276m gain at nosebleed elevations, won in dramatic sprints by Quintero and Severino amid 1,500 riders. UCI prestige meets local fire—must-do for qualifiers.[1][2]

### Granfondo Felice Gimondi
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 92 mi | — | Bergamo, Lombardy, Italy | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-felice-gimondi/

30 years in the saddle of a legend. Six valleys. Selvino's 19 hairpins. Bergamo's cycling soul, named for the man who won all three Grand Tours. Six Bergamasque valleys connected by sustained climbs, starting and finishing in the cultural city of Bergamo. The climbs are not short punchy walls — they are genuine valley ascents of 7-11 km at 5-7% average gradients, with steeper pitches up to 12%. Selvino's 19 hairpins are the signature feature: nearly 11 km of climbing through switchbacks named after local cycling legends, reaching 946m above sea level. Between climbs, the valleys offer rolling terrain through stone villages, river gorges, and alpine meadows. The rhythm is valley-floor recovery, gradual approach, sustained climb, hairpin descent, repeat. The long route does this six times across 148 km. Signature challenge: Selvino's 19 hairpins — 10.9 km of sustained climbing through switchbacks named after Bergamasque cycling legends. It is not the steepest climb on the route (Colle Gallo has steeper pitches), but it is the most sustained and the most iconic. The 19 bends create a hypnotic rhythm that either carries you upward or slowly grinds you down. On the long route, Selvino comes early enough to be manageable but late enough that the remaining four climbs are ridden on Selvino-depleted legs. The Granfondo Felice Gimondi is what happens when a cycling-obsessed city names its signature ride after its greatest son. Felice Gimondi — born in Sedrina, 10 km from Bergamo — won the Tour de France, the Giro d'Italia three times, and the Vuelta a Espana. One of only seven riders to complete the Grand Tour triple crown. The event bearing his name threads through six Bergamasque valleys, climbs 19 hairpins up to Selvino where each bend carries a local cycling legend's name, passes the Sanctuary of Our Lady of Cyclists at Colle Gallo, and returns to a medieval city that breathes cycling the way other cities breathe football. Thirty years of continuous operation. Bianchi co-branding. A EUR45 entry fee that feels like a gift. This is not the most famous gran fondo in Italy — it is perhaps the most authentically Italian. Yes if you want an authentically Italian gran fondo in cycling's heartland — great value, deep heritage, and honest climbing through beautiful pre-Alpine valleys. Yes if Gimondi's name means something to you. Yes if EUR45 for a well-organized FCI event with a Santini jersey option sounds like a bargain. Skip if you want Alpine drama at 2,500m — Selvino tops out under 1,000m. Skip if you need a massive international field and English-language everything — this event is proudly Italian first.

### Istria Granfondo
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 69.6 mi | 5,725 ft | Umag, Istria County, Croatia | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/istria-granfondo/

UCI-approved Istrian climbfest punches way above its field size. Blasts off 10km Adriatic flats before unleashing central Istrian hell: relentless climbs, swooping descents, wine-valley vistas. Paved perfection demands rhythm—coast lull crushes into hilltop pain. Granfondo's 25km Oprtalj loop doubles down on signature brutality; finish sprint rewards survivors. Signature challenge: Oprtalj's double-dose: 6km grinder averaging 7.8%, spiking 12% via switchbacks. Twice in Granfondo, it's the leg-breaker defining Istria's rep—climb it twice to earn UCI stripes. Istria Granfondo nails the Euro gran fondo formula: savage Oprtalj climbs amid wine-soaked vistas, UCI glory on tap for 2026 Japan. Resort luxury meets road savagery—family tagging along seals the dream vacay. Field's modest, distances vary by source, but who cares when panoramas pop? Prestige elevates this Croatian gem; climbers, qualify here. Yes if you're an age-grouper hunting Japan Worlds slots amid epic scenery. Skip if elite depth or mega-distance defines your fix—opt for Dolomites instead.

### L'Etape Norway by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 80.8 mi | 4,921 ft | Trondheim, Trøndelag, Norway | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-norway/

A prestigious Nordic debut that trades cost efficiency for adventure and Tour de France cachet. L'Étape Norway weaves through central Norwegian terrain, combining coastal fjord approaches with inland mountain climbing. The course likely mirrors Arctic Race of Norway's philosophy—mixing technical descents, gravel sections, and sustained climbs across varied terrain. Riders experience the psychological advantage of extended daylight while navigating rolling to mountainous topography. The route balances accessibility for serious amateurs with enough technical and climbing challenges to create meaningful selection. Expect a course that showcases Norwegian cycling culture while delivering Tour de France-caliber route design. Signature challenge: The Målselv climb stands as the course's signature challenge—a 3-4km alpine ascent at 7-8% that creates genuine selection while remaining achievable for trained amateurs. This climb defines the race's character and determines the final general classification. The exposed Nordic terrain and potential wind make it tactically interesting beyond pure climbing power. L'Étape Norway represents the Tour de France brand's successful expansion into Scandinavia, delivering a prestigious inaugural event in one of Europe's most dramatic cycling regions. The 130km course through Trondheim and surrounding fjord terrain offers genuine climbing, technical interest, and the psychological boost of nearly 24-hour daylight. This is not a bargain event—Norway's premium pricing and the international travel required make it expensive—but the combination of Tour de France pedigree, Nordic scenery, and first-edition exclusivity justifies the investment for serious amateur cyclists seeking a memorable experience beyond typical European cyclosportives. The course design leverages Arctic Race of Norway expertise, promising quality route planning and professional support infrastructure. This event appeals to cyclists who value prestige, adventure, and unique geography over cost efficiency. Yes if you value prestige, Nordic scenery, and inaugural event status enough to justify premium travel costs and unpredictable weather. Skip if you're budget-conscious, prefer proven multi-year events, or want guaranteed warm conditions.

### L'Etape Poland by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 52.2 mi | 5,077 ft | Zakopane, Lesser Poland Voivodeship, Poland | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-poland/

Tatra's TdF debutant punches like a veteran climber. Tatra masterpiece mashes steep ramps, flowing valleys, peak panoramas. 84km Race demands climbing soul amid UNESCO-worthy drama. Paved purity favors road weapons; mountain finish crowns kings. Varied pitch keeps watts guessing through Polish paradise. Signature challenge: 1,547m cumulative Tatra tooth in compact 84km—brutal VAM rivals pro stages. Mountain finish amid jagged peaks delivers Tour de France theater. Compact distance amplifies per-km pain. L'Etape Poland crashes Poland's cycling party with Tour de France swagger and Tatra tooth. 84km/1,547m serves compact savagery amid peaks rivaling Chamonix. ASO magic ensures polish, but newbie status means field lacks bite. Family options sweeten domestic appeal. This sleeper hits T2 velocity fast. Yes if you crave alpine pain on a budget or Polish passport flex. Skip if chasing deep fields or 100+km epics. Perfect mid-pack hero maker.

### L'Etape Taiwan by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 64.6 mi | 4,921 ft | Sun Moon Lake, Nantou County, Taiwan | — | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-taiwan/

TdF flair conquers Taiwan's lake paradise Starts fast downhill from Xiangshan into Puli flats, then unleashes KOM beast on Road 147. Winding lake returns via Road 21 grind steady pain amid emerald bliss. Pro closed roads amplify Tour intensity. Signature challenge: The Road 147 KOM: 7km of escalating pain peaking at 12%, freshly paved for max suffering. Tests resolve amid rice fields—true TdF suffering in Taiwan. L'Etape Taiwan transplants Tour de France magic to Sun Moon Lake's stunning shores, blending 1,500m Taiwanese climbs with pro spectacle. Fresh pavement and global fields make it a must for adventure seekers tired of Alps reruns. Humidity tests grit, but emerald vistas and prestige jersey reward every watt. Emerging star that's already essential. Yes if craving prestige jersey and Asian vibes with manageable suffering. Skip if mega-elevation junkie or hate humidity.

### Maraton Franja BTC City
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 97 mi | 4,281 ft | Ljubljana, Slovenia | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/maraton-franja/

Europe's oldest gran fondo. 43 editions since 1982. UCI World Series founding member. EUR 44 entry. The Slovenian institution that proves longevity beats hype. A 156 km loop through the Ljubljana Basin and Julian Alps foothills. The route leaves Ljubljana, climbs gently over the Vrhnika Hill, traverses the Logatec Plateau, descends through the historic mercury-mining town of Idrija, then tackles the defining Kladje climb — 7 km at 6.3% average with a 10% maximum pitch. After a long, rough descent through alpine forest, the route passes through medieval Skofja Loka before rolling back to Ljubljana across pastoral terrain. The course is diverse: urban start, rolling plateau, alpine forest climbs, historic towns, and flat agricultural finish. Maximum elevation is 801m — this is a foothills event, not an Alpine epic. Signature challenge: The Kladje climb: 7 km at 6.3% average with a maximum gradient of 10%. It arrives at approximately km 90 after the plateau crossings and the Idrija valley, on legs that have already absorbed 60+ km of rolling terrain. The climb itself is honest — no false flats, no respite sections. But the real test comes immediately after: a 10 km descent on rough road surface that demands concentration when you most want to coast. Maraton Franja is not the hardest gran fondo, not the most scenic, not the most famous. What it is, without qualification, is one of the most genuine. Running since 1982 — before 'gran fondo' was even a recognized category — this Slovenian institution has quietly built a 43-year legacy of accessible, well-organized road cycling through the Ljubljana Basin and Julian Alps foothills. Named after a secret WWII partisan hospital hidden in a mountain gorge, the event carries historical weight that no Italian monument or French classic can replicate. The Kladje climb will test you. The descent will scare you. The finish in Ljubljana will satisfy you. And the EUR 44 entry fee will make you wonder why everyone else charges three times as much. Yes if you want a well-organized UCI gran fondo qualifier at a fraction of the cost of Italian or French alternatives. Yes if the historical connection to a WWII partisan hospital resonates. Yes if you want to discover Slovenia — an underrated European cycling destination. Skip if you need Alpine drama — the maximum elevation is 801m. Skip if you need a globally recognized name on your cycling resume.

### Niseko Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 87 mi | 6,562 ft | Niseko, Hokkaido, Japan | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/niseko-classic/

Niseko Classic is Asia's most prestigious gran fondo, offering world-class mountain cycling and cultural immersion as the official qualifier for the 2026 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships. A dramatic mountain gran fondo through Hokkaido's famous Niseko ski region, combining steep alpine climbs with fast descents, forested passages, and glimpses of the Sea of Japan. The 140 km route is a true mountain stage with over 2,370 meters of elevation gain, featuring a signature 20 km climb to 1,000 meters elevation. Signature challenge: The Niseko Panorama Line climb to Hokkaido's highest KOM, followed by a fast descent into Kyowa Town Niseko Classic delivers world-class mountain cycling in an exotic, underexplored region. The elevation and technical climbing rival European gran fondos, while the Hokkaido setting offers cultural immersion and stunning scenery. As the official 2026 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships qualifier and Asia's premier amateur cycling event, it carries significant prestige. However, high travel costs, uncertain registration fees, and limited field depth information temper the overall value proposition for international riders. Niseko Classic stands as a landmark event in amateur cycling, combining exceptional elevation gain (2,370 m), technical mountain terrain, and stunning Hokkaido scenery. Its status as the official 2026 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships qualifier elevates its prestige significantly. The event delivers on climbing quality and organization, with fully closed roads and professional support. However, international participants face substantial travel costs, uncertain registration fees, and limited information on field depth. Best suited for experienced climbers seeking a unique Asian cycling experience and potential qualification pathway to the World Championships. The June timing in early summer Hokkaido offers mild conditions but potential rain. Overall, a high-quality event with significant cultural and competitive value, though not optimized for budget-conscious riders.

### Nordsjørittet
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 96 mi | 4,900 ft | Egersund to Sandnes, Norway | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/nordsjorittet/

Norway's ultimate coastal crusher: massive, scenic, and welcoming for every pedal-pusher.
