Signature challenge: The relentless North Sea wind combined with 4900 ft of elevation on exposed coastal roads, rewarding resilient riders with unparalleled fjord-edge views.

### Race Across Germany North–South
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 683 mi | 24,606 ft | Flensburg to Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-across-germany/

Germany's ultimate ultracycling test: 1,100 km of non-stop riding from Baltic coast to Alpine peaks, combining geographic diversity, championship-level competition, and transformative endurance challenge. An epic north-south traverse of Germany's complete geographic and cultural diversity, beginning at the Baltic harbor in Flensburg and concluding beneath the Zugspitze in the Bavarian Alps. The route progresses through distinct regional zones: coastal plains, low mountain ranges (Harz, Hessian uplands, Rhön, Franconia), and Alpine foothills, with each section presenting unique environmental and physical challenges.[1] Signature challenge: Maintaining pace and mental focus across 1,100 km of non-stop riding through Germany's complete geographic spectrum—from coastal exposure to Alpine altitude—while meeting strict time cutoffs (final finish by July 5, 23:20 UTC).[1]

### Race Across Spain
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 621 mi | 35,000 ft | Santander to Valencia, Spain | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-across-spain/

Spain's ultimate road ultra odyssey: 621 miles of mountains, culture, and unbreakable spirit. A grand traverse of Spain from the wild Cantabrian north to Valencia's Mediterranean warmth, blending relentless climbs, picturesque villages, and diverse terrains through mountains, plateaus, and coasts. Signature challenge: Overcoming the cumulative 35,000ft elevation across 621 miles, punctuated by Spain's most scenic passes and cultural gems.

### Styrkeproven
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 335 mi | 11,800 ft | Trondheim to Oslo, Norway | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/styrkeproven/

540 km from Trondheim to Oslo over Dovrefjell. The Strength Test since 1967. A 540 km point-to-point journey from Norway's third city across the Dovrefjell mountain plateau to the capital. Front-loaded climbing over the first 160 km, followed by a long descent and valley run to Oslo. The Dovrefjell crossing is the crux — exposed mountain plateau at 1,000+ meters with unpredictable weather. The second half from Lillehammer to Oslo is predominantly flat along Lake Mjøsa. Signature challenge: The Dovrefjell crossing. Eighty kilometers of exposed mountain plateau at 1,000+ meters where the weather can shift from calm to hostile in minutes. Snow in June. Headwinds of 15 m/s. Near-freezing temperatures when you left Trondheim in 18C. This is where Styrkeprøven separates those who brought enough layers from those who did not. Styrkeprøven is 540 km from Trondheim to Oslo across the Dovrefjell mountain plateau in the midsummer night. It is not technically demanding, it is not a race, and it will never be cheap. What it is: one of the most transformative point-to-point cycling experiences on earth. The name means 'The Strength Test' and 57 years of history confirm it earns that name every June. If ultra-distance road cycling through wild Norwegian terrain in the midsummer night sounds like your idea of a life experience — absolutely yes. Budget for Norway's costs and respect Dovrefjell's weather. If you want a competitive race with a large international field, look elsewhere.

### Tour de Romandie Cyclosportive
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 100 mi | 8,500 ft | Lausanne, Switzerland | April | sportive
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-romandie-cyclosportive/

Pro-caliber road suffering in postcard Switzerland—pure gran fondo gold. Hilly road sportive emulating the Tour de Romandie's punchy Jura and Alpine foothills, featuring rolling terrain, steep kicks, and lake views culminating near Lausanne. Signature challenge: The 'Romandie Rollercoaster': 8500ft over 100 miles of Swiss climbs, blending endurance with explosive efforts akin to pro Queen stages.

### 947 Ride Joburg
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 60 mi | 3,937 ft | Johannesburg, South Africa | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/947-ride-joburg/

Africa's greatest mass-participation cycling event. Own the streets of Joburg. A rolling 97km urban road race at altitude through Johannesburg's streets, highways, and suburbs. The course is deceptively challenging - the constant altitude above 1,500m and rolling M1 highway sections wear riders down more than the elevation numbers suggest. Signature challenge: The M1 highway. Seventeen kilometers of rolling highway that car drivers barely notice but cyclists feel in their legs. At 1,500m+ altitude, with 60km already in the legs, the false flats and headwinds of the M1 South section separate those who paced well from those who went out too hard on the Kyalami circuit. The 947 Ride Joburg is not the hardest race you will ever do. It is not the most technical, the longest, or the most remote. But it might be the most electric. Twenty thousand riders taking over the M1 highway, lapping Kyalami, and finishing at FNB Stadium in Soweto creates an atmosphere that makes your chest tight before you even clip in. The race is Johannesburg itself - ambitious, energetic, slightly chaotic, and completely unforgettable. If you want to understand South African cycling culture, this is where you start. If you want to experience the energy of 20,000+ riders owning the streets of one of Africa's great cities, this race delivers. It is not an extreme endurance test or a technical challenge. It is a celebration of cycling in Johannesburg, and the atmosphere alone makes it worth the entry fee. For international visitors, the favorable exchange rate and the unique experience of riding the M1 highway and Kyalami circuit make it a compelling bucket-list event.

### Amashova National Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 66 mi | 3,200 ft | Pietermaritzburg to Durban, KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/amashova-national-classic/

South Africa's iconic mass-participation classic blending Comrades legacy with Valley of a Thousand Hills beauty.

### Arlberg Giro
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 93 mi | 8,200 ft | St. Anton am Arlberg, Tyrol, Austria | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/arlberg-giro/

150 km of Austrian Alpine gran fondo perfection — two passes, world-class scenery, and ski-resort-level organization in one of the Alps' most charming villages. A circular alpine gran fondo starting and finishing in St. Anton am Arlberg, crossing two major mountain passes through the Austrian states of Tyrol and Vorarlberg. The route demands sustained climbing ability and confident descending on well-maintained alpine roads with world-class mountain scenery throughout. Signature challenge: The Silvretta Hochalpenstrasse climb: 22.5 km at 5.8% average to the Bielerhöhe at 2,032m — the defining ascent of the Arlberg Giro, arriving after 90+ km of riding and the Arlbergpass already in the legs. The Arlberg Giro is the kind of event that makes you understand why European gran fondos exist as a distinct category. 150 kilometers through the Austrian Alps with 2,500 meters of climbing, two major passes, and scenery that belongs on a postcard. The organization — run by the St. Anton tourism association with 13 editions of experience — is polished without being corporate. The weekend package of night sprint, professional criterium, and Sunday gran fondo creates a genuine cycling festival in one of the Alps' most charming villages. It is not the hardest gran fondo in Austria (the Otztaler has that locked), and it is not the most prestigious (no UCI sanction, no lottery mania). But it may be the most enjoyable — the balance of challenge, scenery, organization, and village atmosphere is hard to beat. If you want a well-organized Alpine gran fondo with stunning scenery and a genuine festival atmosphere — absolutely. This is European cycling at its most enjoyable. If you are chasing UCI points, maximum vertical, or global prestige — the Maratona dles Dolomites or Otztaler Radmarathon may be better targets. If you are coming from outside Europe, combine it with a broader Alps cycling trip to justify the travel.

### Axel Merckx Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 3,937 ft | Penticton, British Columbia, Canada | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/axel-merckx-gran-fondo/

Premier Canadian gran fondo in stunning wine country led by cycling legend Axel Merckx.

### Cyclassics Hamburg
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 96 mi | 1,150 ft | Hamburg, Germany | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cyclassics-hamburg/

Free entry. 22,000 riders. Closed roads through Germany's second city. The Waseberg at 16%. Europe's biggest cycling party — not its hardest ride. The Cyclassics Hamburg is not a race that tests your body. It tests your appreciation for what cycling events can be. Twenty-two thousand riders on closed roads through a major European city, alongside a UCI WorldTour professional race, with free registration — that combination exists nowhere else in the world. The physical challenge is minimal by any serious cyclist's standard: 109 km of flat roads with one 700-meter climb on the Waseberg, which is over before your heart rate settles into zone 5. You will not suffer here. You will not dig deep into reserves you did not know you had. What you will do is experience cycling as a mass cultural event — a city that shuts its streets for bikes, spectators lining the Waseberg and Mönckebergstraße, the energy of twenty thousand riders rolling through Hamburg's best scenery.

The prestige comes from the pro race connection. Since 1996, the Cyclassics has been Germany's flagship professional road race — the country's only event at cycling's highest level since the Deutschland Tour disappeared in 2009. Elia Viviani, Andre Greipel, Tyler Farrar, Peter Sagan, Alexander Kristoff — the sport's greatest sprinters have won on these roads. Riding the same tarmac, on the same day, on closed roads, with the same finish line on Mönckebergstraße: that means something. The Waseberg climb in Blankenese — 700 meters at gradients touching 16%, rising sharply from the Elbe's north bank — is the one moment where the amateur and professional experiences converge in physical effort. The rest is about the atmosphere, the city, and the sheer improbability of free entry to an event of this caliber.

If you want a cycling challenge, go elsewhere. If you want to experience European cycling culture at its most accessible and democratic — the biggest party on two wheels, in one of Europe's great cities, for zero euros — Hamburg in August is the answer. Yes if you want to experience the scale and energy of one of Europe's largest mass-participation cycling events. Yes if the WorldTour pro race connection matters to you. Yes if free registration and world-class ASO organization sound too good to pass up. Yes if a cycling festival weekend in Hamburg appeals. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or a genuine physical test — this is flat and short. Skip if you want adventure, wilderness, or remoteness — this is a city sportive.

### Cyclotour du Léman
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 109 mi | 2,920 ft | Lausanne, Switzerland | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cyclotour-du-leman/

Switzerland's largest non-competitive cyclosportive: stunning Lake Geneva loop for all levels.

### Ford RideLondon
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 3,900 ft | London / Essex, England | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/ford-ridelondon/

The world's greatest festival of cycling: 25,000 riders, 100 miles of closed London and Essex roads, and an Olympic legacy that raised £85 million for charity across 10 editions. A tale of two halves: iconic London landmarks on closed roads bookend a flat, fast loop through the Essex countryside. The experience is the London sections; the miles are the Essex sections. Signature challenge: The challenge is not technical or vertical — it is completing 100 miles in the company of 25,000 other riders, navigating the dense peloton safely, and managing your energy across a flat course where the temptation to go too fast too early is constant. The Essex headwinds on the return leg have ended many riders' centuries prematurely. Ford RideLondon was the cycling equivalent of the London Marathon — a mass-participation event where the experience of riding through the capital on closed roads mattered more than your finishing time. Born from the 2012 Olympics, it inspired half a million people to ride and raised £85 million for charity across 10 editions. The move from Surrey to Essex removed the dramatic hills but kept the unique thrill of London's streets without traffic. Now on indefinite pause, it leaves a gap that no other British cycling event can fill. If RideLondon returns from its indefinite pause, it is an automatic yes for anyone who wants to ride through central London on closed roads surrounded by 25,000 other cyclists. It is the perfect first century — flat, fully supported, brilliantly organized. It is not an event for riders seeking physical challenge, technical terrain, or competitive racing. It is an event for riders who want an unforgettable experience on two wheels in one of the world's great cities.

### Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 40.4 mi | 951 ft | Wevelgem, West Flanders, Belgium | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gent-wevelgem-cyclo/

A prestige-laden, historically rich mirror of a UCI WorldTour classic with multiple distance options and genuine Flemish character—ideal for riders seeking authentic spring classic experience. A prestige-laden Flemish spring classic featuring wind-exposed polders, cobbled climbs, and war-history landmarks. Less mountainous than Ardennes classics but technically demanding with steep, unpredictable terrain. Routes emphasize the Kemmelberg's notorious 20% cobbled gradient and multiple ascents of Baneberg, Monteberg, and Plugstreets' unpaved sections. Finish is flat after final Kemmelberg descent, allowing for sprint finishes. Strong headwind exposure and echelon-forming conditions typical. Signature challenge: The Kemmelberg: A steep (20% gradient), cobbled climb ascended twice in the race finale. The descent is notoriously technical and dangerous, with unpredictable terrain and tight turns. Riders must manage both the physical demand and technical bike-handling skill; crashes are common in the professional race. Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo is the thinking cyclist's spring classic—a chance to suffer on the same cobbles and climbs that define professional racing's most storied one-day event. The Kemmelberg will humble you, the Plugstreets will test your bike handling, and passing through Ypres's Menin Gate adds gravitas that most sportives lack. Late March weather is unpredictable and often brutal, but that's part of the Flemish classic experience. If you want to understand why cyclists revere the spring classics, this is essential. Not the easiest or most scenic ride, but undeniably authentic. Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo delivers what few sportives can: direct access to a legendary professional race's course, complete with the Kemmelberg's infamous cobbles, Plugstreets' technical unpaved sections, and the emotional weight of riding through Ypres's Menin Gate. The 2026 edition's alignment with the pro race's new Middelkerke-to-Wevelgem route ensures you're riding current UCI WorldTour terrain. Multiple distance options (65–220 km) make it accessible to various fitness levels, though even the shortest routes include serious climbing and technical descents. Late March conditions are typically cool, wet, and windy—classic Flemish spring racing weather that adds authenticity but demands preparation. At an estimated €47–60 entry with 5,000 participants (40% international), it offers strong community and prestige without premium pricing. Best suited for riders who value cycling history, don't mind suffering on cobbles, and want to experience the spring classics' soul rather than chase personal records.

### GFNY New York
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 85 mi | 6,380 ft | Fort Lee, New Jersey / Hudson Valley, New York, USA | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-new-york/

100 miles from the George Washington Bridge to Bear Mountain and back. The world championship of gran fondos, started by two people who paid $50K to close the busiest bridge in America. An out-and-back loop that starts with one of cycling's most dramatic send-offs — 5,000 riders rolling across the closed George Washington Bridge — and then heads north through the Hudson Valley's mix of river flats, suburban rollers, and genuine mountain terrain. The first half builds toward Bear Mountain, the course's physical and emotional apex. The second half punishes you with the Rockland County ridgeline — a relentless sequence of sharp climbs and technical descents that hit when your legs are already compromised. The final miles back along 9W are a flat-road time trial to the finish in Fort Lee. The course rewards riders who can climb, recover on descents, and still produce power in the final 30 miles. Signature challenge: Bear Mountain gets the name recognition, but the Rockland County ridgeline is the real race-breaker. Gate Hill and Cheesecote hit when you're 55-65 miles deep with 5,000+ feet already in the legs. The 18% maximum on Cheesecote stops riders who are already cooked. The course is designed to punish poor pacing — it's back-loaded by design. GFNY New York is the race that proved American gran fondos could be taken seriously. When Uli and Lidia Fluhme paid the Port Authority $50,000 to close the George Washington Bridge for a cycling event in 2011, they were making a bet that the US market wanted European-style competitive cycling with real timing, real anti-doping, and real racing — not another charity ride with a fruit cup at the finish. Fifteen years and 30+ global editions later, that bet has paid off spectacularly. The NYC World Championship draws 5,000 riders from 90+ countries to race 100 miles through the Hudson Valley, summit Bear Mountain, and grind through the Rockland County ridgeline. The course is demanding without being mythical. The organization is professional without being corporate. And that start on the closed GWB — with the Manhattan skyline receding behind you as 5,000 riders roll out together — remains one of the most visually stunning moments in amateur cycling. Yes if you want the most competitive, most international gran fondo in North America. Yes if starting on a closed George Washington Bridge with the NYC skyline behind you is on your bucket list. Yes if you want anti-doping, real timing, and a world championship classification. Skip if you want Alpine grandeur — this is the Hudson Valley, not the Dolomites. Skip if NYC-area costs are a dealbreaker.

### GFNY Pittsburgh
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 70 mi | 7,756 ft | Monroeville, Pennsylvania, USA | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-pittsburgh/

The hardest GFNY USA event delivers relentless climbing and professional organization, but only for climbers. GFNY Pittsburgh is a relentless climbing test through the rolling hills of Allegheny and Westmoreland Counties. The 70-mile route features 10 signature climbs distributed throughout, meaning there are no flat recovery sections—just sustained elevation gain from start to finish. The course winds through scenic countryside with paved roads and police-moderated intersections, offering beautiful vistas of the region while delivering genuine suffering. This is not a technical challenge or an adventure ride; it's a pure climbing fitness test designed to separate strong climbers from the rest. The medium 41-mile option shares the same 10 climbs, making it a legitimate alternative for building fitness. Signature challenge: The 10 signature climbs distributed throughout the 70-mile course define GFNY Pittsburgh. With 7,756 feet of elevation gain over 70 miles (111 feet per mile), there is no mercy—every section climbs. The climbs are shared across both long and medium distance options, meaning the medium course tackles the same brutal climbing in fewer miles. This is climbing-focused racing at its most unforgiving, with no flat sections to recover and no technical descents to break the monotony. GFNY Pittsburgh is a masterclass in organized climbing. The 70-mile route with 7,756 feet of elevation gain is genuinely the hardest GFNY USA event, and the organization reflects three decades of mass-participation racing expertise. This is not a technical challenge or an adventure ride—it's a pure climbing test on paved roads with professional support. If you're seeking to prove your climbing fitness against a quality field and global GFNY brand, Pittsburgh delivers. The inaugural 2025 event executed flawlessly, and the September timing avoids extreme weather. However, the road-only format and lack of technical terrain limit broader appeal. This is a specialist event for climbers, not a well-rounded cycling challenge. Race GFNY Pittsburgh if you're a climber seeking to prove fitness against quality competition with GFNY's global prestige and three decades of organizational expertise. Skip it if you prefer technical terrain, adventure riding, or well-rounded cycling challenges—this is pure climbing, nothing more.

### Gran Fondo Il Lombardia Felice Gimondi
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 68 mi | 5,577 ft | Cantu, Como, Lombardy, Italy | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-il-lombardia/

The last Monument of the season — now yours to ride. Muro di Sormano at 27%. Madonna del Ghisallo. Lake Como in the falling leaves. A race of two legendary climbs bookended by the rolling Brianza hills. The route starts and finishes in Cantu, diving immediately into the undulating roads of the Brianza — the gentle, green foothills south of Lake Como that lull you into thinking this will be manageable. It is not. Within 30 km, the road tilts upward toward the Colma di Sormano, and then the gradient goes vertical: the Muro di Sormano, 1.9 km at 15.8% average with ramps touching 27%. After the Muro's savage descent, the route winds along Lake Como toward the second act: the Madonna del Ghisallo, 8.5 km of steady climbing to the church of cycling's patron saint. A sweeping 20 km descent to Merone gives temporary respite before the final 20 km of short, sharp climbs back to Cantu destroy whatever is left in your legs. The autumn colors, the lake views, the chapel at the Ghisallo summit — this course is as beautiful as it is brutal. Signature challenge: The Muro di Sormano. 1.9 kilometers at 27% maximum gradient. The road that was removed from the professional Monument for being too cruel. The road that was reinstated because cycling realized it needed places where the strongest riders separate from the merely strong. On the Gran Fondo, the Muro is where your training, your gearing, your body weight, and your willingness to suffer converge into a single, vertical question: can you ride this wall? There are five Monuments in professional cycling. Five races that define the sport's soul. Il Lombardia is the last of them — the autumn closer, the Classic of the Falling Leaves, the race that has crowned the season's strongest one-day rider since 1905. And now you can ride it. The Gran Fondo Il Lombardia puts you on the Muro di Sormano — 27% gradient, the road so brutal it was removed from the professional race for three decades. It sends you up the Madonna del Ghisallo, past the church of cycling's patron saint, with Lake Como spread out below like a postcard that became reality. It wraps you in the autumn colors that gave the race its name. RCS Sport — the people who run the Giro d'Italia — handle the organization. Felice Gimondi's name is on the event. And the day after you finish, you can stand on the same roads and watch Pogacar, van der Poel, and Evenepoel race the Monument itself. This is as close as amateur cycling gets to the sacred. Yes if you want to experience a cycling Monument from the inside — the same climbs, the same roads, the same autumn atmosphere that has defined professional cycling's season finale since 1905. Yes if the Muro di Sormano's 27% gradient is the kind of challenge that excites rather than terrifies you. Yes if UCI age-group competition and World Championship qualifying matter to your cycling ambitions. Skip if you want a pure gravel or off-road challenge. Skip if the October rain risk in northern Italy concerns you. Skip if you prefer large, festival-atmosphere events over the focused intensity of a competitive Italian gran fondo.

### TotalEnergies Gran Fondo Alberto Contador
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 91 mi | 8,327 ft | Oliva, Valencia, Spain | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-alberto-contador/

Ride with a legend. 146 km through the Costa Blanca mountains, alongside the man who won the Tour, the Giro, and the Vuelta — and still shows up to pedal with the field. A circular route from the Mediterranean coast into the limestone mountain passes of the Costa Blanca interior and back. The first kilometers are flat — the Valencian coastal plain around Oliva is rice-paddy territory, sea-level warm-up before the mountains begin. Then the road tilts upward and does not truly relent for 80 km. The climbing is concentrated through the Marina Alta and Vall de Gallinera: a series of 5-8 km passes at 5-6% average gradient, connected by rolling valley roads through ancient stone villages. Coll de Rates is the signature — the most famous cycling climb on the Costa Blanca, a timed section that separates those who paced wisely from those who did not. Sa Creueta is the high point, with its sharp final kilometer and panoramic ridge views. The Vall d'Ebo descent through bare limestone landscape feels like riding through a geology textbook. The return to Oliva via Pego is mercifully flat, a Mediterranean homecoming with the salt air announcing the finish before you see it. Signature challenge: Coll de Rates — the timed section that defines the competitive day. 6.6 km of consistent climbing at 5.3% average, arriving early enough in the route (km 30) to tempt riders into going too hard. The climb itself is not the hardest on the route — Sa Creueta is steeper, Vall d'Ebo is longer — but the timed section creates a race-within-a-race dynamic. Ride it as a time trial and pay later. Ride it as a tempo effort and carry strength through the remaining 116 km. Every year, Coll de Rates sorts the field into those who raced the climb and those who raced the event. The Gran Fondo Alberto Contador is the rare event where the name on the marquee actually shows up and rides with you. Alberto Contador — two-time Tour de France winner, two-time Giro champion, three-time Vuelta conqueror, one of the seven members of the Grand Tour triple crown club and arguably the most devastating pure attacker of his generation — does not just lend his name to this event. He starts at the front, drops back to wait for the last rider, and works his way through the entire peloton, greeting cyclists, posing for photos, and probably putting in a few accelerations that remind everyone within visual range exactly why he won seven Grand Tours. The route through the Costa Blanca's interior mountains — Coll de Rates, Sa Creueta, the Vall de Gallinera — is a concentrated dose of Mediterranean climbing at its finest. The Fundacion Alberto Contador channels the event's proceeds into cycling development, stroke awareness, and providing bikes to disadvantaged communities. This is not a vanity project. It is a UCI-sanctioned qualifier with real competitive structure, ridden through genuinely beautiful mountains, organized by professionals, and anchored by a champion who clearly still loves the bike. Yes if you want the once-in-a-lifetime experience of riding alongside Alberto Contador through Mediterranean mountain passes. Yes if a UCI qualifier with timed climbing sections suits your competitive instincts. Yes if the Costa Blanca's combination of mountain cycling and beach recovery sounds like the perfect September weekend. Skip if you want pure mountain extremity — the passes top out under 800m. Skip if you need a massive international field and English-language everything — this event is proudly Spanish first. Skip if September Mediterranean weather risk gives you anxiety.

### Granfondo Bulgaria
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 87 mi | 6,250 ft | Velingrad, Bulgaria | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-bulgaria/

First-ever UCI Gran Fondo qualifier in Bulgaria's stunning Rhodope Mountains Granfondo Bulgaria debuts as Bulgaria's first UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier, delivering 140km/1905m of Rhodope Mountain suffering around stunning Batak Reservoir. The 21.6km @ 5.4% monster climb (km 78-100) crowns the highest point at 1337m, flanked by long rewarding descents. Velingrad's spa culture and Balkan beauty elevate this beyond typical qualifiers. Strong T2 contender for Eastern European climbers seeking World Champs qualification.

### Granfondo Eddy Merckx
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 101 mi | 8,674 ft | Andenne, Wallonia, Belgium | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-eddy-merckx/

The greatest cyclist's namesake gran fondo. 162 km, 18 climbs, 2,644m of Walloon Ardennes punishment. The Cannibal's roads. A race of 18 successive walls through the Walloon Ardennes, bookended by the Meuse river valley at Andenne. The route begins with an immediate climb — the Cote de Peu d'Eau, 2.7 km of La Fleche Wallonne terrain that sets the tone from the gun. There is no warmup. From there, the course winds through the Condroz plateau — the rolling upland between the Meuse and Ourthe valleys — hitting classified climb after classified climb in a rhythm that is uniquely Belgian: short, steep, partial recovery, repeat. The signature moment is the Triple Mur de Monty — 1.9 km with three successive ramps hitting 20-24%, each one arriving just as you think the worst is over. Between the named climbs, the terrain never flattens. The Condroz is a landscape of gentle ridges and hidden valleys, and every false flat tilts upward. The descents are fast but technical — narrow communal roads through forest, hedgerows tight against the tarmac, the occasional farm vehicle adding authenticity. The final approach back to Andenne delivers panoramic views over the Meuse valley, a finishing reward for six-plus hours of Ardennes punishment. On a clear June day, the green Walloon countryside — stone farmhouses, church spires, cows in fields that slope toward hidden streams — is hauntingly beautiful. On a wet one, it is Belgium. Signature challenge: The Triple Mur de Monty. Three successive walls hitting 20-24% gradient over 1.9 km. A sign at the steepest point reads 23.9%. Tougher than the Mur de Huy that decides La Fleche Wallonne. On the Granfondo Eddy Merckx, the Triple Mur is where you discover whether your training, your gearing, your body weight, and your willingness to suffer are sufficient for Belgian hills. The Cannibal would have attacked on the first ramp and been over the top before most riders reached the second. You are not The Cannibal. There is one name in cycling that sits above all others. Not Coppi. Not Anquetil. Not Hinault. Eddy Merckx — The Cannibal — won 525 races, 11 Grand Tours, all five Monuments at least twice, three World Championships, and the Hour Record. He was so dominant that rivals stopped trying to win and raced for second. And this gran fondo bears his name. The Granfondo Eddy Merckx sends you out of Andenne on the Cote de Peu d'Eau — La Fleche Wallonne terrain — and then hammers you across 162 km of Walloon Ardennes hills. Eighteen classified climbs. The Triple Mur de Monty with its three successive 20%+ ramps. 2,644 meters of climbing that never lets you recover. This is the terrain that forged Belgian cycling's greatest generation. Golazo runs it with typical Belgian efficiency. The UCI sanctions it for World Championship qualifying. And at EUR 44, it costs less than dinner in Brussels. Yes if you want to ride in the name of the greatest cyclist who ever lived — through the Ardennes terrain that forged Belgian cycling's dominance. Yes if 18 climbs in 162 km excites rather than terrifies you. Yes if UCI age-group competition and World Championship qualifying matter to your ambitions. Yes if Belgium's cycling culture — the deepest in the world — is something you want to experience from the inside. Skip if you want warm weather guarantees. Skip if you prefer large festival-atmosphere events over focused European gran fondo racing. Skip if the relentless, puncheur-style climbing of the Ardennes is not your terrain.

### Granfondo La Guadeloupe
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 77.1 mi | 9,459 ft | Capesterre-Belle-Eau, Guadeloupe | December | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-la-guadeloupe/

Exotic UCI qualifier blending steep climbs and Caribbean allure. Coastal undulations to major climb, then rolling west with smaller ascent; tropical mountain roads with sea and volcano views. Signature challenge: Col des Mamelles climb Brand new UCI Gran Fondo in a stunning tropical setting—perfect for winter training with Worlds qualifier spots and post-race beach recovery. The Col des Mamelles climb delivers the pain amid paradise vistas; pair it with the discovery ride for full island immersion. Logistics favor Europeans, but worth the trip for adventure seekers. Granfondo La Guadeloupe stands out as a fresh 2025 addition to the calendar, offering 124km/2883m of tropical road suffering with top-25% age-group qualification to 2026 Worlds in Japan. Signature 13.5km Col des Mamelles (4km at >8.5%) anchors a scenic Basse-Terre loop from Capesterre-Belle-Eau. Ideal for amateurs seeking prestige points in paradise, with expo, non-competitive 80km option, and abundant tourism. Travel via Pointe-à-Pitre airport; French infrastructure ensures quality support.

Epic climbs to NJ's highest point on quiet roads, just 30 minutes from NYC, with timed racing in a national series.

### L'Etape Turkey by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 66.4 mi | 5,200 ft | Beykoz, Istanbul, Turkey | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-turkey/

Tour prestige meets Turkish splendor in a 106km stunner. Beykoz loop unleashes Istanbul's wild side—paved climbs snake through forests, villages, and pavilions. Traffic-free bliss lets you smash king-of-mountain segments amid Polonezkoy charm. 106km blends endurance grind with touristic euphoria, Tour-style without the jet lag. Signature challenge: Beykoz's cumulative climbs rack 1600m+ over 66 miles, blending relentless gradients with Istanbul panoramas. No single monster but endless testing forges Tour grit. Traffic-free freedom amplifies the hurt. L'Etape Turkey blasts onto the scene fusing Bosphorus beauty with Tour de France pedigree—106km of paved punishment through Beykoz's hidden gems. Traffic-free pro treatment elevates amateurs to heroes amid 3K global riders. Prestige punches way above untested climbs; Istanbul's allure seals the deal for adventure-starved roadies. Minor unknowns like vert pale against the hype. Yes if craving exotic road prestige sans Alps agony. Skip if chasing 4K+ vert or gravel grit.

### London Edinburgh London
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 950 mi | 36,000 ft | Chelmsford, Essex, UK | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/london-edinburgh-london/

Ultimate UK ultra-randonnée: 950 miles self-supported to Edinburgh and back.

### Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 7.6 mi | 4,618 ft | Gorham, New Hampshire | August | hillclimb
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/mt-washington-hillclimb/

7.6 miles. 4,678 feet. 12% average grade. Summit finish on the mountain with the worst weather in America. Since 1973. There is no subtlety to the Mt. Washington Auto Road Bicycle Hillclimb. It is 7.6 miles of going uphill at 12% average gradient until you reach the summit of the highest peak in the Northeastern United States, a mountain so hostile that it holds the world record for the highest wind gust ever directly measured on Earth's surface. Since 1973, cyclists have lined up at the base of the Auto Road to race the clock, the gradient, and whatever weather the mountain decides to deliver. Tom Danielson did it in 49:24. Tyler Hamilton did it four times (though his records were vacated). Ian Boswell set the current mark at 50:18. Most riders take 60-90 minutes. All of them suffer.

The Mt. Washington Hillclimb is not a long race. It is not a technical race. It is not an altitude race. It is a gradient race on a mountain with a personality disorder — a 6,288-foot peak in New Hampshire that produces weather systems normally reserved for peaks three times its height. The 2022 completion of the road paving project reset the records and the conversation: every time set on the fully paved road is a modern mark. The 50th edition that year was a line in the sand. Everything before it was a different race on a different surface. Everything after is the pure, paved truth: how fast can you ride uphill at 12% for 7.6 miles into the worst weather in America?

This is not a race you enter casually. The $350 entry fee, the remote New Hampshire location, and the single-digit mileage all conspire to make this a deliberate choice. You come to Mt. Washington because you want to test yourself against the steepest sustained road climb in the Eastern United States. You stay because the summit finish — fleece blanket in hand, legs destroyed, staring out across the Presidential Range — is one of the most earned moments in American cycling. Yes if you are a hill climb specialist, power-to-weight obsessive, or simply need to know if you can ride up the steepest sustained paved climb in the Eastern US. Yes if you want a bucket-list summit finish on the highest peak in the Northeast. Skip if you want distance, technical terrain, or a mass-participation festival atmosphere. Skip if remote New Hampshire logistics are a dealbreaker.

### Peaks Challenge Falls Creek
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 146 mi | 13,123 ft | Falls Creek, Victoria, Australia | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/peaks-challenge-falls-creek/

Australia's definitive mountain challenge. Three alpine summits. 235 km. 4,000 meters of climbing in a single day. A linear epic across the Victorian Alps. The three-peak structure gives the ride a natural narrative: conquer Hotham, survive the valley heat, endure Tawonga, and somehow find the legs for Falls Creek at the end. The terrain alternates between long sustained climbs to cool alpine summits and hot, exposed valley roads where the sun beats down relentlessly. There is almost no flat ground — you are either climbing, descending, or wishing you were descending. Signature challenge: The cumulative weight of 235 km and 4,000m. No single climb is the hardest you'll ever ride, but stacking three alpine summits in a single day — with valley heat between them — creates an event where the whole is devastatingly greater than the sum of its parts. Peaks Challenge Falls Creek is Australia's answer to the great European mountain cyclosportives, and it holds its own. The format is brutally simple: climb three alpine summits, cover 235 km, gain 4,000 meters. The Victorian High Country provides the backdrop — eucalyptus forests, alpine meadows, panoramic ridgeline views — but the real story is the sheer physical and mental challenge of the day. Mount Hotham's 30 km climb is the physical crux. The final ascent to Falls Creek at kilometer 220, on legs that have been working for 10+ hours, is the emotional one. If you finish, you know exactly what you're made of. Yes if you want a genuine ultra-distance mountain cycling challenge outside Europe. Yes if completing Australia's most respected single-day cycling event is on your list. Skip if you can't commit to training for 10-14 hours in the saddle. Skip if the 4.5-hour drive from Melbourne (or long-haul flight from overseas) is a dealbreaker.

### Race Across Belgium
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 621 mi | 39,042 ft | Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg (traverse) | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-across-belgium/

A distinctive European ultra-cycling challenge combining Benelux geography, cycling heritage, and demanding terrain for serious endurance cyclists.

### Race Across Benelux

Epic ultra-road odyssey blending flat crossings, Ardennes climbs, and Benelux culture.

### Škoda Ring of Clare Sportive

Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/ring-of-clare/

Ireland's most scenic and festive road sportive: cliffs, cakes, and community magic. A scenic loop showcasing County Clare's rugged cliffs, rolling hills, quiet country lanes, and Shannon Estuary views; festive atmosphere with challenging yet rewarding climbs. Signature challenge: The Hand climb combined with coastal winds and estuary rollers, blending steep efforts with endurance-testing scenery.

### Sado Long Ride 210
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 130 mi | 9,843 ft | Sado Island, Niigata Prefecture, Japan | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/sado-long-ride-210/

Japan's premier island road epic: breathtaking coasts meet savage climbs.

### Seorak GranFondo
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 129 mi | 11,500 ft | Gangwon-do, South Korea | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/seorak-granfondo/

Brutal 11,500ft paved beast through Seoraksan—Korea’s climb fest for the masochistic.

### Tour de Big Bear
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 8,500 ft | Big Bear Lake, California, USA | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-big-bear/

100 miles at 7,000 feet with legendary aid stations that serve ribs, have DJs, and make you forget you are dying on a bike. A deceptive course that lulls you with rolling lakeside roads for 50 miles before delivering the altitude hammer in the second half. The Onyx Summit climb at 8,443 feet is the crown jewel, and the Cedar Falls descent-and-return is where tired legs at altitude learn about humility. Signature challenge: Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet. The highest paved road in Southern California, reached after 50+ miles of riding at altitude. The KOM/QOM competition makes the climb honest. Then you descend 2,000 feet to Cedar Falls and must climb back up. The Tour de Big Bear is what happens when a Southern California cycling community builds their dream ride: 100 miles at 7,000 feet with 8,500 feet of climbing, legendary aid stations that serve BBQ ribs and have DJ booths, and a mountain lake setting that delivers 55-65F start temperatures while the rest of SoCal melts in August.

The climbing is real. Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet is the highest paved road in Southern California, and the KOM competition up its slopes draws genuine efforts. The profile is back-loaded and punishing: rolling lakeside roads lull you for 50 miles, then the Onyx push and Cedar Falls loop deliver the hurt. The 12:30 PM cutoff at Blue Sky Meadows is there for a reason -- undertrained riders bonk at altitude.

But the aid stations are what make this event legendary. Full buffets, DJs, couches, ribs, live music. The volunteers are locals who have been doing this for 15 years and take genuine pride in their hospitality. Named the #1 Gran Fondo in North America by Gran Fondo Guide in 2023. If you are a SoCal cyclist, this is your annual pilgrimage. Yes if you want the definitive SoCal mountain cycling experience with altitude, climbing, and aid stations that redefine hospitality. Skip if you cannot handle 7,000+ foot altitude, need a sanctioned race, or live far from California.

### Tour de Fukushima
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 87 mi | 6,562 ft | Hamadori coastal region, Fukushima Prefecture | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-fukushima/

UCI Gran Fondo qualifier through Fukushima's recovery zones Two-day format with **time trial**, **granfondo (140km)**, and **mediofondo (80km)** options through Hamadori coastal region. Routes traverse Pacific coastline, rugged inland highlands, and recently de-lifted evacuation zones (Namie/Odaka areas). **Suffering zones**: sustained climbs in coastal headwinds through disaster-affected terrain. Unique blend of competitive UCI gran fondo racing with profound cultural recovery narrative through recently decontaminated evacuation zones. Must-do for Japan-based riders seeking Niseko Worlds qualification.

Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-tucson/

Massive Tucson perimeter sportive blending charity, desert miles, and community buzz.

A masterpiece of route planning offering some of the UK's best road cycling scenery on fully closed roads with exceptional organisation and community spirit.

### SBS Bank Tour of Southland

### Tour of Thekkady — Kerala Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 90 mi | 9,800 ft | Kottayam to Thekkady, Kerala, India | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-of-thekkady/

India's must-do Gran Fondo: TdF climbs meet Kerala paradise. A scenic rollercoaster of relentless climbs through Kerala's lush plantations, serene lakes, iconic dams, and wildlife-rich forests, blending Tour de France-style elevation with tropical tranquility on smooth, low-traffic roads. Signature challenge: The 3000m+ elevation gain over 145km, replicating TdF stage difficulty amid Kerala's ever-changing landscapes from backwaters to highland forests.

### UCI Gran Fondo World Championships
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 87 mi | 7,776 ft | Venue rotates annually (2026: Niseko, Hokkaido, Japan) | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/uci-gran-fondo-world-championships/

The only UCI World Championship for amateur road cyclists. Qualify through 36 global events. Race 2,000 riders from 58 countries. Win the rainbow jersey. Course character changes completely with each host venue, but the UCI imposes design principles that create consistency: the Gran Fondo road race must challenge climbing ability, reward tactical racing, and culminate in a decisive finale. The 2026 Niseko course epitomizes this: 140 km through Hokkaido's volcanic mountain landscape, starting near sea level, building to an 840m main climb at the halfway mark, and finishing with a steep final kilometer that rewards explosive power on tired legs. The Medio Fondo at 80 km delivers the same decisive finish with 1,572m of climbing. The individual time trial on a flat 16.3 km course tests pure power. Three disciplines, three different demands, one championship. Signature challenge: The qualification system IS the signature challenge. Before you even reach the start line, you must race a UCI-sanctioned qualifier event and finish in the top 25% of your age group. Then you must travel to the championship venue — a different country each year — and race against 2,000 of the best amateur cyclists from 58 nations. The course varies, but the depth of competition does not. Winning a rainbow jersey requires not just fitness, but a year-long competitive commitment. The UCI Gran Fondo World Championships are the only event in amateur road cycling where you can earn the rainbow jersey — the single most recognizable symbol of cycling supremacy. This is not a participation sportive. It is a qualified-entry world championship with anti-doping, commissaire panels, and age-group classifications that attract the strongest amateur road cycling field assembled anywhere on the planet. You earn your start by finishing in the top 25% of your age group at one of 36 qualifying events worldwide. Then you race 2,000 riders from 58 countries on a course that changes every year — from Italian mountains to Danish flatlands to Japanese volcanoes. The 2026 edition in Niseko, Hokkaido is the first ever held in Asia: 140 km with 2,370m of climbing through volcanic mountain terrain, 1.5 hours from Tokyo. The rainbow jersey awaits at the finish. One winner per age group. For the rest, there is the knowledge that you raced the deepest amateur field in the world. Yes if you are a competitive age-group cyclist who wants the ultimate test against your global peers. Yes if the rainbow jersey means something to you. Yes if you want a multi-year global cycling adventure — different country, different course, every year. Skip if you want a fixed, repeatable course to benchmark against. Skip if the qualification requirement and international travel expense are dealbreakers. Skip if you want a participation experience — this is a race.

### AIDS/LifeCycle
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 545 mi | 25,000 ft | San Francisco to Los Angeles, California, USA | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/aids-lifecycle/

The most emotionally powerful cycling event ever created. 545 miles of purpose, community, and remembrance down the California coast. Seven days of California coastal beauty and emotional intensity. The route traverses the full diversity of the state's Central Coast — from San Francisco fog through redwood forests, over mountain ridges, through agricultural valleys, past wine country, over coastal passes, and along the Pacific Coast Highway to a Santa Monica finish. The terrain is varied but always paved, with significant climbing concentrated on Days 1, 3, 4, and 6. The real challenge is cumulative: 545 miles over 7 consecutive days in the saddle, compounded by camping, emotional weight, and California's variable June weather. Signature challenge: The cumulative toll of 545 miles over 7 days. No single day is impossible, but the relentless accumulation of miles, climbing, weather, camping, and emotional weight creates an endurance challenge that transforms riders. The body adapts; the spirit grows. By Day 7, riding into Santa Monica feels like crossing into a different version of your life. AIDS/LifeCycle is not a race. It is not a gran fondo. It is not a charity ride in the way most people understand charity rides. It is 545 miles of collective grief, joy, defiance, and love, pedaled down the California coast by thousands of people who refuse to let a disease define or defeat their community. You will climb the Quadbuster with disco music blasting. You will cry at camp listening to names of the dead read aloud. You will finish in Santa Monica having raised thousands of dollars and having been fundamentally changed by the experience. Over 30 years, this event raised more than $300 million and created a community that transcends cycling. The 2025 final ride ended an era — but the legacy is permanent. AIDS/LifeCycle ended in 2025 after 30 years and $300 million raised. If you rode it, you know what it meant. If you never did, the successor event Cycle to Zero (3-day, SFAF-produced) launches in May 2026. The 545-mile journey from San Francisco to Los Angeles is gone, but the community and mission endure. This was never about the miles — it was about why you rode them.

### GFNY Chiang Mai
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 75 mi | 5,500 ft | Chiang Mai, Thailand | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/chiang-mai-gran-fondo/

GFNY's tropical road epic: big climbs, Thai vibes, unbeatable value. Rolling road gran fondo through Chiang Mai's lush mountains, rice terraces, and rural villages, featuring sustained climbs and fast descents in northern Thailand's scenic highlands. Signature challenge: The 'Chiang Mai Wall' - a 3km climb at 9% average with sections hitting 14%, offering panoramic views of the Ping River Valley.

### Les Cinglés du Mont Ventoux
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 85 mi | 14,436 ft | Bédoin, Vaucluse, Provence, France | June | hillclimb
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cingles-du-mont-ventoux/

Three faces. One mountain. 4,400 meters of climbing. The most honest challenge in cycling — EUR 20 and a brevet card against the Beast of Provence. Three ascents of the same mountain from three different directions, linked by rolling Provençal country roads through lavender fields and vineyards. Each climb has a distinct personality: Bédoin is the classic — dense forest giving way to exposed moonscape, the route the Tour de France made famous. Malaucène is the underestimated equal — steep early ramps, varied terrain, fewer cyclists. Sault is the long, deceptive approach — gentle gradients through lavender plateau that lull you before the final shared summit cone brutalizes you. All three routes converge in the last 6 km, where the trees vanish and the white limestone begins — a barren, lunar landscape that looks like no other place in cycling. The observatory tower at 1,912m is visible from kilometers away, growing imperceptibly closer with each pedal stroke. You pass the Tom Simpson memorial three times. You stand at the summit three times. And three times, you descend. Signature challenge: The third summit. The first ascent is excitement — the mountain is fresh, the legs are willing, the scenery is extraordinary. The second ascent is determination — the mountain is familiar, the legs are heavy, the summit seems farther away. The third ascent is reckoning. Your body has already climbed 3,150 meters. The Sault approach's gentle gradients offer false hope. The final 6 km of exposed limestone — the same white moonscape you have already climbed twice — must be climbed one more time. The observatory tower is visible. The Tom Simpson memorial is ahead. The wind is blowing. The third summit is where the Cinglés challenge becomes exactly what its name promises: crazy. Les Cinglés du Mont Ventoux is not a race. It is not a sportive. It is not an organized event. It is a personal reckoning with the most revered mountain in cycling, repeated until either you or the mountain blinks. Three ascents — Bédoin's relentless forest grind, Malaucène's savage opening ramps, Sault's deceptively gentle approach — each delivering you to the same barren, windswept summit at 1,912m where an observatory tower stands like a lighthouse above the clouds and Tom Simpson's memorial reminds you that this mountain does not care about your suffering. The genius of the Cinglés is in its simplicity: EUR 20, a brevet card, three stamps, three summits, midnight to midnight. No marshals. No aid stations. No chip timing. Just you, the mountain, and 4,400 meters of vertical truth. The Club des Cinglés has existed since 1988 because Christian Pic understood something fundamental: some challenges do not need organization. They need audacity. The word 'cinglé' means 'crazy' in French, and by the third ascent — when your legs remember the first two and the summit looks impossibly far and the Mistral is shredding your jersey — you understand that it is not a name. It is a diagnosis. Yes if you want to experience the most complete day possible on a single mountain — three ascents, three landscapes, one barren summit that never gets less shocking. Yes if you value self-reliance and the purity of a challenge that has no finish-line arch, no announcer, no medal ceremony — just a stamped card and the knowledge of what you did. Yes if Mont Ventoux means something to you. Skip if you need organized event support, medical coverage, and feed stations. Skip if Provençal summer heat is a deal-breaker. Skip if you want to race against other people rather than against yourself.

### Cycle Oregon
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 300 mi | 17,000 ft | Southern Oregon — Grants Pass and Jacksonville basecamps (2026). Route and region change annually. | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cycle-oregon/

Oregon's iconic multi-day touring event. 600 riders. Five days. A different Oregon every year. Cycle Oregon is Oregon distilled into a cycling event. Since 1988, this nonprofit organization has been taking riders into the corners of Oregon that most Oregonians have never seen — the Wallowa Mountains, the Applegate Valley, the high desert, the coast — and doing it with a level of care and support that turns five days on a bike into something approaching a life experience. The 2023 sunset of the legendary 7-day Classic was a genuine loss for American cycling, but the 5-day Rally format preserves the essence: a rolling community of 600 riders exploring Oregon's most spectacular landscapes, fully supported, with meals and camping and live music and the camaraderie that only comes from sharing the road for multiple days.

The price tag is real — $1,400-1,600 is not casual money — but what you get is five days of worry-free riding through terrain that will rearrange your understanding of Oregon. The route changes every year, meaning you could do Cycle Oregon for a decade and never repeat a mile. The Cycle Oregon Fund has donated millions to the rural communities that host the event, so your registration dollars are doing genuine good. The community is warm, the organization is impeccable, and the landscapes are world-class. If you want to race, go somewhere else. If you want to ride through one of the most beautiful states in America with 600 people who feel the same way, Cycle Oregon is it. You do not race Cycle Oregon — you experience it. Yes if you want a premium, fully supported multi-day cycling tour through some of the most beautiful scenery in the American West. Yes if you value community, adventure, and discovery over competition. Skip if you need a competitive event, if the $1,400+ price tag is prohibitive, or if you want a massive social scene (600 riders is intimate, not a festival).

### Death Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 103 mi | 14,000 ft | Markleeville, Alpine County, California | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/death-ride/

5 passes. 14,000 feet. The California Alps' ultimate climbing challenge since 1978. Relentless climbing. The up-and-back format on each pass means you're constantly ascending or descending. There is almost no flat riding. The passes come in waves — each summit brings the descent that takes you to the base of the next climb. By pass 4, the cumulative altitude exposure and climbing fatigue make every gradient feel steeper. Signature challenge: Ebbetts Pass — the steepest, most dramatic, and highest summit. Climbing to 8,732 ft on tired legs in the Sierra Nevada is the Death Ride's defining moment. The Death Ride is American road cycling's ultimate climbing challenge. 5 passes. 14,000 feet. One of the least-populated counties in California. No prize money, no timing chips, no Instagram-ready finish line — just you and the Sierra Nevada. Since 1978, serious cyclists have made the pilgrimage to Markleeville to test themselves against the California Alps. The name isn't hyperbole — it's a warning and a promise. If you finish all 5 passes, you've earned something that can't be bought. Yes if you want the defining high-altitude climbing experience in the American West. Yes if you can handle sustained riding above 8,000 ft. Skip if you need race infrastructure or a competitive field.

### Epic Gran Canaria

Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/epic-gran-canaria/

### Eroica Dolomiti
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 68 mi | 8,990 ft | San Candido, South Tyrol, Italy | September | sportive

### GFNY Bali
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 85 mi | 7,316 ft | Lebih, Klungkung, Bali, Indonesia | February | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-bali/

Climb from Bali's black-sand coast to a volcanic crater rim through rice terraces and Hindu temples — the most spectacular setting in the GFNY franchise. A point-to-summit-and-back volcanic mountain gran fondo that starts at Bali's coast, climbs relentlessly through the island's cultural and agricultural heartland to the rim of the Kintamani volcanic caldera, then descends back to the sea. The course is defined by one sustained ascent and one sustained descent, bookended by rolling coastal terrain. The climbing is not Alpine-steep but it is Alpine-long, and the tropical heat transforms moderate gradients into savage efforts. The Kintamani crater rim provides the emotional and physical summit — a view of Lake Danau Batur and Mount Batur that rewards every watt spent getting there. Signature challenge: The Kintamani volcano ascent. A 25-30 km sustained climb from the lowland rice terraces to the volcanic crater rim at 1,500 meters, performed in equatorial tropical heat. The gradient is steady (4-6% average, 8-12% pitches) but the length and conditions make it brutal. This is where the race breaks apart every year — the lead group of 15-20 fragments to 5-7 by the summit. The crater rim view of Lake Danau Batur is the payoff: one of the most dramatic summit finishes (mid-course) in world gran fondo cycling. GFNY Bali is the rare franchise event that transcends its brand. Strip away the GFNY timing chips and age-group classifications, and you still have one of the most visually spectacular road cycling courses on earth: a 134 km loop from a black-sand volcanic coast through UNESCO-worthy rice terraces and Hindu temple villages to the crater rim of an active volcano, then back down through monkey forests and the cultural heart of Ubud. The Kintamani ascent — 25-30 km of sustained climbing in equatorial heat to 1,500 meters — is a genuine test that combines European mountain-pass difficulty with Southeast Asian thermal punishment. The field is regionally competitive but internationally thin, and the franchise structure means the prestige caps below what this setting deserves. But no other GFNY event — and frankly, very few road cycling events anywhere — can match the combination of volcanic terrain, tropical ecology, and living spiritual culture that Bali delivers from the saddle. Yes if you want a world-class adventure cycling experience wrapped in a GFNY competitive framework. Yes if volcanic terrain, tropical conditions, and Balinese culture sound like the ride of a lifetime. Yes if you're based in the Asia-Pacific region and want a serious climbing gran fondo with professional organization. Skip if field depth matters to you — the competitive front is thin. Skip if you can't handle sustained climbing in equatorial heat. Skip if 20-30 hours of travel from the Americas is a dealbreaker.

### GFNY Philippines
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 84 mi | 4,000 ft | Subic Bay, Philippines | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-philippines/

GFNY Philippines delivers pro-level road racing in tropical paradise. Rolling paved roads departing Subic Bay Freeport Zone into the scenic Bataan province, featuring coastal views, moderate climbs, and a fast return to the finish. Long course emphasizes endurance with qualifier potential for top age-groupers. Signature challenge: The 84-mile loop through Bataan province, mirroring pro routes with scenic yet demanding paved roads.

GFNY Uppsala blends Swedish history, rolling gravel-road loops, and GFNY prestige near Stockholm.

### Gran Fondo Coimbra Region
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 100 mi | 6,500 ft | Coimbra, Portugal | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-coimbra/

UCI qualifier blending Coimbra's heritage with hilly Portuguese roads for solid sportive suffering.

### Gran Fondo Hincapie
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 83 mi | 8,100 ft | Travelers Rest, South Carolina | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-hincapie/

George Hincapie's Tour de France training roads. Skyuka Mountain at 8.7%. The Southeast's premier gran fondo with 15 years of pedigree. George Hincapie trained on these roads for 17 Tours de France, and the Gran route makes you understand why. The 83-mile course from Hotel Domestique punches north into the Blue Ridge foothills, crossing into North Carolina's Polk County for the signature climbs before looping back to the Travelers Rest finish. Three timed segments — Skyuka Mountain, Green River Cove, and the final climb to the finish — structure the suffering around sustained, gradient-heavy climbing. Between the major climbs, rolling Blue Ridge countryside and Lake Road's technical turns keep the legs honest. The autumn foliage through dense forest canopy creates postcard scenery that's easy to miss when you're redlined on Skyuka's 8.7% gradient. This is a road event on paved surfaces throughout, but the mountainous character, the altitude variation from 1,100 ft to nearly 3,000 ft, and the relentless vertical accumulation make it one of the hardest gran fondos in the Eastern United States. Signature challenge: Skyuka Mountain. Four miles at 8.7% average gradient, 1,814 feet of vertical gain, a summit at 2,967 feet, and a technical descent so steep that carbon clincher rim brakes are banned from the course. This is where the Gran Fondo Hincapie earns the right to carry a Tour de France veteran's name. George Hincapie spent 17 years as professional cycling's most trusted domestique — the rider who sacrificed personal glory so others could stand on the Tour de France podium. His retirement project is the opposite philosophy: an event designed entirely for your experience. Gran Fondo Hincapie routes through the Blue Ridge foothill roads where Hincapie actually trained for July in France, summiting Skyuka Mountain at 8.7% and navigating Green River Cove's switchbacks — the same climbs that built the engine behind nine Tour de France champions. The 83-mile Gran with 8,100 feet of climbing is legitimate suffering, not a branded fun ride. But the Hotel Domestique base, the Michelin-starred chef dinner, the post-ride festival, and the parade of cycling legends create an experience layer that most gran fondos can only aspire to. The 2024 Hurricane Helene pivot — when the event raised $750,000 for the devastated Polk County communities that had hosted the fondo for 13 years — showed that this isn't corporate cycling tourism. It's an event with a soul. Yes if riding a Tour de France domestique's personal training roads alongside cycling legends appeals to you. Yes if you want a legitimate 83-mile, 8,100-foot climbing challenge wrapped in a luxury cycling weekend. Skip if you need competitive racing depth — this is participatory, not a race. Skip if premium pricing for a branded experience isn't your thing.

### Gran Fondo Marco Pantani
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 90 mi | 7,054 ft | Cesenatico, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-marco-pantani/

Il Pirata's training roads. His family's event. Cesenatico to the Romagnol hills and back. Since 2005. Three routes of increasing ambition, all starting from the Marco Pantani monument on the Cesenatico waterfront and heading inland through the Romagnol hills. The terrain transitions from flat Adriatic coastal roads to rolling countryside to the punchy, steep-gradient hills that characterize the pre-Apennine foothills. The Lungo route ventures into the Marche border country, adding the Botticella and additional climbing to the Ciola and Montevecchio that feature on all routes. The rhythm is distinctly Romagnol — short, sharp climbs separated by rolling valleys with small villages, vineyards, and stone walls. These are not long Alpine grinds; they are the repeated punchy efforts that Pantani used to build explosive power before translating it to the high mountains. Signature challenge: The cumulative climbing across the Ciola, Botticella, and Montevecchio is honest rather than heroic — 2,150m on the Lungo route. The real challenge is emotional. These are the roads where Marco Pantani trained. The gradients he ground out on winter mornings. The hills he repeated until his legs could dance on the pedals at 90 rpm uphill. Riding them with the knowledge of what he became — and how it ended — adds a dimension that no elevation profile can capture. The Gran Fondo Marco Pantani is not the hardest ride in Italy, not the most scenic, and not the most famous. It is the most personal. Organized by the Pantani family, starting from the bronze statue of Il Pirata on the Cesenatico waterfront, following the roads he rode as a teenager and as a Tour de France champion, passing the monument at Montevecchio where his spirit lives in the hills. Marco Pantani — born in Cesenatico 1970, dead in Rimini 2004, the last man to win the Giro and the Tour in the same year — built his legendary climbing legs on these Romagnol hills before he ever touched the Galibier or the Mortirolo. This event lets you ride in his ghost. The climbs are honest, the countryside is charming, and the emotional weight is unlike anything else in amateur cycling. Yes if Pantani's story moves you and you want to ride the roads where he built his legendary climbing engine. Yes if September in Italy with warm weather and honest Romagnol hills sounds like your kind of weekend. Skip if you want pure athletic challenge — Nove Colli in the same town offers more distance and climbing. Skip if you have no connection to Pantani's legacy — without that context, this is a pleasant but mid-tier Italian sportive.

### Gran Fondo Pirineus
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 89 mi | 11,811 ft | Camprodon, Girona, Spain | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-pirineus/

Epic Pyrenean road sportive with brutal climbs and stunning valleys, SCOTT-sponsored gem for endurance cyclists.

### Gran Fondo Via del Sale
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 87 mi | 4,800 ft | Cervia, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-via-del-sale/

A well-organized early-season regional classic offering scenic sea-to-hills routing and strong community experience for intermediate-to-advanced cyclists.

### Granfondo do Porto
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 81 mi | 5,200 ft | Porto, Portugal | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-gf-do-porto/

Scenic Douro masterpiece blending savage climbs with wine country bliss. Hilly road loop through Douro Valley's terraced vineyards, blending relentless climbs with sweeping descents and river views. Signature challenge: The return via Muro do Cadão, a 3.1km beast at 10.2% that breaks the field in spectacular wine country.

### Granfondo Leiria Region – Sico Lands
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 92 mi | 6,500 ft | Pombal, Portugal | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-leiria/

UCI qualifier gem in Portugal's scenic heartland blending heritage, climbs, and coast. Rolling road gran fondo through Leiria region's pine forests, medieval castle towns, and coastal plains, blending sustained climbs with technical descents in Serra de Sicó landscapes. Signature challenge: The Serra de Sicó undulations combining 6500ft of climbing with fast, technical descents through central Portugal's karst terrain.

### UCI Gran Fondo Brasil – Pomerode
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 68 mi | 4,265 ft | Pomerode, Santa Catarina, Brazil | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-pomerode/

South America's sole UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier, combining championship-level racing with German-Brazilian cultural heritage in a professionally organized, moderately challenging 120 km loop. Looped road course combining rolling hills, winding roads, and two distinct climbing sections. Starts and finishes in Pomerode's historic center with full UCI road signage and fan-friendly setup. Shared route between Gran Fondo and Mediofondo, with Mediofondo taking a shortcut halfway. Signature challenge: The 7 km mid-course climb at 4% average gradient reaching 400 m elevation, positioned as the race's decisive section where performance gaps typically emerge. Granfondo Pomerode represents a landmark event for South American cycling, offering the continent's only UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier with genuine championship stakes. The German-heritage setting and well-organized course deliver a premium experience, though the event's relative youth (founded 2023) and limited international profile compared to European counterparts position it as an emerging rather than established prestige event. Best suited for cyclists seeking UCI qualification, South American cycling tourism, or cultural immersion in Brazil's cycling heartland. UCI Gran Fondo Brasil – Pomerode delivers authentic UCI-sanctioned gran fondo racing to South America with a clear qualification pathway to the 2026 World Championships. The 120 km course offers balanced climbing (1,239 m elevation) across two distinct sections, supported by scenic Vale Europeu Catarinense terrain and German-influenced architecture. Event organization meets UCI standards with expo, kit pickup, full signage, and athlete support. The Mediofondo option (80 km, 809 m) accommodates older age categories. Participation requires UCI license and personal insurance. As the region's only World Series event, it attracts international competitors but remains less established than European gran fondos. Ideal for cyclists prioritizing championship qualification, South American cycling culture, or European-style cycling in a developing market.

### Granfondo Vosges
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 62 mi | 5,900 ft | La Bresse, Vosges, France | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-vosges/

Mountain UCI qualifier with epic Vosges finales perfect for 62-mile climbers.

### Gruyère Cycling Tour

### Jedermann-Rennen Rund um Köln
