### L'Étape Slovenia by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 75 mi | 5,900 ft | Kranj, Slovenia | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-slovenia/

Pro-level amateur road event with Slovenian flair and Julian Alps scenery. Scenic, hilly loops through Gorenjska villages and foothills; traffic-free paved roads with pro support. Signature challenge: Race's second loop via Besnica, Jamnik, Kropa, Golnik—hilly test in Julian Alps foothills. A gem for road enthusiasts seeking Tour de France vibes in Slovenia's stunning foothills. The 150km Race delivers hilly suffering with pro polish, while Matej Mohorič's ambassadorship ties it to world-class heritage—perfect mid-tier adventure without Alpine brutality. L'Étape Slovenia stands out in the Étape series for its accessible yet challenging routes from Kranj, blending Tour branding, closed roads, and local hero appeal. Ideal for sportive riders chasing prestige without overcrowding.

### Marmotte Granfondo Sestriere
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 75 mi | 10,400 ft | Cesana Torinese, Piedmont, Italy | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/marmotte-granfondo-sestriere/

The only granfondo in the world with Colle delle Finestre's legendary unpaved summit. Giro d'Italia dirt at 2,178 meters. A focused Alpine assault centered on one defining climb. The route through the Susa and Chisone Valleys is scenic and demanding, but everyone is here for the same reason: Colle delle Finestre. The course builds through steady valley climbs and fast descents before delivering you to the base of the Finestre's 18.5 km ascent. The first 10 km on tarmac are relentless (9.2% average), but the real event begins when the road surface changes. The final 7.8 km on dirt — switchbacking through larch forests with gradients reaching 14% — is the granfondo's entire reason for existence. The descent and return climb to Sestriere at 2,035m ensures the legs never fully recover. Signature challenge: The 7.8 km of unpaved road on Colle delle Finestre. The transition from tarmac to dirt at approximately 1,700m altitude — on gradients that reach 14%, at oxygen levels measurably reduced by altitude — is the single most distinctive feature of any road granfondo in the world. You can train for the climbing. You cannot fully prepare for the psychological impact of losing your road surface when the gradient kicks hardest. The Granfondo Sestriere exists for one reason: Colle delle Finestre. And that one reason is enough. This is the only organized cycling event in the world that sends you up the 7.8 km of unpaved road that has produced some of the most dramatic Giro d'Italia stages in modern history. The event itself is young and still finding its identity after splitting from the Marmotte brand, but the mountain does not care about branding. When the tarmac ends and the gravel begins at 1,700m, you are riding the exact surface that broke champions and made legends. Everything else — the Susa Valley, the Olympic infrastructure, the finish at Sestriere — is context for that singular experience. Yes if you want to ride cycling's most famous unpaved climb in an organized event. Yes if you value Giro d'Italia heritage and want to feel what the professionals felt on that dirt. Yes if you want a high-value Italian Alpine experience without the logistics of deep-Alpine events. Skip if you need a large, prestigious international granfondo. Skip if you want a 150+ km all-day epic — at 103 km, this is more focused than sprawling.

### NXT Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 93 mi | 6,561 ft | Eijsden, Limburg, Netherlands | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/nxt-classic/

A well-established, accessible season opener featuring professional-level climbs in a scenic hilly region with strong value and community atmosphere.

### Pan-Mass Challenge
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 177 mi | 6,800 ft | Worcester to Provincetown, Massachusetts, USA (multiple routes across Massachusetts) | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/pan-mass-challenge/

6,500 riders. 177 miles. $1.125 billion raised. The bike ride that fights cancer. A two-day crossing of Massachusetts on paved roads, from the rolling hills of central Mass to the windswept tip of Cape Cod. Day 1 is the harder day — 100 miles from Worcester featuring genuine New England climbing in the first 50 miles (long, rolling ascents and fast descents), gradually flattening as the route approaches the Cape Cod Canal and the finish at Mass Maritime Academy in Bourne. The overnight at MMA is a PMC institution — thousands of riders sleeping in dorms, tents, and ship quarters, fueling for Day 2. Sunday's 77 miles from Bourne to Provincetown are rolling-to-flat Cape Cod terrain with ocean breezes, cranberry bogs, and the charming towns of the outer Cape, punctuated by short steep climbs in Truro before the emotional finish at the Provincetown Monument. Signature challenge: The emotional weight. The PMC's signature challenge is not a climb or a headwind — it is riding 177 miles while carrying the names and stories of people fighting cancer. The physical effort is real. The emotional effort is greater. The Pan-Mass Challenge is not a bike ride. It is a $78-million-a-year cancer research funding machine that uses bicycles as its engine. Since 1980, when Billy Starr convinced 36 riders to cycle from Springfield to Provincetown, the PMC has raised $1.125 billion for Dana-Farber Cancer Institute — 100% of every rider-raised dollar going directly to research, treatment, and patient care. Today, 6,500 riders cross Massachusetts on routes from 25 to 177 miles, supported by 3,500 volunteers and an organization refined over 46 years. The cycling is honest New England terrain — rolling hills, coastal roads, and a two-day format that demands real fitness. But the experience transcends sport. You ride past signs that say 'Thank you — my daughter is alive because of Dana-Farber.' You pedal alongside survivors wearing jerseys with their diagnosis dates. You finish in Provincetown knowing that your effort — every dollar you raised, every mile you rode — went directly to the fight. The PMC does not ask you to race. It asks you to matter. You do not race the PMC — you ride it with purpose. Yes if you are ready to commit to raising $2,000-$6,000+ for cancer research and want the most emotionally powerful cycling experience in America. Yes if you can handle 100+ miles of rolling New England roads in August heat. Skip if you need competitive cycling, dramatic terrain, or cannot commit to the fundraising requirement.

### Pedal IMUA
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 62 mi | 5,800 ft | Maui, Hawaii, USA | December | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/pedal-imua/

Paradise charity Gran Fondo conquering West Maui's volcanic majesty.

### Suzuka Endurance Road

Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/suzuka-endurance-road/

Iconic Japanese motorcycle endurance racing on legendary Suzuka Circuit.

### Tour de Yorkshire Sportive
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 87 mi | 6,890 ft | Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom | April | sportive
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-yorkshire-sportive/

Tour de France heritage. Yorkshire climbs. Roadside passion. On hiatus since 2019 — but if it returns, ride it. The Tour de Yorkshire Sportive changed its route each year to mirror the professional race's final stage, giving riders a rotating tour of Yorkshire's finest cycling terrain. The consistent element was the terrain character: short, steep, punishing climbs with exposed moorland between them, narrow English lanes, and descents that demanded respect in wet conditions. Buttertubs (6 km at 6% with 20% ramps), Grinton Moor, Cote de Harwood Dale, and similar Yorkshire classics rotated through. The route always included the final 50 km and finish line of the pro race, and riders could watch the professional peloton cross the same line hours later. Signature challenge: Buttertubs. Six kilometers at 6% average with pitches hitting 20%. Exposed, relentless, and iconic. The quintessential Yorkshire climb — short enough to be over quickly, steep enough to make you wish it was over sooner. The Tour de Yorkshire Sportive was born from the most electrifying moment in British cycling history: the 2014 Tour de France Grand Depart. When the Tour came to Yorkshire, 2.6 million people lined the roads. A cycling culture was ignited. The professional Tour de Yorkshire launched in 2015, and the sportive gave amateurs the chance to ride the same roads, hours before the pros.

The format was simple and powerful: ride the route of the final stage — including its iconic Yorkshire climbs — then watch the pro race from the finish line. The routes varied each year: Leeds, Scarborough, Sheffield, each bringing different Yorkshire terrain. Buttertubs, Grinton Moor, Cote de Harwood Dale — the classic climbs rotated through, keeping each edition fresh.

The event is currently on hiatus. The professional Tour de Yorkshire was cancelled from 2020 onwards (COVID, then financial difficulties with Welcome to Yorkshire), and the sportive has not returned. If it does, the combination of Tour de France heritage, Yorkshire's passionate cycling community, and the county's beautiful-brutal terrain makes it one of the UK's strongest sportive propositions. Yes if the event returns and you want to ride iconic Yorkshire climbs with the Tour de France heritage and passionate roadside support. Yes if English cycling culture and the Dales landscape appeal to you. Skip if the event remains on hiatus. Skip if you need guaranteed good weather — Yorkshire in late April is a gamble.

### Two Volcano Sprint
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 746 mi | 78,740 ft | Ercolano (Vesuvius) to Catania (Etna), Italy | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/two-volcano-sprint/

Epic self-supported odyssey linking Italy's iconic volcanoes for hardened ultra cyclists.

### Alpe d'HuZes
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 51 mi | 21,089 ft | Alpe d'Huez, Isere, France | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/alpe-dhuzes/

Twenty-one hairpins. Six ascents. EUR 100 million for cancer research. Cycling's most profound charity event. One climb. Repeated. The 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez — 13.8 km at 7.9% average, with sections exceeding 11% — climbed up to six times in a single day. The route is simple to describe and punishing to execute. Each ascent follows the same road: the steep lower hairpins through the forest, the more open middle section, and the final push into the resort village at 1,860m. Each descent follows the same road back to Bourg d'Oisans. The physical experience changes with each repetition — ascent one is exhilarating, ascent three is grim, ascent six is transcendent. The emotional experience depends entirely on why you're climbing. Signature challenge: Repetition. One ascent of Alpe d'Huez is a great cycling experience. Six ascents transform it into something else entirely — a test of physical endurance, mental fortitude, and the power of purpose. The mountain doesn't change, but you do. Alpe d'HuZes is not a cycling event that happens to raise money for charity. It is a charity event that happens to use cycling's most legendary mountain as its vehicle. The distinction matters. When 5,000 riders climb Alpe d'Huez up to six times in a single day — many wearing jerseys bearing the names of people they've lost to cancer — the physical achievement becomes secondary to the emotional one. The 21 hairpins are still the 21 hairpins, the gradients are still punishing, and six ascents produce more vertical than almost any single-day cycling event on Earth. But what elevates Alpe d'HuZes beyond any sportive or gran fondo is the why. EUR 100 million raised. The largest sporting charity event in the Netherlands. This is cycling at its most human. Yes if you want to climb cycling's most famous mountain for a cause that transcends sport. Yes if the Dutch charity community and the emotional atmosphere appeal to you as much as the physical challenge. Skip if you want a traditional sportive with varied terrain and competitive structure. Skip if the EUR 1,500 fundraising minimum is a barrier.

### Bluewater International Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 93 mi | 2,800 ft | Sarnia, Ontario, Canada | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/bluewater-gran-fondo/

Ontario's top gran fondo: scenic, supported, and community-driven perfection. Scenic paved routes along Lake Huron shores, quiet country roads, rolling hills in Arkona, and picturesque orchards, with timed segments for competitive fun. Signature challenge: Timed segments on rolling terrain allowing pacing between efforts, finishing with stunning Lake Huron views.

### Bra-Bra Fenix Internazionale

### Chase the Sun Norway
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 280 mi | 10,827 ft | Bergen to Kristiansand, Norway | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/chase-the-sun-norway/

### Copper Triangle
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 79 mi | 6,500 ft | Copper Mountain Resort, Colorado | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/copper-triangle/

3 passes. 79 miles. Never below 9,000 feet. Colorado's premier high-altitude loop. A three-act alpine loop that never lets you forget where you are. The Copper Triangle traces a triangle through the heart of the Colorado Rockies, connecting three mountain passes through three distinct valleys. You climb past industrial mining heritage on Fremont, descend into America's highest city at Leadville, ascend through WWII military history at Camp Hale, and close the loop on the car-free Vail Pass bike path. The entire route sits above 9,000 feet — there is no low-altitude recovery. The grades are moderate, the road surfaces are good, and the scenery is relentless. What makes this ride hard is not the climbing profile on paper. It's the thin air that makes every gradient feel steeper than it reads. Signature challenge: The cumulative altitude. No single pass is a leg-breaker. The grades are moderate, the distances between passes are manageable. But the Copper Triangle never lets you below 9,000 feet. By the time you're climbing Vail Pass — your third summit above 10,000 feet — the oxygen deficit has been compounding for 65 miles. The thin air is the opponent, not the gradient. The Copper Triangle is a masterclass in what altitude does to honest cycling. 79 miles and 6,500 feet of climbing sounds manageable until you register the fine print: the entire route sits above 9,000 feet, the high point reaches 11,318, and you never get a breath of thick air. Three mountain passes — Fremont, Tennessee, Vail — through three Colorado valleys, past Leadville and Camp Hale and Minturn, finishing where you started at Copper Mountain. Twenty years of raising money for the Davis Phinney Foundation. Not the longest ride. Not the most climbing. But an altitude event that quietly exposes the gap between your sea-level fitness and what the Rocky Mountains actually demand. Yes if you want to experience what genuine high-altitude cycling feels like across three mountain passes in a single day. Yes if you value a well-organized charity ride through Colorado's most historic mountain terrain. Skip if you need race competition or if riding above 10,000 feet is a medical concern.

### CRC 506 Gran Fondo Costa Rica
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 87.6 mi | 2,759 ft | Jacó, Garabito, Puntarenas Province, Costa Rica | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/crc-506-gran-fondo-costa-rica/

Strong regional UCI event with international appeal and qualification prestige. 141.4km Gran Fondo: Neutral 2km start Jacó Civic Center, south on Ruta 34 coastal highway to Quepos loop/turnaround, return to Jacó (841m elevation, flattish). Medio Fondo (72.9km): Quepos start 8AM covering return leg to Jacó finish. **Suffering zones**: Heat/humidity, headwinds on exposed coast, potential rain[1][2] Tropical paradise UCI qualifier with stunning coastal views but flattish profile and wet season risks. Perfect for speed-focused qualifiers seeking Worlds qualification in beach setting.

### GFNY Aguascalientes
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 90.0 mi | 4,957 ft | Isla San Marcos, Aguascalientes, Mexico | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-aguascalientes/

BE A PRO FOR A DAY in Mexico's cycling hotbed with views of Cerro del Muerto Starts/finishes Isla San Marcos. Rolling hills w/ 6.3% max gradient, 4 notable climbs. **Suffering Zone 1: El Ocote** - 5km early climb w/ rock climbing range vistas. Quiet roads + Cerro del Muerto views after[1] GFNY's Mexico expansion hits with a culturally rich, scenic highland challenge. El Ocote climb and thermal bath recovery make this inaugural event instantly bucket-list worthy for LatAm endurance cyclists.

### GFNY Punta del Este
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 90.7 mi | 5,423 ft | Punta del Este, Maldonado Department, Uruguay | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-punta-del-este/

Uruguay's hilly beach party punches way above its vert. Playa Mansa erupts into 22km coastal flats before El Chorro's 800m hammer splits the bunch. Pine forests and wild beaches yield to Punta Ballena's iconic sting, looping back via Piriápolis oceanway. Interbalnearia finale panoramas reward survivors amid luxury ranches and lakes—windy, wavy, wildly scenic road poetry. Signature challenge: Punta Ballena's mid-race bite atop early El Chorro damage—where groups shatter and solo quests ignite. Winds amplify the isolation, turning paradise into personal hell. GFNY Punta del Este fuses St. Tropez glamour with sneaky South American hills, turning beach bums into qualifiers. Winds howl off Playa Mansa while Punta Ballena snaps the elastic—pure pro drama in paradise. Redesigned yearly, it's the GFNY you didn't know you craved. Families lounge while you suffer; post-race feasts seal the legend. Skip if flats bore you—this undulating beast demands respect. Yes if you're chasing qualifiers in sun-soaked style or opening North American legs jet-lag free. Skip if deep fields or mega climbs define your fix—stick to Euros for that.

### L'Étape Ciudad de México by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 68.3 mi | 4,900 ft | Mexico City, Mexico | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-mexico-city/

Premier urban sportive with Tour de France pedigree. Perfect for prestige seekers who want city spectacle over mountain suffering. Starts Avenida Reforma beneath **Angel de la Independencia**, Mexico City's Champs-Élysées. Passes **Ciudad Universitaria**, **Torres de Satélite**, **World Trade Center**. Finishes near **Campo Marte** flag monument. Urban parcours with cultural landmarks as suffering zones rather than steep climbs. Tour de France branding in Reforma Avenue with Angel de la Independencia start is pure magic. Urban sportive perfection.

### Majka Gran Fondo

### Sparkassen Munsterland Giro
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 78 mi | 2,953 ft | Munster, North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/munsterland-giro/

Germany's largest amateur cycling sportive — up to 7,500 riders through the Munsterland, finishing at Munster's Schlossplatz alongside a UCI 1.Pro professional race on German Unity Day. A flat to gently rolling sportive through the Munsterland region of North Rhine-Westphalia, with three distance options designed for mass participation. The routes radiate out from a rotating start city through pastoral park landscape, Baumberge hills, and the edge of the Teutoburg Forest before converging on the iconic Schlossplatz finish in central Munster. The course changes annually as start cities rotate through Munsterland districts, but the character remains constant — wide German roads through gentle countryside with a festival finish. Signature challenge: Wind across the open Munsterland. The course may be flat, but the exposure to North German wind transforms gentle terrain into a genuine endurance test. The Munsterland offers no shelter — no hills, no forests, no buildings — just flat agricultural landscape where a 25 km/h headwind adds 30 minutes to your finishing time and strips the romance from pastoral cycling. The Sparkassen Munsterland Giro is the kind of event that makes you appreciate what German organizational efficiency can do with a flat landscape and a public holiday. Up to 7,500 riders on three distances through the Munsterland, finishing at a Baroque palace, followed by watching WorldTour sprinters contest the same finish line — all on German Unity Day. The terrain will not challenge your climbing legs. The wind might challenge your soul. But the combination of massive amateur participation, UCI professional racing, a 5,000-square-meter cycling trade fair, and Munster's deep-rooted cycling culture creates an experience that transcends the simplicity of the course. This is not a ride you do for the suffering. This is a ride you do for the spectacle, the community, and the singular pleasure of finishing at the Schlossplatz just hours before Jasper Philipsen does the same. If you want to experience Germany's cycling culture at its most concentrated — absolutely. The Munsterland Giro is the country's biggest amateur cycling event in its most cycling-obsessed city, on a national holiday, with WorldTour professionals racing the same roads. If you need mountains, vertical, or technical terrain — this is not your event. If you are visiting from outside Europe, combine it with the Velothon Berlin, a Rhine cycling trip, or Cologne cycling events to build a worthwhile German cycling tour.

### Old Mutual Wealth Double Century
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 126 mi | — | Swellendam, Western Cape, South Africa | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/old-mutual-wealth-double-century/

South Africa's premier endurance road cycling challenge combining scenic wine country beauty with demanding 202km team-based racing since 1993.

### Rad am Ring
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 93 mi | 11,024 ft | Nurburgring, Nurburg, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/rad-am-ring/

Race the Green Hell. 12,000 cyclists on the world's most famous motorsport circuit — the Nurburgring Nordschleife. A relentless, undulating closed circuit combining the legendary Nordschleife (20.8 km) with the modern Grand Prix circuit (5.1 km). No flat sections exist — you are always either climbing or descending through 84 turns per lap. The forest-enclosed Nordschleife feels otherworldly, while the GP circuit provides the F1 arena start/finish spectacle. Perfect tarmac throughout, but the elevation profile punishes anyone who starts too fast. Signature challenge: The Hohe Acht climb at 17% gradient — the roof of the Nordschleife where every lap extracts its toll. Combined with the relentless undulation of the full circuit (560m elevation per lap), there is no recovery. The Green Hell simply grinds you down. Rad am Ring does something no other cycling event can: it puts you on the Nurburgring Nordschleife. The same 20.8 km of relentless, forest-enclosed circuit where motorsport legends were forged since 1927. Where Rudi Altig won the Road World Championship in 1966. Where Jackie Stewart coined the name 'Green Hell.' Nearly 12,000 cyclists now invade this sacred motorsport ground every July, racing through 84 turns per lap, surviving the 17% Hohe Acht climb, and hitting 100 km/h on the Fuchsrohre descent. The 24h race adds a surreal dimension — riding the Nordschleife at 3 AM through fog with only a headlamp. This is not gravel, this is not a gran fondo — this is road cycling on the most storied racing circuit on Earth, and the experience is absolutely singular. If you have any connection to motorsport culture, this is a mandatory pilgrimage on two wheels. The 24h race is the crown jewel — 24 hours of closed-circuit racing through the Nordschleife, including surreal night laps through forest fog. The public races (25/75/150 km) offer the venue experience in a single day. For German and European cyclists, this is one of the continent's most unique events. For international visitors, combine with a broader European cycling trip.

### Raul Alcala Challenge Oaxaca
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 68 mi | — | Oaxaca de Juárez, Oaxaca, Mexico | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/raul-alcala-challenge-oaxaca/

Iconic Mexican road challenge through stunning Sierra Norte mountains, named for a cycling legend.

### Redlands Bicycle Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 70 mi | 5,700 ft | Redlands, San Bernardino County, California, USA | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/redlands-bicycle-classic/

40 years of where legends are born. Now ride the same roads yourself. Southern California climbing roads through citrus country and oak-studded foothills, punctuated by the iconic professional race climbs. The Gran Route combines 70 miles of Sunset Loop, Oak Glen, and Walnut climbs with a gravel sector and the downtown criterium circuit. The course rewards consistent climbing power over 5,700 ft of elevation gain in warm SoCal conditions. Signature challenge: Oak Glen — the sustained climb through apple orchards that has been the GC battleground of the Redlands Bicycle Classic for 40 years. The same road, the same gradient, the same afternoon sun. The Redlands Bicycle Classic has launched more professional cycling careers than any other American race. Since 1985, the Sunset Loop, Oak Glen, and downtown criterium have been the courses where American legends first proved themselves. The Legends' Fondo puts amateur riders on those same roads, led by Phil Gaimon, finishing at the iconic RBC finish line. This isn't about the climbing or the distance — it's about 40 years of history seeping through the asphalt. Ride Oak Glen and you're riding where the next Olympic cyclist might be racing that same weekend. Yes if you value American cycling history and want to ride iconic professional race courses. Yes if you want a well-organized SoCal weekend with professional racing to watch. Skip if you want dramatic scenery or extreme physical challenge.

### TjejVättern
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 62 mi | — | Motala, Sweden | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tjejvattern/

Scandinavia's premier women-only cycling event combining scenic beauty, genuine physical challenge, and exceptional community spirit.

### Tour de Okinawa
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 124 mi | 6,560 ft | Nago City, Okinawa, Japan | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-okinawa/

Japan's biggest citizen road race — subtropical island, shut-down roads, Asian amateur racing at its finest. Deceptively demanding. The two cross-island climbs through Yanbaru forest are the race's signature — honest 5-8% gradients through subtropical canopy that shatter groups and determine results. Between those climbs, the constant coastal undulations from sea level create cumulative fatigue that the raw elevation numbers disguise. The course follows the coastline of northern Okinawa, cutting twice across the island's spine where the mountains live. It is not a mountain course, but it is not flat either — 2,000 meters of climbing from sea level is 2,000 meters of absolute gain. Signature challenge: The accumulation. Neither climb alone is extraordinary, but the combination of subtropical humidity, constant undulation, and two honest forest climbs creates progressive fatigue that catches riders who went out too hard on the flat coastal opening. The race rewards patience and punishes aggression. Tour de Okinawa is the race that Japanese amateur cyclists build their entire season around. It is not the hardest course, not the deepest field by global standards, and not the most internationally recognized. What it is: the most professionally organized citizen road race in Asia, held on a subtropical island with fully closed roads, wrapped in Okinawan hospitality, and steeped in 35 years of racing history. The two Yanbaru climbs separate the strong from the ambitious, and the November timing makes it the perfect season capstone. For Japanese cyclists, this needs no justification — it is the season finale that defines your year. For international riders, Tour de Okinawa rewards those willing to navigate the logistics: you get a UCI-grade racing experience on a subtropical island with some of the best on-course support in amateur cycling. The two Yanbaru climbs are honest and the coastal roads are stunning. Come for the race, stay for the sashimi.

### Vélomediane Claudy Criquielion

### Velothon Berlin

Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/velothon-berlin/

Berlin's fastest, flashiest road race - pure speed through history's epicenter. Lightning-fast urban circuit showcasing Berlin's landmarks on completely closed roads, prioritizing pure speed and spectator energy over climbing challenges. Signature challenge: Sustaining 45+ kph average speeds for 99 miles on flat roads past Reichstag, East Side Gallery, and Victory Column with 13,000 competitors.

### 3RIDES Gran Fondo Südliche Weinstrasse
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 62 mi | 1,969 ft | Porte des Vins Allemands (German Wine Gate), Schweigen-Rechtenbach, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/3rides-gran-fondo/

Technically demanding UCI road qualifier in scenic Pfalz wine lands. Rolling 100 km loop: vineyards, forests, gentle hills; 15 km into France; technically challenging, car-free. Signature challenge: 15 km French border section through wine trails This UCI qualifier delivers a picturesque yet punchy ride through wine country, with the France border jaunt as a standout. Corrects user myths on distance/elevation; solid T2 road event for Euro spring training. ### Adelaide Epic Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 85 mi | 5,906 ft | Adelaide, South Australia, Australia | January | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/adelaide-epic-ride/

136 km on a UCI WorldTour stage route through the Adelaide Hills with 1,800 m of climbing, ridden on the same day as the pros. The community ride of Australia's biggest professional cycling race. A sealed-road journey from the Adelaide coast through the southern suburbs and into the Adelaide Hills, following the exact route of the UCI WorldTour Men's Stage 3. The 136 km route begins at Henley Beach at sea level and heads south and east through Adelaide's suburban fringe before the terrain tilts decisively upward into the Adelaide Hills. The character shifts dramatically across the ride: flat coastal kilometres give way to rolling suburban hills, which in turn yield to the punchy categorised climbs that define the stage. Chandlers Hill arrives first — a short, sharp shock to legs that have been spinning on flat road. Wickham Hill follows, a more sustained effort through Adelaide Hills bushland. The final act is Mount Barker Summit, arriving with less than 5 km to the finish in Nairne, when accumulated fatigue and January heat conspire to make every gradient feel twice as steep. The descents between climbs are fast and rewarding — wide, well-surfaced roads swept to professional racing standards. Signature challenge: Mount Barker Summit with less than 5 km to the finish — the final categorised climb after 130+ km of riding and 1,500+ metres of accumulated climbing in January heat. Every pedal stroke on this climb is earned. The gradient is honest, the heat is real, and the finish at Nairne is close enough to be cruel. The Adelaide Epic Ride is the rare community ride that lets you put your wheels on a genuine UCI WorldTour stage route and ride it on the same day as the professionals. The 136 km route from Henley Beach to Nairne through the Adelaide Hills delivers 1,800 metres of honest climbing — Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, and Mount Barker Summit are real climbs, not token hills — under the full force of South Australian summer heat. This is not the hardest sportive in Australia. But it is the one most directly connected to the highest level of professional cycling, and that connection is what makes it special. Yes if you want the unique experience of riding a genuine WorldTour stage route on race day, embedded in the Santos Tour Down Under festival atmosphere. Yes if you can handle South Australian summer heat and want a well-supported 136 km ride with real climbing. Skip if you need competitive racing, if you cannot tolerate extreme heat, or if you find $225 AUD steep for a community ride.

### Atlas Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 99 mi | 5,906 ft | Taroudant, Morocco | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/atlas-gran-fondo/

Morocco's premier road sportive blending epic Atlas climbs with cultural adventure.

### Cape Town Cycle Tour
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 67.7 mi | 3,937 ft | Grand Parade, Cape Town → Green Point | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cape-town-cycle-tour/

35,000 riders around the Cape Peninsula — the world's largest individually timed bike race, where the Cape Doctor wind is the real opponent. A 109km loop around one of the most beautiful peninsulas on Earth, with 35,000 individually-timed riders on fully closed roads. The course is more spectacle than suffering — the climbing is modest and the challenge comes from wind, distance, and the sheer scale of the peloton. Chapman's Peak Drive carved into a cliff 600m above the Atlantic is the jewel. The Cape Doctor wind is the wildcard that can turn a pleasant ride into a survival exercise. Signature challenge: The Cape Doctor wind. In calm years (like 2025), record times fall. In windy years, the second half of the course becomes a headwind survival exercise. In 2017, 100km/h winds forced the first-ever cancellation. Cape Town Cycle Tour is unlike anything else in cycling. 35,000 riders on fully closed roads around one of the most beautiful peninsulas on Earth. The climbing is gentle, the scenery is stunning, and the entry fee ($53) is absurdly cheap. But this is not a mountainous challenge — it's a community celebration at scale. The Cape Doctor wind is the real antagonist: it has cancelled the event, collapsed infrastructure, and turned Chapman's Peak into a wind tunnel. When conditions cooperate, it's a perfect day on a bike. Yes, if you appreciate the scale and setting over course difficulty. This is a celebration, not a sufferfest. Chapman's Peak alone is worth the trip. The UCI GFWS status in 2026 adds competitive credibility. Just know that the climbing is modest and the wind is the variable.

### Engadin Radmarathon
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 137 mi | 13,123 ft | Zernez, Engadin Valley, Graubunden, Switzerland | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/engadin-radmarathon/

Three days of Swiss Alpine precision. Five passes above 2,000m. Glaciers, national parks, and UNESCO heritage — through the Engadin, where the roads are perfect and the scenery is better. A three-act Alpine drama staged across the Engadin Valley. The Friday prologue is a sharp appetizer: 6.7 km straight up toward the Ofenpass through the Swiss National Park, legs burning from the first pedal stroke. Saturday's La Cuorta is the international chapter: through a closed tunnel into the duty-free Italian enclave of Livigno, over the Forcola di Livigno and the Bernina Pass in rapid succession — with the glaciers of Piz Bernina and Piz Palu gleaming above the descent to Pontresina. Sunday's La Svizra is the masterpiece: the Fluela Pass at 2,383m rising from Susch in 13 km of sustained climbing, a fast descent into Davos, the long Landwasser Valley approach to the Albula Pass alongside the UNESCO World Heritage Rhaetian Railway, and the final descent back into the Engadin to Zernez. The cumulative effect of three days, five passes, and 221 km at altitude is a slow-building immersion in some of the finest cycling terrain in the Alps. Signature challenge: The Albula Pass on Sunday. After two days of Alpine climbing — five passes already in the legs — the 26 km approach from the Landwasser Valley alongside the UNESCO World Heritage Rhaetian Railway is the event's emotional and physical crescendo. The climb is not the steepest, not the highest, not the most dramatic. It is the most relentless — and it comes when you have the least to give. The Engadin Radmarathon is what happens when Swiss organizational culture meets some of the most spectacular cycling terrain in the Alps. Three days, five passes above 2,000 meters, 221 km of pristine tarmac through the Engadin Valley — crossing into Italy, riding through the Swiss National Park, climbing alongside a UNESCO World Heritage railway, and staring at the Bernina glacier massif from the saddle. It is not the hardest Alpine sportive (the Otztaler and La Marmotte are harder). It is not the most famous (the Maratona and L'Etape are better known). What it is, consistently, is one of the most beautiful — and one of the best organized. The three-day stage format creates something rare in the sportive world: a journey, not just a ride. You sleep in a small Swiss village, wake up, and climb another pass. By Sunday evening, the Engadin feels like home. Yes if you want a multi-day Alpine stage experience with world-class scenery and Swiss-level organization. Yes if five passes above 2,000m through the Engadin, with cross-border adventure into Italy, is your definition of a good weekend. Skip if Swiss prices are a dealbreaker — accommodation and dining add up fast across a multi-day format. Skip if you want a competitive race environment or a field bigger than 1,500 riders.

### GFNY Bremen
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 69.6 mi | 984 ft | Bremen, Germany | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-bremen/

T2 GFNY franchise event: Fast Euro qualifier with wind character Neutralized 0-4km to airport → racing km4-18 turny roads → double flat loop (farmland/forest/false flats/500m cobbles) → timing finish km106 sprint → neutralized 6km Ochtum River cooldown to Tabakquartier[1][2][3] GFNY Bremen delivers the franchise polish on a fast, windy North German course. ### GFNY República Dominicana
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 62 mi | 3,500 ft | Punta Cana, Dominican Republic | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-dominican-republic/

A wind-dependent Caribbean challenge that tests tactical racing and mental resilience in a stunning tropical setting. A wind-dependent Caribbean cycling challenge featuring a deceptively fast outbound leg with favorable tailwind through rural tropical terrain, followed by a punishing headwind return that tests mental toughness and pacing discipline. The course transitions from coastal roads to lush hinterland and back, with the Caribbean Sea and impressive hill backdrops providing scenic contrast. Signature challenge: The wind reversal from outbound to return leg. Riders must exercise extreme restraint during the fast, favorable outbound section (km 0-67) despite the pace feeling easy and the field moving quickly. The return journey's headwind separates those who paced conservatively from those who burned matches early. GFNY Dominican Republic is a wind-defined race that rewards tactical patience and mental toughness. The outbound leg's false sense of ease—powered by favorable tailwind through beautiful rural terrain—sets up a humbling return journey. This is not a race to chase early moves; it's a race to conserve energy, read the wind, and unleash in the final 10km when others are suffering. The Caribbean setting elevates the experience beyond pure cycling metrics, and the professional race organization ensures riders feel like legitimate competitors. Best suited for cyclists who enjoy wind strategy and don't mind a challenging finish. GFNY Dominican Republic delivers a unique racing experience where wind becomes the primary opponent rather than terrain. The 149.7km long course offers genuine competitive racing with a well-organized international field, while the 93.8km medium course provides an accessible alternative. The race's greatest strength is its strategic depth—the favorable outbound leg tempts aggressive racing, but the brutal headwind return punishes poor pacing decisions. Punta Cana's resort infrastructure, direct international flights, and beautiful Caribbean scenery make this an attractive destination race. However, the relatively modest elevation gain and lack of technical terrain limit its appeal for climbers or technical specialists. The race shines for cyclists seeking a professional racing experience in an exotic location, particularly those who thrive in wind-based tactical racing. Not ideal for first-time gran fondo participants or those seeking a pure climbing challenge, but excellent for experienced riders wanting to test their racing acumen in paradise.

### UCI Gran Fondo Loutraki
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 69.6 mi | 3,281 ft | Loutraki, Isthmus of Corinth, Greece | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-loutraki/

A well-organized UCI World Series qualifier offering Mediterranean scenery and legitimate competition with direct pathway to world championships. Mediterranean Gran Fondo combining coastal and mountain terrain. The route transitions from flat seaside riding along the Saronic Gulf through inland rolling climbs in the Peloponnese interior, returning to coastal finish. Characterized by scenic beauty, moderate climbing, and well-marked professional course management. Signature challenge: The two-part inland climb transitioning from coastal flats to interior mountains, offering sustained rolling gradient (3.1% average) with maximum elevation of 300 m above sea level. Provides legitimate climbing test while remaining accessible to diverse ability levels. Gran Fondo Loutraki represents an excellent entry point into UCI Gran Fondo World Series racing with genuine competitive value. The inaugural 2025 event attracted 400+ international riders and delivered on its promise of combining Greek coastal beauty with legitimate climbing challenges. The automatic qualification pathway to the 2026 World Championships in Japan elevates its prestige beyond a typical regional event. However, the 112 km distance and moderate 966 m elevation gain position it as an accessible rather than ultra-challenging Gran Fondo, making it ideal for riders seeking international competition without extreme demands. Gran Fondo Loutraki successfully launched as Greece's first UCI Gran Fondo World Series event in November 2025, establishing itself as a quality international cycling event. The 112 km route intelligently balances accessibility with challenge, featuring flat coastal sections that welcome diverse ability levels alongside rolling inland climbs that test fitness. The automatic qualification system for top 25% finishers in each age category provides meaningful competitive stakes. Professional organization by the Municipality of Loutraki and Hellenic Cycling Federation ensures world-class standards. The location's proximity to Athens and established tourism infrastructure supports international participation. For cyclists seeking UCI-sanctioned racing with genuine world championship qualification opportunities, combined with Mediterranean scenery and strong community atmosphere, Loutraki delivers exceptional value. The event's inaugural success and integration into the prestigious UCI Gran Fondo World Series calendar positions it as a must-do qualifier for ambitious Gran Fondo racers.

### Pelotonia
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 100 mi | 2,500 ft | Columbus, Ohio, USA | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/pelotonia/

$339 million raised. 7,500 riders. 100% of every dollar to cancer research. The terrain is Ohio. The mission is everything. A point-to-point ride from downtown Columbus across Central Ohio's gently rolling farmland. The route leaves the urban grid within the first 10 miles, transitioning through suburbs and into genuinely rural countryside — corn and soybean fields, white farmhouses, quiet county roads lined with hardwood trees. The terrain is a series of short rollers, none individually significant but cumulatively honest over 100 miles in August heat. The 100-mile route culminates at Kenyon College in Gambier — a hilltop liberal arts campus that transforms into a finish line celebration. The 165-mile two-day option adds a Sunday return from Kenyon through different countryside to New Albany. Signature challenge: The heat. Not the terrain, not the distance, not the climbing — the August Ohio heat and humidity that turns an otherwise moderate century into a genuine test of preparation and resilience. Every experienced Pelotonia rider will tell you the same thing: respect the weather. Pelotonia is the rare cycling event where the suffering has a point beyond personal satisfaction. $339 million raised for cancer research — every dollar from riders going directly to the Ohio State Comprehensive Cancer Center, operational costs covered by corporate partners so nothing leaks to overhead. The riding itself is honest but unremarkable: flat-to-rolling Ohio farmland in August heat, paved roads, no technical demands. What makes Pelotonia extraordinary is the community — 7,500 riders who have raised thousands of dollars each, many riding for deeply personal reasons, united by a single goal that is bigger than any of them. The Opening Ceremony will give you chills. The finish line might make you cry. The terrain will not. Yes if you want to be part of one of the most impactful cycling communities in America. Yes if cancer has touched your life and you want to ride with meaning. Yes if you value community and mission over scenery and competition. Skip if you cannot commit to the fundraising requirement, need competitive racing, or want dramatic terrain.

### Prosecco Cycling
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 62 mi | 5,118 ft | Valdobbiadene, Veneto, Italy | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/prosecco-cycling/

100 kilometers through UNESCO vineyards, Prosecco at the aid stations, Nessun Dorma at the start line. The only gran fondo where the feed zone serves shrimp scampi and sparkling wine. A 100 km circuit through the UNESCO World Heritage Prosecco Hills, starting and finishing in Valdobbiadene. The terrain is relentlessly rolling — short, punchy climbs through terraced vineyards connected by valley-floor sections through medieval villages. The climbing is concentrated in sharp, steep pitches rather than sustained alpine passes. The Muro di Ca' del Poggio is the signature effort: 1.1 km at 12.7% average with a 19% maximum gradient, a Giro d'Italia climb compressed into a furious sprint. Between the four timed climbs, the route meanders through Combai, Follina, Tarzo, Resera, Refrontolo, San Pietro di Feletto, Conegliano, and Collalto before returning via Sernaglia della Battaglia, Col San Martino, and Guia. The rhythm is climb-descend-roll-climb, with the rolling sections offering UNESCO vineyard panoramas at every turn. The route is designed for experience first, suffering second. Signature challenge: The Muro di Ca' del Poggio — 1.1 km of 12.7% average gradient with a 19% maximum, certified by the Italian Cycling Federation and featured in the Giro d'Italia. It is the kind of climb that sorts riders into those who can hold 300+ watts for 5 minutes and those who cannot. The 19% pitches are the steepest gradient most amateur cyclists will ever ride on paved road. The reward at the top — Prosecco and Scampi at the San Pietro di Feletto ristoro — is the most Italian thing that has ever happened at a cycling aid station. Prosecco Cycling is the only gran fondo in the world where the aid stations serve sparkling wine and shrimp scampi. That sentence alone should tell you what kind of event this is. Starting with Puccini's Nessun Dorma blasting across Valdobbiadene's central piazza while waiters wave Prosecco bottles at 2,700 cyclists, the 100 km route threads through a UNESCO World Heritage vineyard landscape during the grape harvest, climbs the Giro d'Italia's Muro di Ca' del Poggio (19% maximum gradient, 1.1 km of pure punishment), and returns to a town-wide celebration of everything that makes Italian cycling culture irreplaceable. The riding is honest but not brutal. The scenery is extraordinary. The food and wine are non-negotiable. This is not an endurance test — it is Italy's end-of-season cycling party, and everyone is invited. Yes if you want the most uniquely Italian cycling experience available — wine, food, UNESCO scenery, and Puccini at the start line. Yes if EUR58 for a ride through a World Heritage landscape with Prosecco at the aid stations sounds like the best deal in European cycling. Yes if the Muro di Ca' del Poggio (19% max, Giro d'Italia climb) is on your bucket list. Skip if you need 150+ km and 3,000+ meters of climbing — this is 100 km and 1,560m, deliberately approachable. Skip if you want a pure competitive environment — all bike types are welcome and the atmosphere is celebratory.

### Race Around Austria
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 348 mi | 21,300 ft | St. Georgen im Attergau, Austria | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-around-austria/

Austria's emotional ultra gateway via 560km Upper Austria border grind.

### Race Around Poland
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 2237 mi | 108,610 ft | Poland (circumnavigation) | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-around-poland/

An elite ultracycling championship offering multiple distance formats around Poland's circumference, combining extreme endurance with organized support infrastructure.

### Sovev Jerusalem
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 31 mi | 4,921 ft | Jerusalem, Israel | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/sovev-jerusalem/

Jerusalem's iconic mass cycling celebration blending athletic challenge with historic wonders.

### Tour de Brisbane
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 68.3 mi | 4,068 ft | Brisbane, Queensland, Australia | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-brisbane/

Innovative urban gran fondo with excellent logistics but modest climbing and prestige. Tour de Brisbane transforms urban infrastructure into a cycling playground, starting at City Botanic Gardens and weaving through Brisbane's heart on fully closed roads. The course loops through the city center, crosses the Brisbane River via major bridges, navigates through tunnels and expressways, and features five technical turns requiring constant attention. Mount Coot-tha provides the only significant climbing challenge, while the remainder emphasizes urban navigation, tactical positioning, and the unique experience of riding through normally car-filled streets. The finish returns to South Bank Cultural Precinct, completing a comprehensive tour of Brisbane's cycling-friendly infrastructure. Signature challenge: Mount Coot-tha's 2km climb at 9% gradient serves as the race's defining moment, arriving at 67km to determine final positioning. While modest by gran fondo standards, the climb's placement late in the event and its role as the course's only significant elevation gain make it tactically crucial. The summit's panoramic Brisbane views reward the effort before the final descent to South Bank finish. Tour de Brisbane represents a genuinely innovative approach to gran fondo racing—taking the closed-road concept into a vibrant city rather than rural mountains. The event delivers on its promise of fully closed urban infrastructure, excellent logistics, and strong community engagement. However, it's fundamentally a different animal than traditional gran fondos: flatter, shorter, and more about urban cycling experience than climbing or distance. For Australian cyclists or those seeking a unique international cycling destination, it's compelling. For riders chasing elevation, prestige, or classic gran fondo suffering, look elsewhere. Yes if you value unique urban cycling experiences, seek UCI Worlds qualification, or want to explore Brisbane's cycling culture. Skip if you're chasing serious elevation, classic gran fondo suffering, or established prestige—this isn't that event.

### Tour Down Under Community Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 93 mi | 4,921 ft | Adelaide, South Australia | January | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-down-under-community-ride/

Fun, supported ride on pro WorldTour roads with Willunga Hill climax amid Adelaide's cycling festival.

### Tour of Pembrokeshire
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 105 mi | 10,000 ft | St Davids, Pembrokeshire, Wales | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-of-pembrokeshire/

Iconic Welsh sportive with breathtaking scenery and brutal climbs that sells out annually.

### Around the Bay in a Day
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 137 mi | 2,812 ft | Melbourne, Victoria, Australia | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/around-the-bay-in-a-day/

210 km around Port Phillip Bay with 10,000 riders and a ferry. Australia's biggest one-day cycling event, and a Melbourne institution since 1993. A flat, coastal circumnavigation of Port Phillip Bay on sealed roads and paths, punctuated by a 40-minute ferry crossing between Queenscliff and Sorrento. The Classic route runs anti-clockwise: west from Albert Park over the West Gate Bridge, through Werribee and Little River to Geelong, along the Bellarine Peninsula to Queenscliff, across the water by ferry to Sorrento, then back along the Mornington Peninsula and Beach Road to the finish at Albert Park. The terrain is relentlessly flat — the challenge is distance, wind, and endurance rather than gradient. The scenery shifts from urban Melbourne to industrial western suburbs, open farmland, Geelong's waterfront, the coastal Bellarine Peninsula, and finally the iconic Beach Road run home. Signature challenge: The sheer distance — 210 km of flat coastal riding with no hills to break the monotony and no shelter from the wind. The western shore between Werribee and Geelong is where Around the Bay reveals itself: in a headwind, this section is a war of attrition. The ferry provides a 40-minute reprieve, but you still have 80 km to ride on the other side. Around the Bay is not the hardest ride in Australia. It is not the hilliest, the most technical, or the most remote. It is the biggest, the most inclusive, and the most culturally embedded cycling event on the continent. Ten thousand riders circumnavigating Port Phillip Bay on a single Sunday in October, crossing the water by ferry, finishing on Beach Road as the afternoon light hits the bay — this is Australian cycling at its most democratic. The flat terrain means the challenge is pure endurance. The 210 km distance means it is not trivial. The community means you never ride alone. Yes if you want to experience Australia's largest cycling event in one of the world's great cities. Yes if you value community over competition and want a well-supported 210 km endurance challenge on flat terrain. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or a competitive racing environment.

### Belgrade Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 75 mi | 5,906 ft | Belgrade, Serbia | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/belgrade-gran-fondo/

Belgrade's premier gran fondo blending city vibes, mountain challenge, and Balkan hospitality.

### Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 77 mi | 3,796 ft | Geelong, Victoria, Australia | January | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cadel-evans-great-ocean-road-ride/

Cadel Evans' legacy. The Great Ocean Road. Challambra Crescent at 10%. Ride the pro course the same weekend. A coastal sportive with a sharp sting. The Great Ocean Road sections provide the visual spectacle — crashing surf, dramatic coastal cliffs, wide Pacific views — while the Surf Coast hinterland delivers the physical challenge through relentless short rollers. The route mimics the professional race course, with Challambra Crescent as the decisive climb. It's not a mountain sportive, but the accumulated climbing, the heat, and the coastal wind make 153 km harder than the distance suggests. Signature challenge: Challambra Crescent. At 1.5 km, it's over before it starts — but 10%+ gradients on tired legs, in the heat, on the same tarmac that WorldTour professionals contest, make it the most meaningful 1.5 km of the day. The Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride trades on two things: a legendary name and a legendary road. It delivers on both. Riding the same course that WorldTour professionals race the same weekend — attacking Challambra Crescent where races are decided — gives this sportive a connection to elite cycling that most events can only dream of. The Great Ocean Road provides the visual payoff, and the Geelong-Surf Coast region's cycling culture creates a genuine festival atmosphere. The ride itself is moderate by gran fondo standards — 153 km with 1,600m of climbing won't break experienced riders — but the experience transcends the physical challenge. Yes if riding the same roads as WorldTour professionals appeals to you. Yes if the Great Ocean Road and the Cadel Evans connection are meaningful to your cycling fandom. Skip if you need a mountain challenge — this is a coastal sportive with moderate climbing. Skip if Australian summer heat is a dealbreaker.

GFNY's Andean gateway: accessible distance, scenic climbs, pro experience. Two laps through Ullum Valley's plains and hills, climbing to Punta Negra dam via spectacular Interlagos route, finishing in San Juan city center. Transitions from flatlands to Andean foothills on smooth pavement. Signature challenge: Double lap structure in Ullum Valley culminating at Punta Negra dam - power endurance meets Andean backdrop.

### Gran Fondo Florida
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 100 mi | 3,248 ft | Dade City, Florida, USA | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-florida/

Recommended for intermediate to advanced road cyclists seeking an organized, community-focused gran fondo with national series credentials. Best suited for early-season training and age-group competition. Not recommended for riders seeking extreme terrain, elite-level racing, or exotic destinations. Gran Fondo Florida is a well-executed, accessible gran fondo that balances competitive timed segments with a welcoming festival atmosphere. The rolling Central Florida terrain and quiet country roads offer a pleasant early-season ride without extreme technical or elevation demands. Ideal for riders seeking national series points and a supportive community event rather than a hardcore racing experience. The 100-mile distance and 3,248 feet of elevation are substantial but not punishing. March weather is excellent. Primary drawbacks: field size unknown, exact distance ambiguous, and prestige limited to regional/national series level rather than international recognition.

### UCI Gran Fondo Greece Loutraki

### Gran Fondo San Diego
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 101 mi | 6,600 ft | San Diego, California, USA | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-san-diego/

A well-organized, community-focused century ride through San Diego's scenic backcountry with excellent support infrastructure and celebratory atmosphere. The Gran Fondo San Diego features the classic 'Great Western Loop' through East County's scenic backcountry, combining rolling terrain with strategic climbing challenges. The route showcases San Diego's diverse athletic landscape and natural beauty, transitioning from urban downtown San Diego through suburban areas into the rugged backcountry before returning to the waterfront finish. Signature challenge: The timed Japatul Road climb at mile 36, which determines KOM/QOM winners across eight age categories and represents the event's primary competitive climbing test.

### Granfondo Belgium
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 85.7 mi | 6,890 ft | Les Lacs de l'Eau d'Heure, Hainaut, Belgium | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-belgium/

Car-free Ardennes qualifier punches way above rookie weight. Ardennes microcosm: 33km opener splits the pack on 3km@3% (8%max) before 4x24km laps south to Couvin. Lakeside calm yields to valley crushers. Paved, rolling brutality tests pacing over raw distance. Signature challenge: Fourfold Petit Poggio laps forge unbreakable engines amid repeat suffering. Cumulative 2100m demands fueling mastery. Car-free flow lets pain purify. Granfondo Belgium 2.0 torches its turbulent debut with car-free roads and selective savagery around Eau d'Heure's emerald basins. 138km of Ardennes pain qualifies elites while capping chaos at 2k riders. UCI prestige meets Belgian grit—spring sharpeners, this is your qualifier ticket. Laps grind souls but scenery soothes; post-2025 glow makes it unmissable. Yes if chasing UCI worlds quals with Ardennes appetite and rainproof grit. Skip if spring slop or lap monotony kills your vibe—wait for field maturity.

### Granfondo Ecuador
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 62 mi | 2,500 ft | Salinas, Santa Elena, Ecuador | November | gran_fondo

### Granfondo Pinarello — La Pina
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 98 mi | 8,200 ft | Treviso, Veneto, Italy | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-pinarello/

Pinarello's own gran fondo through the Prosecco Hills and over the Muro di Ca' del Poggio. The brand ride done right. Rolling Veneto pre-Alpine terrain punctuated by short, savage wall climbs. The route leaves Treviso and winds through the Prosecco Hills — UNESCO-protected vineyard country — before hitting the Muro di Ca' del Poggio, a Giro d'Italia wall that ramps to 18% and leaves you wondering why you didn't fit a 34-tooth cassette. The Montello climb adds a longer, forested ascent. Between the signature climbs, the terrain is rolling and fast on good Italian tarmac. Signature challenge: The Muro di Ca' del Poggio — a 1.2 km Giro d'Italia wall with ramps to 18%. Short, savage, and impossible to pace if you don't know it's coming. Granfondo Pinarello is a brand ride, and it doesn't pretend otherwise. You're riding through the Veneto countryside that shaped one of cycling's most storied manufacturers — the same roads where Pinarello has tested frames for five decades. The Muro di Ca' del Poggio is a genuine Giro landmark: a short, vicious wall that separates riders who brought the right gearing from those who didn't. The Prosecco Hills are UNESCO-lovely. The organization reflects Pinarello's brand standards. What it isn't: a monument, a classic, or an event that transcends its corporate origins. It's a very good Italian gran fondo hosted by a very famous bike company. Yes if you appreciate cycling heritage and want to ride roads shaped by one of the sport's great manufacturers. Yes if you want a well-organized Italian gran fondo with a Giro landmark climb. Skip if you want alpine epicness or an event with its own competitive identity beyond the brand.

### GranFondo Klasika San Sebastián
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 106 mi | 9,843 ft | San Sebastián (Donostia), Basque Country, Spain | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-san-sebastian/

The amateur edition of a WorldTour classic. Same roads, same Jaizkibel, same Bay of Biscay. One day earlier. Relentless Basque terrain — constant climbing through green coastal headlands and inland valleys. The route leaves San Sebastián and winds through the Basque interior before hitting the coast and the signature ascent of Jaizkibel, an exposed headland climb overlooking the Bay of Biscay. The roads are narrow and winding. The terrain never flattens for long — classic Basque geography of short, sharp climbs connected by brief valleys. 3,000m of climbing across 170 km means you're always going up or down. Signature challenge: Jaizkibel — the exposed Atlantic headland climb that defines the Clásica San Sebastián. Wind, views, and history on a 456m coastal ascent. GranFondo San Sebastián is the rare amateur event that inherits genuine pro racing DNA. You ride the same Basque roads — including Jaizkibel's exposed Atlantic headland — that the WorldTour peloton will race the next day. That's not marketing; that's the actual route. San Sebastián as a host city is extraordinary: pintxos bars, La Concha beach, old-town atmosphere. The 170 km route with 3,000m of climbing is a proper day's work — relentless Basque rollers that never let you rest. What keeps this from the top tier is independence: strip away the pro race connection, and you have a good Basque gran fondo. But you don't need to strip it away — the connection is the point. Yes if you want to ride a WorldTour route and experience Basque cycling culture in one of Europe's best cities. Yes if you want a genuinely demanding gran fondo (170 km / 3,000m). Skip if you need sunshine guarantees or want an event with its own independent identity.
