Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-siete-lagos/

Seven lakes, one unforgettable day in Patagonia.

### Hong Kong Cyclothon
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 31 mi | 1,200 ft | Hong Kong, China | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/hong-kong-cyclothon/

Urban bridge-blasting sportive blending Hong Kong's skyline with mass-ride energy.

### Le Race
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 62.1 mi | 6,070 ft | Christchurch to Akaroa, Canterbury, New Zealand | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/le-race/

New Zealand's one-day road classic. 100 km across an extinct volcano. Olympic medalists in the Hall of Fame. Six hundred riders. One French harbour town. A volcanic crossing, not a circuit. Le Race is a 100 km point-to-point race that traverses Banks Peninsula — the eroded remnant of two extinct shield volcanoes — from Christchurch to Akaroa. The course climbs from sea level to the Port Hills ridgeline, traverses exposed summit roads with 360-degree panoramas, descends to the Little River valley, climbs back up to the Hilltop crater rim, and plunges down to the French harbour village of Akaroa at sea level. The terrain is relentless: you are climbing, descending, or fighting wind on an exposed ridge for virtually the entire 100 km. There is almost no flat road. The course rewards climbers, punishes poor descenders, and exposes anyone who has not prepared for crosswinds on volcanic ridgelines. Signature challenge: The Hilltop climb is the centrepiece, but Le Race's true challenge is the accumulation: Dyers Pass, Summit Road crosswinds, Gebbies Pass descent, the Hilltop grind, more Summit Road, and the steep Long Bay descent — all compressed into 100 km with nearly 1,900 metres of climbing. There is no recovery. There is no flat road. The course is designed by volcanic geology, not by committee, and it does not care about your legs. Michael Vink's course record of 2:37 means averaging nearly 40 km/h with 1,900m of climbing — a mark that shows how brutally honest this course is. Le Race is New Zealand's one-day road cycling classic — 100 kilometres from Cathedral Square in earthquake-rebuilt Christchurch to the French harbour village of Akaroa, across the crater rim of an extinct volcanic complex that juts into the Pacific Ocean. The route climbs Dyers Pass out of the city, traverses the exposed Summit Road with panoramic views of Lyttelton Harbour and the Southern Alps, descends to Little River, then grinds back up to the Hilltop — 30-50 minutes of sustained climbing to the Banks Peninsula crater rim — before plunging down to the French-flavoured finish. The Hall of Fame includes Olympic medalists, world champions, and Commonwealth Games winners. Nate Pringle's 2024 victory — cramping on the final climb, squeezing out one last attack to hold off the chasing bunch — is the kind of desperate, decisive racing this course produces. Six hundred riders line up each March for New Zealand's most prestigious day on a road bike. Yes if you want New Zealand's most prestigious one-day road cycling experience with genuine competitive racing on a world-class course. Yes if you are drawn to volcanic landscapes, dramatic coastal ridgelines, and a finish in the most charming village in New Zealand. Skip if you need a large-field mass-participation atmosphere — this is 600 riders, not 6,000. Skip if the 24+ hour journey to Christchurch from the Northern Hemisphere is a dealbreaker.

### L'Etape Brasil by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 66 mi | 7,546 ft | Campos do Jordao, Sao Paulo State, Brazil (flagship stage; series also visits Cunha and Rio de Janeiro) | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-brazil/

Latin America's biggest amateur cycling event: ASO-organized, closed-road mountain racing through Brazil's Serra da Mantiqueira with Tour de France classification jerseys and 3,500 riders. Mountain road course through the Serra da Mantiqueira -- Brazil's 'Swiss Alps.' The 107 km long course packs 2,300m of climbing into a compact route that winds through pine forests, mountain ridges, and the scenic countryside surrounding Campos do Jordao. The terrain is relentlessly hilly: there is very little flat riding between the climbs. Gradients alternate between sustained 5-7% drags and punchy 10%+ ramps. The course starts and finishes in the Capivari district, creating a natural amphitheater for the start/finish spectacle. Signature challenge: The accumulated climbing density: 2,300m of elevation gain packed into 107 km means you are climbing for approximately 60% of the race. There is no extended flat recovery between climbs. Every descent transitions directly into the next ascent. The compact, relentless nature of the climbing separates this from longer, flatter gran fondos. L'Etape Brasil brings ASO's Tour de France franchise to South America with impressive execution -- 107 km of closed-road mountain climbing through the Serra da Mantiqueira, classification jerseys, Shimano support, and 3,500+ riders chasing yellow in the 'Brazilian Switzerland.' Now in its second decade, this is the largest amateur cycling event in Latin America, and it runs with the professional-grade infrastructure you expect from the Tour de France brand. Campos do Jordao delivers 2,300m of genuine climbing in a charming mountain resort town where dawn starts at 5C give way to warm afternoon celebrations. The Brazilian cycling community brings infectious energy that makes the event experience genuinely special. The limitation is scale and context: this is a franchise event, not the original, and the 107 km course is shorter than the French edition's 170 km. The competitive field is strong domestically but thin internationally. What it does brilliantly is bring world-class cycling event production to a growing market that deserves it. Yes if you want Tour de France atmosphere at a fraction of European pricing. Yes if you are based in South America and want the most professionally organized sportive on the continent. Yes if 2,300m of mountain climbing on closed roads through stunning scenery sounds like your kind of weekend. Skip if you want the prestige of the French original -- this is a franchise, not the main event. Skip if you need deep international competition -- the field is overwhelmingly domestic.

### L'Etape Italy Piemonte by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 31 mi | 2,608 ft | Entracque, Cuneo, Piemonte, Italy | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-italy-piemonte/

A charming, inclusive Tour de France experience that sacrifices competitive challenge for community and accessibility. L'Etape Piemonte is a scenic cycle-touring loop designed for recreational enjoyment rather than competitive racing. The route winds through Valle Gesso's varied alpine landscapes—forests, open valleys, and historic settlements—with paved provincial roads and steady, manageable gradients. The course prioritizes visual and cultural immersion over physical suffering, passing through charming villages like Valdieri, Borgo San Dalmazzo, and Roccavione. The 795 meters of climbing is distributed evenly across the 50 km, creating a rhythm of gentle ascent and descent rather than dramatic Alpine suffering. This is deliberately beginner-friendly terrain that welcomes e-bikes and families while remaining scenically rewarding. Signature challenge: The signature challenge is not a single dramatic climb but rather the sustained, steady climbing across the entire 50 km loop. With 795 meters distributed evenly, riders face consistent 5-7% gradients rather than a single punishing ascent. The real challenge is maintaining pace and enjoying the scenery simultaneously—a mental and physical balance rather than a pure suffering test. L'Etape Italy Piemonte is a masterclass in inclusive event design—it brings Tour de France atmosphere to recreational cyclists without pretense or gatekeeping. The 31-mile loop through Valle Gesso is genuinely scenic, the €25 entry fee is absurdly fair, and the community focus is refreshing in a cycling world obsessed with racing credentials. However, serious cyclists will find it underwhelming: the distance is short, the climbing is moderate, and the terrain is beginner-friendly. This is deliberately not a granfondo for racers; it's a celebration of cycling culture for families, e-bike users, and casual touring enthusiasts. If you're seeking a competitive test or serious Alpine suffering, look elsewhere. If you want to experience Tour de France magic without the suffering, this inaugural 2026 edition delivers. Yes if you're a recreational cyclist, family, or e-bike user seeking Tour de France atmosphere without suffering. Skip if you're a serious racer wanting Alpine challenge—the 31 miles and 2,608 feet of climbing won't test you.

### Levi's GranFondo
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 103 mi | 8,500 ft | Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California, USA | October | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/levis-granfondo/

Levi Leipheimer's backyard roads. King Ridge through the redwoods. 103 miles of Sonoma County's finest. A greatest-hits reel of Sonoma County cycling compressed into 103 miles. The route oscillates between three distinct landscapes — sun-drenched wine country, dark redwood forest canyons, and exposed Pacific Coast ridgeline — with enough climbing to keep the legs honest throughout. The climbing is punchy and repeated rather than sustained Alpine grinding. King Ridge is the centerpiece: a 4-mile effort through cathedral redwoods that defines the entire ride. Signature challenge: King Ridge through the coastal redwoods. The climb itself is honest — 4+ miles at 5-9% — but the setting elevates it beyond mere physical effort. The transition from dark forest to sunlit Pacific ridgeline is one of American cycling's great moments. Levi's GranFondo is the best gran fondo in the western US, and it's not particularly close. The King Ridge climb through coastal redwoods is the reason — a 4-mile sustained effort through cathedral groves that opens onto Pacific Coast views, giving you the kind of ride-defining moment that most US sportives simply cannot manufacture. The rest of the route is a greatest-hits tour of Sonoma County: vineyard-lined roads, rolling wine country, and enough climbing to make 103 miles feel significantly harder than the distance suggests. It lacks the Alpine gravitas of European events, but what it delivers — variety, scenery, and a well-run experience — is genuinely excellent. Yes if you want Northern California's premier cycling experience with world-class scenery and variety. Yes if King Ridge through the redwoods and Pacific Coast views sound like your kind of riding. Skip if you need Alpine-scale climbing or international competitive prestige. Skip if Sonoma County wine country pricing doesn't fit your budget.

### London to Brighton Bike Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 54 mi | 2,500 ft | London to Brighton, England, UK | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/london-to-brighton/

54 miles from London to the sea. The ride that defined mass-participation charity cycling. Ditchling Beacon is the final exam. A 54-mile journey from the heart of London to the English seaside, crossing four counties and climbing over the South Downs. The route begins in the urban bustle of Clapham Common, transitions through South London's suburbs, and quickly enters the green countryside of Surrey. Rolling country lanes through the Weald of Sussex provide the middle miles — undulating terrain through small villages, past farms, and along hedgerow-lined roads. The final 10 miles deliver the ride's dramatic crescendo: the approach to Ditchling Beacon across open downland, the brutal 1-mile climb to the summit of the South Downs, the breathtaking view of the sea from the top, and the fast, euphoric descent into Brighton to finish on Madeira Drive along the seafront. Signature challenge: Ditchling Beacon — 1 mile at 9% average with a 16% maximum gradient. The most famous climb in southern England, tackled by 15,000+ riders every June. Spectators cheering, riders walking, and the view of the sea from the top. This single mile defines the entire ride. The London to Brighton Bike Ride did not invent cycling from a capital city to the coast. But it did more than any other event to establish mass-participation charity cycling as a concept that millions of people now take for granted. Since 1976, over 650,000 riders have left Clapham Common on a Sunday morning in June, rolled through the green lanes of Surrey and Sussex, dragged themselves up Ditchling Beacon, and descended in triumph to Brighton seafront. Fifty million pounds raised for the British Heart Foundation. One climb that has become the most famous in southern England. And a finish on the seafront that feels like every good thing that cycling can deliver — exhaustion, achievement, community, and the sea. This is not a race — it is a rite of passage. Yes if you want to experience the most iconic one-day charity cycling event in Europe. Yes if Ditchling Beacon is on your bucket list. Yes if you believe that 15,000 people sharing a road and a purpose is what cycling should feel like. Skip if you need timing chips, competition, or serious distance. The 54 miles will not test a strong cyclist's fitness — but the experience will stay with you regardless.

### Sudety Tour
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 93 mi | 8,200 ft | Broumov, Czechia | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/sudety-tour/

A technically demanding, culturally rich cross-border gran fondo with authentic climbing challenges, hampered by limited logistical infrastructure and uncertain field depth. A challenging, undulating cross-border gran fondo through the Sudetes mountains. The route alternates between forest climbs, cobblestone sections, and rolling terrain, with constant elevation changes throughout. The grandfondo features two major climbs and incorporates both Czechia and Poland, creating a unique multinational cycling experience. Signature challenge: The combination of the 10 km Polish forest ascent and the brutal 1 km cobblestone climb at Vysoka Srbska, separated by rolling terrain that prevents full recovery. The mediofondo shortcuts the eastern loops but retains both signature climbs. Sudety Tour represents a compelling resurrection of a historic cycling event, now elevated to UCI Gran Fondo World Series status. The cross-border Czechia-Poland routing and two signature climbs—particularly the cobblestone Vysoka Srbska—create a genuinely challenging experience. However, the event's recent revival and limited historical track record introduce uncertainty around organization quality and field depth. For cyclists seeking a technical, elevation-rich challenge in an underexplored region with cultural attractions, this delivers. Logistics and accessibility remain the primary friction points. Sudety Tour merits consideration for experienced gran fondo cyclists seeking elevation, technical terrain, and cultural immersion. The 152 km granfondo's 2,086 m elevation gain and signature climbs—the 10 km Polish forest ascent and 1 km Czech cobblestone section—deliver genuine suffering. The cross-border routing through the Sudetes adds adventure and novelty. However, the event's 2023 revival and 2026 UCI integration mean limited historical data on execution quality, participant experience, and field competitiveness. Logistical challenges (remote location, 180 km from Prague airport, sparse accommodation details) and absent pricing information create friction. Best suited for self-sufficient cyclists with flexible schedules and interest in Eastern European cycling culture.

### Tour de l'Île de Montréal
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 31 mi | 500 ft | Montreal, Quebec, Canada | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/tour-de-lile-de-montreal/

25,000 riders circle the island of Montreal on car-free streets. A former Guinness World Record holder, now in its 41st year. The Tour de l'Île de Montréal is not about the cycling. It is about 25,000 people on bicycles claiming an entire island for a day. Montreal — a city that lives and breathes cycling culture — shuts down its streets and hands them to riders of every age, every ability, every bicycle type imaginable. The ride is flat, short, and easy. The experience is singular.

You will see Montreal the way it was meant to be seen: from a bicycle, in late spring, with the St. Lawrence glittering beside you and 25,000 strangers sharing the same improbable joy. The route circumnavigates the island at river level, threading through neighborhoods that range from gentrified to gritty, from leafy suburban streets to industrial canal banks. Children ride alongside retirees. Cargo bikes alongside carbon road machines. The atmosphere is festive and communal — more block party than bike ride.

This event once held the Guinness World Record as the largest gathering of cyclists on Earth, with 45,000 riders sustained for six consecutive years in the 1990s. It helped transform Montreal into North America's most bike-friendly city. At $30-36 USD entry, it is one of the best-value cycling events anywhere. Do this ride for the experience of Montreal, not the experience of cycling. Come for the ride, stay for the poutine. You do not race the Tour de l'Île — you ride it. Yes if you want to experience Montreal from a bicycle on car-free streets with 25,000 other cyclists. Yes if you want a family-friendly, affordable, superbly organized mass-participation event. Yes if you want to visit one of the world's great cycling cities during its most beautiful season. Skip if you need athletic challenge, competitive racing, elevation, or technical terrain.

### TransAtlanticWay
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 1553 mi | 96,128 ft | Cork to Derry, Ireland | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/transatlanticway/

An epic self-supported road cycling odyssey along Ireland's most dramatic coastline, combining extreme distance and elevation with notable scenery and personal challenge.

### Vatternrundan
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 196 mi | 4,593 ft | Motala, Östergötland, Sweden | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/vatternrundan/

315 km around Lake Vättern through the Swedish midsummer night. 22,000 riders. No results. No rankings. Pure cycling. Vätternrundan is not a race. It has never been a race. Since 1966, when a sports medicine physician and a bike shop owner sent 334 riders around Lake Vättern to study what happens to the human body over 315 km, it has been a personal challenge wrapped in a Nordic ritual. There are no results. No rankings. No prizes. There is only the lake, the midsummer night, and the 22,000 other riders sharing the road.

The sun barely sets — the sky glows amber and indigo through the night hours, never reaching true darkness this close to the solstice. You ride through sleeping villages, past midsummer bonfires, past fields of barley lit by twilight. At midnight, you eat hot soup in a Swedish countryside service station with strangers who will become friends by dawn. At 3:30 AM, the sun rises over the eastern shore and you ride the final 80 km home to Motala with the lake on your left and 230 km in your legs.

It is 315 km of gently rolling road around a lake. And it is one of the most extraordinary experiences in cycling. Yes if you want an ultra-distance experience unlike anything else in cycling — the midsummer night is genuinely magical. Yes if you value community and shared adventure over competition. Yes if you are pursuing the Swedish Classic Circuit. Skip if you want to race — there are no results. Skip if you need dramatic terrain — this is flat Swedish farmland around a lake. Skip if remote Scandinavian logistics are a dealbreaker.

### Best Buddies Challenge: Miami
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 100 mi | 500 ft | Miami, Florida, USA | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/best-buddies-challenge-miami/

Luxury charity peloton ride with pros through Miami's icons.

### Eroica California
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 110 mi | 7,500 ft | Cambria, Central Coast, California, USA | September | sportive
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/eroica-california/

A challenging Central Coast gravel adventure with stunning ocean and wine country views, though event continuity and logistics require verification

### GFNY Bahamas
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 60.9 mi | 1,391 ft | West Bay Street near Goodman's Bay Park, Nassau, New Providence Island, The Bahamas | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-bahamas/

Flat-fast paradise ride in Nassau's turquoise playground. Flat and fast loops through western New Providence with Caribbean beach views; two loops for long route. Signature challenge: Wind on flat coastal roads testing endurance. GFNY Bahamas delivers a dreamy Caribbean escape with flat speed and stunning views, perfect for a fun GFNY entry without gravel grind—pure vacation vibes on two wheels. Inaugural success with world champs signals promise; ideal for scenic endurance without suffering.

### Gran Fondo Eilat
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 85 mi | 4,593 ft | Eilat, Negev Desert, Israel | December | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-eilat/

Rare winter desert gran fondo with pro-level organization and stunning Negev climbs.

### De Rosa Granfondo Firenze
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 83 mi | 6,562 ft | Florence, Tuscany, Italy | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-firenze/

Over the Ponte Vecchio and into the Tuscan hills — Florence turns its Renaissance streets into a starting grid and the Mugello into your proving ground. There are gran fondos with bigger climbs. There are gran fondos with bigger fields. There are gran fondos with more prestige and deeper history. But there is no gran fondo that starts where this one starts. You line up at Piazzale Michelangelo with the entire Florence skyline below you — Brunelleschi's Dome, the Palazzo Vecchio, the Arno catching the morning light — and then you ride down into it. You cross the Ponte Vecchio on a bicycle. Let that sink in. The most photographed bridge in Italy, a medieval jewelers' row that has spanned the Arno since 1345, and you are riding across it in lycra with a race number pinned to your jersey. After the city releases you, the Tuscan hills take over: the climb to Fiesole through cypress groves, the Mugello's rolling countryside dotted with olive groves and stone farmhouses, the feed stations where the volunteers pour you wine alongside the energy drink.

The De Rosa Granfondo Firenze is not Italy's hardest ride or its most famous. It does not have the Muro di Sormano's 27% gradient or the Maratona's 4,000m of Dolomite climbing. What it has is Florence — a city that has defined Western civilization's relationship with beauty for six centuries — as both its starting line and its backdrop. The Via Salviati finish, where Rui Costa won the 2013 World Championship road race on a 20% gradient wall, provides the competitive punch. The Mugello countryside provides the Tuscan pastoral. And the city itself provides something no other gran fondo can offer: the feeling that you are not just racing through scenery, but through history. Pack your climbing legs and your appetite. You will need both. Yes if the idea of racing across the Ponte Vecchio and starting from Piazzale Michelangelo makes your heart beat faster. Yes if you want a challenging but accessible Italian gran fondo wrapped in world-class cultural scenery. Yes if you value the total experience — city, countryside, food, history — over pure climbing brutality or maximum competitive depth. Skip if you want an extreme mountain challenge — look to the Maratona dles Dolomites or La Marmotte instead. Skip if you need a massive, festival-atmosphere field — the Nove Colli or Granfondo Campagnolo deliver that. Skip if you prefer gravel or off-road — the GF Strade Bianche is the Tuscan alternative for dirt road lovers.

### Gran Fondo Guadeloupe
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 78.3 mi | 9,462 ft | Capesterre-Belle-Eau, Guadeloupe | December | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gran-fondo-guadeloupe/

A demanding tropical mountain road race that combines serious climbing with Caribbean island adventure and world championship qualification opportunity.

### Helsinki Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 93 mi | 2,600 ft | Helsinki Velodrome, Helsinki, Finland | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/helsinki-gran-fondo/

Welcoming non-race gran fondo with great value and Finnish scenery. Diverse paved journey from vibrant city to peaceful countryside, starting with controlled lead-out to Leppävaara. Helsinki Gran Fondo offers an accessible, scenic paved ride ideal for enthusiasts seeking community and enjoyment over competition, though it lacks gravel challenge and detailed route data until spring 2026. A solid choice for road cyclists wanting a supported, paced group ride through urban-rural contrasts, with e-bikes welcome and strong community elements, but awaits route details for full assessment.

### Lake Taupo Cycle Challenge
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 99.4 mi | 6,407 ft | Taupō, Waikato, New Zealand | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/lake-taupo-cycle-challenge/

New Zealand's biggest cycling event. 7,000 riders circling a supervolcano's caldera. 47 years of one schoolteacher's idea. A circumnavigation, not a criterium. The 160 km circuit follows state highways around Lake Taupō — New Zealand's largest lake and the caldera of the Taupō supervolcano. The terrain is rolling rather than mountainous, with three named hills providing structure: Waihaha (the false summit), Waihi (the big grind), and Hatepe (the late-course sting). The route passes through geothermal steam fields, rolling Waikato farmland, quiet southern-shore settlements, and the crystal-clear eastern lakeside. On a clear day, the volcanic peaks of Tongariro, Ngauruhoe, and Ruapehu dominate the southern horizon. The course rewards steady pacing over 5-8 hours, not explosive power. Signature challenge: The three hills — Waihaha, Waihi, and Hatepe — are the spine of the course. None of them are Alpine monsters, but each has its character: Waihaha lies with its false summit, Waihi demands sustained effort for the best views of the day, and Hatepe arrives when your legs have already given 120 km of effort. The real challenge is the totality: 160 km, 1,953 metres of climbing, potentially exposed to wind and rain, on a course that never lets you fully rest. The Lake Taupō Cycle Challenge is not a race. It is a pilgrimage. Every last Saturday of November since 1977, thousands of New Zealanders have driven to a small volcanic-plateau town to circle a supervolcano's caldera on their bicycles, because a schoolteacher named Walter de Bont once thought it would be a good idea to ride around the lake with 26 of his mates. That ride became New Zealand's largest cycling event — peaking at 10,000 riders in its heyday and still drawing 7,000 annually after 47 editions. The 160 km circuit takes in geothermal steam fields, rolling Waikato farmland, three named hills that each provide their own variety of suffering, and the crystal-clear eastern shore of the largest lake in New Zealand. On a clear day, you ride toward the snow-capped volcanic peaks of Tongariro and Ruapehu. On a bad day, you ride into wind and rain on exposed state highways. Either way, you finish in a town that has been celebrating your ride all day long. Yes if you want an authentic New Zealand cycling experience with volcanic scenery and genuine community atmosphere. Yes if you want a well-supported century ride (or 200-mile ultra) in the Southern Hemisphere. Skip if you need serious competition — this is a community ride, not a race. Skip if geographic isolation is a dealbreaker — getting to Taupō requires commitment.

### L'Etape Chile by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 68.3 mi | 3,937 ft | Valdivia, Los Rios, Chile | November | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-chile/

TdF magic meets Chilean wilderness—worth the trek! Coastal launch from Niebla rockets into Valdivia's emerald valleys. Rollers twist through ancient forests, rivers flashing beside. Gentle climbs build to panoramic payoffs—no endless grinds, all scenic reward. TdF signage elevates every pedal. Signature challenge: The Niebla-to-Valdivia coastal traverse: 110km essence in rainforest-wrapped rollers. 1,200m spread thin but relentless, with humidity amplifying burn. TdF finish line in Valdivia seals the conquest. L'Etape Chile blasts Tour spirit into Chile's lush south, blending coastal beauty with manageable suffering. Debut 110km from Niebla immerses you in rainforest rollers under Tour branding—no French Alps brutality, but fresh adventure. Skeptics yawn at low vert; purists drool over novelty. Valdivia's charm post-ride rivals any podium. Yes if craving exotic road prestige without Alpine agony. Skip if chasing mega-vert or elite competition.

### L'Etape Denmark by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 186 mi | 4,593 ft | Various start points across Jutland, Denmark — all routes finish in Viborg | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-denmark/

ASO's Tour de France franchise meets Denmark's ancient Viking road. 7,000 riders, five routes up to 300 km, TdF jerseys, and a finish festival in Viborg. A point-to-point journey along Denmark's ancient glacial ridge — the Haervejen road that Vikings, pilgrims, and merchants traveled for a thousand years. The terrain is rolling rather than mountainous, with the Jutland ridgeline delivering constant undulation through heathlands, forests, and historic towns. The landscape is understated and pastoral — wide horizons, open farmland, ancient churches, and Viking-era landmarks. Wind is the primary adversary, channeled across the open terrain by prevailing westerlies. Each route starts from a different Jutland town and converges on Viborg, Denmark's former capital, for a shared finish celebration. The 300 km Queen Stage begins across the border in Flensburg, Germany, adding an international dimension to an already mythic route. Signature challenge: The 300 km Queen Stage from Flensburg to Viborg — 186 miles of sustained effort along the Viking road with 2,500 meters of accumulated climbing. The challenge is not any single climb but the relentless accumulation of effort across rolling terrain, exposed to Jutland's winds, for 14-18 hours. Denmark does not have mountains, but it has distance, wind, and the psychological weight of a route that stretches from Germany to the heart of the Danish peninsula. L'Etape Denmark is what happens when ASO's Tour de France franchise lands in the flattest country in Scandinavia — and somehow it works. The Haervejen, an ancient road traveled by Vikings and pilgrims for centuries, provides the narrative backbone that Denmark's topography cannot. Seven thousand riders from 30 countries converge on five routes, all finishing in Viborg — a city older than the Tour de France by about 900 years. The TdF jersey classifications are legitimate, the feed stations are legendary, and the 300 km Queen Stage from Germany to Denmark is a genuine endurance test. DGI's grassroots organization and 1,000+ local volunteers give the event a community warmth that pure commercial sportives cannot replicate. The climbing is modest by any international standard — Mur de Refsgaarde at 8.2% for 550 meters is the hardest the course gets — but the wind across open Jutland will remind you that flat does not mean easy. Yes if you want a well-organized TdF franchise experience in Scandinavia with genuine community atmosphere. Yes if the 300 km ultra-distance Queen Stage appeals to your endurance ambitions. Yes if you want an accessible entry point to international road cycling events — the five distance options accommodate everyone. Skip if you want serious climbing — Denmark's glacial ridgeline is charming but not challenging by Alpine or Pyrenean standards. Skip if you prioritize deep competitive racing over mass-participation experience.

### Race Across Portugal
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 621 mi | 36,417 ft | Porto region, Portugal | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-across-portugal/

Challenging self-supported ultra-road race kicking off the 2026 season in Portugal's diverse landscapes.

### RBC GranFondo Whistler
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 76 mi | 7,500 ft | Stanley Park, Vancouver → Whistler Village | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/rbc-granfondo-whistler/

Sea-to-Sky Highway from Vancouver to Whistler — North America's largest and most celebrated gran fondo, where the scenery outshines the climbing. A 122km point-to-point ride from downtown Vancouver to Whistler along the iconic Sea-to-Sky Highway. The course begins at sea level in Stanley Park, crosses the Lions Gate Bridge car-free, follows the stunning Howe Sound coastline, then climbs gradually through the Coast Mountains to Whistler Village. The challenge is sustained distance rather than steep grades — most of the 2,300m of climbing occurs as a long, gradual drag in the second half after Squamish. Signature challenge: The sustained gradient from Squamish to Whistler — 55km of gradually rising road that grinds riders down through sheer distance rather than steep pitches. The Forte adds the pre-dawn Cypress Mountain Cat 1 climb for those seeking a true mountainous challenge. RBC GranFondo Whistler is North America's answer to the European gran fondo tradition. The Sea-to-Sky Highway is genuinely spectacular, the organization is polished, and the Whistler finish creates a perfect post-ride celebration atmosphere. But the course itself is more scenic cruise than mountain challenge — the climbing is gradual and sustained rather than steep and decisive. The Forte adds real teeth with Cypress Mountain, but the standard Gran Fondo is fundamentally a long, beautiful highway ride. Yes, if you value the full gran fondo experience over pure climbing challenge. The Sea-to-Sky is genuinely beautiful, the organization is top-tier, and the Whistler finish is a celebration. The Forte (152km) is the choice for those wanting real climbing. Best for riders who appreciate scenic production over alpine suffering.

### Round Denmark Bike Race
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 1221 mi | 28,409 ft | Denmark (circumnavigation) | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/round-denmark-bike-race/

Ultimate unsupported circumnavigation of Denmark testing endurance, navigation, and self-reliance.

### Sea Otter Classic Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 90 mi | 5,500 ft | Monterey, California, USA | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/sea-otter-classic-gran-fondo/

90 miles of wine country roads wrapped inside the world's biggest cycling festival. A tale of two halves. The first 40 miles lull you into comfort through flat agricultural valley roads and vineyard aid stations. Then the Cahoon Summit climb punishes complacency with 10+ miles of sustained effort before the Laureles Grade kicker reminds you that the finish is earned, not given. Signature challenge: The Cahoon Summit climb: 10+ miles of sustained 4-6% gradient through East Carmel Valley Road, arriving after 50 miles of riding when glycogen stores are depleting. The Sea Otter Classic Gran Fondo is not the hardest ride you will do this year, and it is not trying to be. It is 90 miles of gorgeous Salinas Valley wine country wrapped inside the world's largest cycling festival, and that combination is more powerful than either element alone.

The route is honest: flat valley roads for 40 miles, then the real work begins with the Cahoon Summit grind and the steep Laureles Grade kicker to finish. 5,500 feet of climbing will remind your legs that this is a proper fondo, not a parade. But the four vineyard-set aid stations, the April coastal weather, and the knowledge that Laguna Seca Raceway awaits at the finish soften the suffering.

What makes this ride special is everything surrounding it. A 4-day festival pass comes with registration. 1,000+ bike brands at the expo. 14 race disciplines across the weekend. Cycling legends signing autographs. It is the season opener for American cycling, and riding the gran fondo puts you at the center of it. Yes if you want the ultimate cycling culture weekend with a beautiful ride attached. Skip if you need competitive racing, chip times, or want to avoid $300/night hotels.

### Seattle to Portland Bicycle Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 206 mi | 5,100 ft | Seattle to Portland, Washington/Oregon, USA | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/seattle-to-portland/

206 miles from Seattle to Portland. 6,000 riders. 47 years. The Pacific Northwest's cycling pilgrimage. A 206-mile south-bound corridor from Seattle to Portland on paved country roads, avoiding Interstate 5. The route threads through the green lowlands of western Washington and Oregon — Puget Sound farmland, the Nisqually and Chehalis river valleys, the Cowlitz corridor, and the Columbia River crossing into Oregon. The terrain is predominantly flat to gently rolling, with one honest climb near Puyallup and energy-sapping rollers between Chehalis and Napavine. Small towns punctuate the route every 15-25 miles, each one a checkpoint and a morale boost. The course crosses from Washington into Oregon via the Lewis and Clark Bridge over the Columbia River. The final miles run through St. Helens and Scappoose before crossing Portland's St. Johns Bridge to the finish at Holladay Park. The character is rural, green, and relentlessly forward — 206 miles in one direction, no turning back. Signature challenge: The distance itself. STP has no single feature that defines the course — no brutal climb, no technical descent, no unrideable sector. The challenge is 206 miles of sustained forward motion, managing nutrition and hydration across 8-14 hours of saddle time, and the psychological discipline of riding one direction for an entire day (or two). The Chehalis-to-Napavine rollers on tired legs are the closest thing to a crux, but the real opponent is the odometer. STP is less a bike ride and more a regional migration. Every July, 6,000+ cyclists ride 206 miles from Seattle to Portland on country roads, through farmland and small towns, sleeping in a field in Centralia, and finishing at Holladay Park with a medal and a beer. The terrain is flat. The competition is nonexistent. The technical demands are zero. None of that matters. What matters is that for 47 years, this ride has been the Pacific Northwest's defining cycling event — a two-day rolling community of thousands, stretching from the Puget Sound to the Willamette Valley, covering 206 miles that transform from intimidating to accomplished to annual tradition. You ride STP once because you want to see if you can. You ride it again because it's July and that's what you do. Yes if you want one of America's great city-to-city cycling experiences. Yes if community and tradition matter more than competition. Yes if riding 206 miles through the PNW countryside with thousands of fellow cyclists sounds like your idea of a perfect July weekend. Skip if you need racing, climbing, technical terrain, or anything beyond flat-road endurance.

### Vietnam Cycling Challenge

### Cape Rouleur
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 373 mi | 19,685 ft | Franschhoek, Western Cape, South Africa | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/cape-rouleur/

600 km through the Cape Winelands on closed roads with pro support. Cycling's most luxurious sportive experience. The Cape Rouleur unfolds across five stages radiating from Franschhoek into the Western Cape's finest cycling terrain. Each stage delivers a different character: wine country rolling roads through Stellenbosch, mountain pass climbs through the Hottentots Holland range, coastal sections with views of False Bay, and the dramatic landscapes around Paarl and Wellington. The riding is demanding — 120 km per day average with 1,200m climbing — but the closed roads and support infrastructure remove every variable except your legs and the scenery. The final day typically includes an approach into Cape Town, setting up the optional Cape Town Cycle Tour. Signature challenge: The queen stage mountain passes. After three prior stages of cumulative fatigue, the most demanding climbing of the week arrives. The Western Cape's mountain passes are not Alpine monsters, but at 30C with the Cape Doctor blowing and four days of riding in the legs, 1,500m of climbing in a single stage is absolutely honest. The Cape Rouleur is what happens when you give 200 amateur cyclists the professional treatment in one of the world's most beautiful cycling destinations. HotChillee's formula is proven: closed roads, motorbike outriders, lead car nutrition, mechanics with spare wheels, a race doctor, and a broom wagon. You don't think about traffic, navigation, or mechanical issues. You just ride.

The setting makes it sing. Five stages through the Cape Winelands — Franschhoek, Stellenbosch, and the surrounding wine country — deliver vineyards, mountain passes, and coastal roads in succession. The hospitality in Franschhoek between stages is exceptional. Wine tastings, gourmet South African food, poolside recovery. This is cycling as luxury experience.

The price reflects the quality: GBP 1,350+ entry is steep, and flights to Cape Town add another GBP 500-1,200. But for the cycling tourist who wants five days of world-class riding with zero logistics burden, there is nothing better. Yes if you want a premium multi-stage cycling holiday with world-class support in a stunning setting. Yes if the combination of serious daily riding and Franschhoek wine country hospitality appeals to your inner cycling tourist. Skip if the GBP 3,000-4,000 total cost is prohibitive. Skip if you want a competitive event with timing and rankings.

### Etape Loch Ness
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 66 mi | 2,953 ft | Inverness, Scottish Highlands | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/etape-loch-ness/

Scotland's largest closed-road sportive — 66 miles around one of the world's most iconic lochs with 900m of climbing and a King of the Mountain at 12%. A 360-degree loop around one of the world's most famous bodies of water on fully closed roads. The route departs Inverness heading west along the north shore of Loch Ness on the A82, following the loch through the Great Glen — a geological fault line that splits Scotland diagonally from Inverness to Fort William. At Fort Augustus, the route reaches the southern tip of the loch where the Caledonian Canal connects Loch Ness to Loch Oich. Here, riders face the King of the Mountain: a 4.8-mile timed climb above the village gaining 380 metres with gradients reaching 12%. The summit delivers Highland panoramas before the descent to the south shore. The return leg follows narrower, quieter B-roads along the south side of Loch Ness — more atmospheric, more exposed to wind, and with rolling terrain that keeps the legs honest. The final miles approach Inverness from the south, with the last section through the city operating on left-lane closure rather than full road closure. The finish returns riders to the event area near Eden Court, completing the circumnavigation. Signature challenge: The King of the Mountain at Glen Doe above Fort Augustus. 4.8 miles gaining 380 metres with gradients reaching 12%. The only timed competitive element in a non-competitive sportive, it is where the Etape Loch Ness transforms from a scenic ride into a proper cycling challenge. Etape Loch Ness is what happens when you take one of the world's most famous natural landmarks, close the roads around it, and let 6,000 cyclists loose at dawn. The result is a sportive that trades on scenery and atmosphere rather than raw difficulty, and it delivers on that promise completely. The 66-mile loop around Loch Ness on fully closed roads is a cycling experience that few UK events can match — the loch emerging from morning mist, the Highland mountains rising on either side, Fort Augustus at the halfway point, and the King of the Mountain climb to test your legs and your resolve. This is not a race that will break elite cyclists, but it is an experience that will stay with every rider who does it. The £2 million raised for Macmillan Cancer Support gives it a soul that pure sporting events often lack. Yes, if riding closed roads around Loch Ness at dawn with 6,000 cyclists appeals to you as an experience. Yes, if you want a well-organized sportive that welcomes all abilities while still offering a legitimate climbing challenge. Skip if you need serious competitive racing depth — this is participatory. Skip if sub-100km on paved roads doesn't challenge you. And check the weather forecast before packing — Scottish April weather demands respect and proper kit.

### Fuji Panorama Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 75 mi | 5,906 ft | Lake Saiko, Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan | — | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/fuji-panorama-gran-fondo/

Stunning Fuji-framed road loops for scenery chasers seeking accessible gran fondo suffering.

### GranFondo Riviera Nayarit
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 87 mi | — | Riviera Nayarit, Mexico | April | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-riviera-nayarit/

Premium beachside gran fondo blending epic coastal rides with Mexican festival vibes.

### Granfondo Via del Sale
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 97 mi | 7,260 ft | Cervia, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | March | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/granfondo-via-del-sale/

The Salt Road. From Cervia's ancient salt pans to the Romagna hills. Pantani showed up at the first one. A road gran fondo that traces the ancient salt trading route from the Adriatic coast into the Romagna hills. The route starts flat from Cervia at sea level, passes the historic salt pans, and heads inland through rolling countryside to a series of punchy climbs. The hills are not long — the longest is 10 km — but gradients reach 18%. The rhythm is distinctive: flat coastal start, progressive climbing through the Romagna foothills, emotional climax at Cima Pantani, a final timed climb, and 32 km of flat back to the Adriatic finish. The salt road runs both ways. Signature challenge: Cima Pantani on Montevecchio — not the longest or the steepest climb, but the one that matters. 373m of gain at up to 14% with Pantani's name painted on the road. The Grand Prix Via del Sale is awarded here. But the real challenge is cumulative: five distinct climbs across 156 km, with the timed segment at Paderno-Collinello coming after 85+ miles of riding. The salt road demands endurance, not just climbing power. The Granfondo Via del Sale traces the ancient route of Cervia's salt miners — from the Adriatic coast's salt pans into the Romagna hills. Twenty-nine editions, a famously low entry fee, and the legend of Marco Pantani showing up unannounced at the first race in 1997. The climbs are not Alpine — Monte Cavallo at 438m, Ciola at 557m — but gradients reach 18% on Santa Maria Riopetra, and Cima Pantani on Montevecchio has 'VAI MARCO... FORZA PIRATA' painted on the road where the champion once rode. This is a solid Italian gran fondo at an exceptional price point, steeped in salt and cycling history. Yes if you want an affordable, well-established Italian gran fondo with genuine historical narrative and a Pantani pilgrimage. Yes if punchy Romagna hills with 18% gradients at EUR35 sounds like your kind of value. Skip if you want Alpine spectacle — these are foothills, not mountains. Skip if you need a large international field — this is an Italian event for Italian cyclists.

### L'Etape Australia by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 98 mi | 9,088 ft | Wollongong to Kiama, New South Wales, Australia | December | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-australia/

The Tour de France comes to Australia -- when it actually happens. Mountainous coastal road course replicating a Tour de France stage -- 143 km with 2,701 m of climbing on fully closed sealed roads through the NSW South Coast, Shoalhaven, and Illawarra escarpment. Signature challenge: Two ascents of Macquarie Pass (Race) separated by 40+ km of rolling terrain -- the second climb at 70+ km on tired legs is where the event is won and lost. L'Etape Australia brings Tour de France branding and ASO production values to the NSW South Coast -- 143 km of fully closed roads, 2,701 m of climbing, classification jerseys, Shimano support, and 3,000+ riders chasing the yellow jersey dream. Chris Froome rode the inaugural edition in the Snowy Mountains in 2016. The experience is genuinely special: cowbells, closed roads, professional timing, and that unmistakable TdF atmosphere. But this event has been plagued by cancellations -- severe weather, council opposition, road landslips -- to the point where showing up and actually racing feels like an achievement in itself. The move from the spectacular Snowy Mountains to the Wollongong-Kiama coastal route was forced by local politics, not course design ambition. At ~$300 AUD it is expensive for a sportive, though the included jersey and full support soften that. If you can actually get to a start line that exists, the riding is excellent. If you are based in Australia and want the closest thing to riding a Tour de France stage without leaving the Southern Hemisphere, this is your event. The 143 km course with 2,701 m of climbing on fully closed roads is a genuine challenge, the TdF atmosphere is infectious, and the included jersey is a legitimate keepsake. But you need to accept the cancellation risk: this event has been cancelled three times in four years. Do not book non-refundable international flights. Do not build your entire season around it. Train for it, enter it, hope for the best, and have a backup Australian sportive on your calendar. If you are traveling from overseas specifically for this event, consider the original L'Etape du Tour in France instead -- same brand, better reliability, more iconic climbs.

### L'Etape Czech Republic by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 82 mi | 5,577 ft | Prague and Central Bohemia Region, Czech Republic | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-czech-republic/

ASO's Tour de France franchise in Prague: 3,000 riders on closed Bohemian roads, strong organization, and Europe's best post-ride beer. Rolling Bohemian countryside on fully closed roads. The hilly stage winds through the Central Bohemia region, passing through the Krivoklat forests, Berounsko river valley, and the Czech Karst -- a landscape of limestone formations, deep forests, ancient alleys, and historic villages. The terrain is characterized by punchy hills rather than sustained mountain climbs: categorized ascents of 2-5 km with gradients that reward explosive power as much as steady-state climbing. The 2024 edition introduced four new summits to freshen the route. Descents wind through forested sections on well-maintained roads. The course delivers a classic European hilly sportive profile -- genuinely challenging but not destructive. Signature challenge: The accumulated vertical across punchy Bohemian hills. No single climb is a monster, but 1,700 m of elevation gain delivered in short, sharp efforts across rolling terrain demands repeated high-intensity efforts. The long route's 25 km/h minimum average time limit adds pressure -- you cannot soft-pedal the hills and expect to make cutoff. L'Etape Czech Republic delivers a polished Tour de France experience on fully closed roads through the scenic Bohemian countryside, starting and finishing in one of Europe's most appealing capitals. The organization -- under ASO patronage and run by Petr Cech Sport -- is genuinely impressive for a 6-year-old event: closed roads, classification jerseys, Shimano support, celebrity riders, and thousands of enthusiastic Czech fans lining the route. Bradley Wiggins rode the 2025 edition and praised the atmosphere. The course through Krivoklat forests and the Czech Karst is beautiful, if not brutal -- 1,700 m of climbing over 132 km is honest work but not Alpine suffering. The year-round Bootcamp community program is a smart differentiator that builds loyalty beyond race day. Prague as a host city is the real bonus: affordable, beautiful, excellent food and beer, direct flights from everywhere. Yes if you want a professionally organized, closed-road sportive with Tour de France branding in one of Europe's most appealing and affordable cities. Yes if you want the L'Etape experience without Alpine-scale suffering. Skip if you need Alpine passes and 5,000 m of climbing to feel challenged. Skip if you want a globally prestigious event with international field depth -- this is still regional.

### L'Étape Romania by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 50 mi | 500 ft | Bucharest, Romania | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/letape-romania/

A festive, accessible urban cycling celebration in Eastern Europe's premier amateur Tour de France event. Urban loop through Bucharest's historic heart on fully closed roads; celebratory, accessible, and landmark-rich rather than technically demanding Signature challenge: Maintaining momentum through dense urban sections and navigating 2,500+ cyclists on closed roads; psychological challenge of distance (85 km) rather than terrain difficulty L'Étape Romania is an excellent entry point into the official Tour de France amateur series, combining accessibility, urban charm, and pro-level logistics. The flat, closed-road course through Bucharest's historic landmarks makes it ideal for first-timers and families, though experienced cyclists seeking elevation challenge should look elsewhere. The qualification pathway to L'Étape du Tour in France adds prestige. Best suited for cyclists seeking a celebratory, community-focused experience over a technical or demanding route. L'Étape Romania delivers a well-organized, inclusive amateur cycling experience with strong community spirit and official Tour de France credibility. The fully closed roads through iconic Bucharest landmarks, three distance options, and Race Village create a festival atmosphere that appeals to all levels. However, the flat urban course lacks elevation challenge and technical difficulty, limiting appeal for experienced cyclists seeking a demanding ride. The qualification pathway to L'Étape du Tour in France and modest registration costs enhance value. Recommended for first-time amateurs, families, and cyclists prioritizing experience and community over physical challenge.

### Gran Fondo Pekan / Pekan Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 101 mi | 2,500 ft | Royal Town of Pekan, Pahang, Malaysia | June | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/pekan-classics/

Scenic, affordable UCI gran fondo blending flat speed with moderate Pahang hills. Flat river-hugging out-and-back with a central loop deviation, featuring moderate rolling hills and short steep pitches amid tropical scenery. Signature challenge: Pahang River double-sided routing: 108km flat borders punctuated by a hilly loop for 2500ft gain.

### Race Around Ireland
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 1336 mi | 72,000 ft | Trim, County Meath, Ireland | August | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-around-ireland/

Brutal non-stop Irish coastal ultra for true endurance masochists.

### Race Around Netherlands
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 1211 mi | — | Netherlands (loop) | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/race-around-netherlands/

Epic self-supported flat loop testing endurance against Dutch winds and distance.

### Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 112 mi | 5,774 ft | Killarney, County Kerry, Ireland | July | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/ring-of-kerry-charity-cycle/

170 km around the Iveragh Peninsula. Ireland's biggest one-day cycling event. EUR 22 million raised. The Atlantic coast is the scenery. Charity is the point. A counterclockwise loop around the Iveragh Peninsula on Ireland's Atlantic seaboard. The route leaves Killarney heading west through Killorglin, follows the coast south through Cahersiveen, Waterville, and Caherdaniel, climbs Coomakista for Skellig views, continues east through Sneem to Kenmare, then returns to Killarney via Moll's Gap — the highest and toughest climb of the day. The terrain is rolling throughout with two significant climbs. The character alternates between exposed Atlantic coast road and sheltered inland valleys. It is not an extreme route by European granfondo standards, but 170 km with 1,760m of climbing and the possibility of Atlantic headwinds makes it a demanding day for recreational riders. Signature challenge: Moll's Gap at 140 km — the highest point on the route with poor road surface and steady gradient. It is the moment when under-prepared riders discover that 170 km of Atlantic coast riding has consequences. The climb itself is moderate, but the context is not. The Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle is not a race. It has no timing chips, no results board, no podium. What it has is 44 years of history, EUR 22 million raised for 200+ charities, 1,200 volunteers who make the roads of Kerry safe for thousands of cyclists, and 170 km of Atlantic coast scenery that ranks among the most beautiful cycling routes in Europe. The Iveragh Peninsula delivers the Skellig Islands, Coomakista's ocean views, Moll's Gap, and the approach through Killarney National Park. The climbing is honest but not savage. The weather is a gamble with the Atlantic. The community spirit is not manufactured — it is the DNA of the event. Founded in 1982, this is Ireland's largest one-day cycling event and one of Europe's most significant charity rides. You do not ride the Ring of Kerry to prove anything. You ride it to contribute to something larger than yourself, and the Atlantic scenery is the reward for showing up. Yes if you want one of Europe's great scenic charity rides — the Iveragh Peninsula coastline is spectacular. Yes if community and charitable purpose matter as much as personal achievement. Skip if you need competition, timing, or race results. Skip if Atlantic weather uncertainty is a dealbreaker for a trip to western Ireland.

### Spinneys Dubai 92 Cycle Challenge
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 57 mi | 200 ft | Expo City Dubai, Al Forsan Park | February | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/spinneys-dubai-92/

Premier UAE road event for speed demons chasing UCI spots. Fast, flat urban loop through Dubai landmarks like Global Village, Hessa Street, Emirates Hills, DMCC; 2km neutral rollout; traffic-free wide roads. Signature challenge: Maintaining group speed on flat tarmac for UCI top 25% qualifier. A slick, fast-paced road gran fondo perfect for qualifiers and personal bests in a luxurious setting, though gravel purists will yawn at the flat tarmac. The Spinneys Dubai 92 stands out as a top mass-participation road race with UCI prestige, build-up series, and Dubai glamour, ideal for 19+ qualifiers aiming for Niseko Worlds.

### Surrey Rumble

Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/surrey-rumble/

Friendly early-season road sportive with hilly Surrey classics at bargain price.

Pro roads, Arabian climbs: winter sportive gold.

Epic road sportive conquering Oman's Al Hajar mountains with unrivaled scenery.

### RBC GranFondo Whistler
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 76 mi | 7,546 ft | Vancouver to Whistler, British Columbia, Canada | September | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/whistler-granfondo/

122 km on a closed highway from Vancouver to Whistler. The Sea-to-Sky belongs to bikes for one day — and it's magnificent. A point-to-point journey from sea level to the mountains along one of North America's most scenic highways. The course starts in Vancouver's Stanley Park, crosses the Lions Gate Bridge, and follows the Sea-to-Sky Highway north along Howe Sound's coastal fjord before turning inland at Squamish to climb through the Coast Mountains to Whistler Village. The first half is coastal and rolling — dramatic ocean-and-mountain views with punchy climbs at Furry Creek and Britannia Beach. The second half is the grind — a sustained ascent from Squamish through the Cheakamus Valley and Garibaldi Highlands, where the ocean recedes and the mountains close in. The highway is fully closed to traffic, giving riders a car-free lane on pavement that was resurfaced for the 2010 Winter Olympics. The finish in Whistler Village at 670 meters elevation feels earned — you started at the ocean and climbed through the clouds to get here. Signature challenge: The second half — from Squamish to Whistler. The coastal scenery fades, the sustained climbing begins, and the relentless 2-4% grade through the Cheakamus Valley separates the trained from the hopeful. Most of the 2,300m of climbing loads into the final 60 km. There is one day a year when the Sea-to-Sky Highway — British Columbia's most famous road, the corridor that connects Vancouver's glass towers to Whistler's snow-capped peaks — belongs entirely to bicycles. That day is GranFondo day. You roll out of Stanley Park at dawn. You cross the Lions Gate Bridge with 5,000 riders and the sun rising over the North Shore. You ride along Howe Sound's fjord-blue water with mountains falling into the ocean on both sides. You climb through the Coast Range on pavement so smooth and so empty of cars that you forget this is normally a highway. You finish in Whistler Village, where a beer and a party and the disbelief that you just did that are all waiting. The RBC GranFondo Whistler is not a hard-man's race. It's not technical, it's not remote, and it won't destroy you. But it will give you 122 km of closed-highway riding through some of the most spectacular scenery in North America, and that experience — the sheer, rare luxury of a car-free highway through the mountains — is something you simply cannot get any other way. Yes if you want one of North America's premier mass-participation cycling experiences with world-class scenery and bulletproof organization. Yes if riding a closed highway through the Coast Mountains sounds like your idea of paradise. Skip if you want a competitive road race, technical terrain, or a budget-friendly weekend.

### Superior Morgul Road Race
Tier 2 | Score: 57/100 | 56 mi | 6,000 ft | Superior, Boulder County, Colorado, USA | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/superior-morgul-road-race/

Colorado's most storied road racing circuit — The Wall, the Coors Classic, LeMond, and American Flyers. History on two wheels. A 7-mile paved circuit on Highway 128, fully closed to traffic. Wide, smooth asphalt through rolling Colorado Front Range terrain. The course is dominated by one feature: The Wall (Super Flag Hill), a steep climb of approximately 750 vertical feet that riders face on every lap. The Wall is the selection point — it is where the race is won and lost on every lap. Between climbs, the course rolls through open grassland with views of the Flatirons. Wind on the exposed loop can fracture a field. The corner at McCaslin Boulevard is the tactical setup point — positioning into The Wall determines who attacks and who suffers. For the Pro/1/2 category doing 8 laps, the cumulative fatigue of repeated Wall efforts is the race's defining challenge. Signature challenge: The Wall (Super Flag Hill) — repeated every lap. For Pro/1/2 riders doing 8 laps, that is 8 times up the most historically significant climb in American road racing. The cumulative fatigue of repeated Wall efforts, combined with wind on the exposed loop and tactical positioning at McCaslin Boulevard, makes this a race that rewards power, repeatability, and racing intelligence. The Superior Morgul Road Race is not the biggest race on the calendar. It is not the hardest. It is not the most prestigious event in its current form. But the ground you race on carries more American cycling history per square meter than almost anywhere else in the country. The Morgul-Bismarck circuit hosted the Red Zinger and Coors Classic — the races that introduced America to professional road cycling. LeMond, Hinault, Phinney, Carpenter, and Kiefel attacked The Wall here. Kevin Costner charged up it in American Flyers. The 1987 National Championships were decided on this loop. Today it is a regional circuit race organized by passionate locals. The field is Colorado-strong, not nationally deep. The course is a modified 7-mile version of the original 13-mile Morgul-Bismarck, compressed by suburban development. But when you crest The Wall and see the crowd lining the road, you are standing where American cycling stood up. Yes if American cycling history moves you and you want to race the same circuit as LeMond and Hinault. Yes if you are a Colorado road racer looking for a well-organized circuit race on closed roads with state championship stakes. Skip if you want a long-distance endurance event or a nationally competitive field.

### GFNY NYC
Tier 2 | Score: 54/100 | 85 mi | 6,371 ft | George Washington Bridge (NJ side) / Fort Lee, NJ | May | gran_fondo
Profile: https://roadielabs.com/race/gfny-nyc/

The most international peloton in the world, starting on the George Washington Bridge — GFNY NYC is a brand that outgrew its course. A grand departure from the George Washington Bridge into the leafy hills of NJ and NY, with modest climbing that doesn't match the event's outsized reputation. Since Bear Mountain was removed in 2023, the course lost its crown jewel — a 7.4km Cat 2 that gave the route genuine mountain character. What remains is a pleasant rolling ride through the Palisades with a few punchy climbs and an 18% wall on Cheesecote. Signature challenge: Cheesecote Mountain — the 18% initial ramp is a rude wake-up call after 60km. Short but it breaks rhythm badly. GFNY NYC is a strange contradiction. The brand is genuinely impressive — 30+ events worldwide, 90+ nationalities in the field, legitimate anti-doping program, and now a UCI pro race. But the course itself, especially after Bear Mountain's removal in 2023, is rolling suburban New Jersey that wouldn't merit a second look without the GFNY name. You're paying $350+ for the jersey, the atmosphere, and the right to race on the GWB — not for the route. Yes, if you value the competitive atmosphere, international field, and NYC setting over course quality. The GFNY brand is real — results count globally, doping tests are real, and the UCI pro race adds legitimate elite racing. Just don't expect the route to match the marketing since Bear Mountain was removed.

| L'Étape Indonesia | 4 | 44 | 87 mi | — | Mandalika, Lombok, Indonesia | May | gran_fondo |

## Data Schema

Each race profile (JSON) contains:
- `race.name`, `race.slug`, `race.tagline`
- `race.vitals`: distance_mi, elevation_ft, location, date, terrain_types, field_size
- `race.fondo_rating`: overall_score, tier, 14 dimension scores + cultural_impact, discipline
- `race.course_description`: character, suffering_zones, signature_challenge
- `race.terrain`: primary, surface, technical_rating, features
- `race.climate`: primary, description, challenges
- `race.logistics`: airport, lodging_strategy, official_site
- `race.history`: founded, origin_story, notable_moments
- `race.final_verdict`: one_liner, should_you_race, alternatives
- `race.non_negotiables`: training requirements with deadlines
- `race.citations`: source URLs
- `race.tire_recommendations`: primary tires, pressure tables

Access individual profiles at: https://roadielabs.com/race/{slug}/
Machine-readable JSON index: https://roadielabs.com/search/race-index.json
